proverb_native: Ancor le volpi vecchie rimangono al laccio
proverb_en: Even old foxes are caught in the snare.

proverb_native: Anco trà le spine nascono le rose
proverb_en: Among thorns grow roses.

proverb_native: A nessun confortator mai duole la testa
proverb_en: No comforter’s head ever aches.

proverb_native: A nessuno piace la giustizia à casa sua
proverb_en: No one likes justice brought home to his own door.

proverb_native: Anni e peccati sempre sono più che non si dice
proverb_en: Years and sins are always more than owned.

proverb_native: A penna a penna si pela l’oca
proverb_en: Feather by feather the goose is plucked.

proverb_native: Aperta ha la porta chiunque apporta
proverb_en: Whoever brings finds the door open for him.

proverb_native: Apri bocca, e fa ch’io ti conosca
proverb_en: Open thy mouth that I may know thee.

proverb_native: Arco per rallentar, piaga non sana
proverb_en: Unbending the bow does not heal the wound.

proverb_native: Arditamente batte alla porta chi buone nuove apporta
proverb_en: He knocks boldly at the door who brings good news.

proverb_native: Arte o sorte ne cava macchia
proverb_en: Skill or fortune will efface the spots.

proverb_native: A’segnali si conoscono le balle
proverb_en: By their marks the bales are known.

proverb_native: Asino che ha fame mangia d’ogni strame
proverb_en: A hungry ass eats any straw.

proverb_native: Asino che ragghia poco fieno mangia
proverb_en: A braying ass eats little hay.

proverb_native: Asino punto convien che trotti
proverb_en: A goaded ass must needs trot.

proverb_native: A’sottili cascano le brache
proverb_en: Cunning men’s cloaks sometimes fall.

proverb_native: Aspetta il porco alla quercia
proverb_en: Look for the hog at the oak.

proverb_native: Aspettare e non venire, stare in letto e non dormire, ben servire e non gradire, son tre cose da morire
proverb_en: To expect what never comes, to lie in bed and not sleep, to serve well and not be advanced, are three things to die of.

proverb_native: Aspetta tempo e loco à far la tua vendetta, che la non si fa mai ben in fretta
proverb_en: Wait time and place to take your revenge, for it is never well done in a hurry.

proverb_native: Assai acqua passa per il molino, che il molinaio non se n’accorge
proverb_en: Much water passes by the mill that the miller perceives not.

proverb_native: Assai basta, e troppo guasta
proverb_en: Enough is enough, and too much spoils.

proverb_native: Assai ben balla à chi fortuna suona
proverb_en: He dances well to whom fortune pipes.

proverb_native: Assai dimanda chi ben serve e tace
proverb_en: Who serves well and says nothing makes claim enough.

proverb_native: Assai è ricco à chi non manca
proverb_en: He is rich enough who does not want.

proverb_native: Assai guadagna, chi vano sperar perde
proverb_en: He gains much who loses a vain hope.

proverb_native: Assai presto si fa quel che si fa bene
proverb_en: That is done soon enough which is well done.

proverb_native: Assai romor e poca lana, disse colui che tosava la porca
proverb_en: Great cry and little wool, as the man said who shaved the sow.

proverb_native: Assai sa, chi non sa, se tacer sa
proverb_en: He who knows nothing knows enough, if he knows how to be silent.

proverb_native: Assenza nemica di amore; quanto lontan dall’occhio, tanto dal cuore
proverb_en: Absence is a foe to love; out of sight out of mind.

proverb_native: A tal labbra, tal lattuga
proverb_en: Like lips, like lettuce.

proverb_native: A tal pozzo, tal secchio
proverb_en: Like well, like bucket.

proverb_native: A tal santo, tal offerta
proverb_en: Like saint, like offering.

proverb_native: A tavola non vi vuol vergogna
proverb_en: At table bashfulness is out of place.

proverb_native: A tela ordita Dio manda il filo
proverb_en: For a web begun God sends thread.

proverb_native: A torto si lamenta del mare, chi due volte ci vuol tornare
proverb_en: He ought not to complain of the sea who returns to it a second time.

proverb_native: Attacca il majo ad ogni uscio
proverb_en: He hangs the May-branch at every door.

proverb_native: A tutti non si adatta una sola scarpa
proverb_en: The same shoe does not fit every foot.

proverb_native: A un gran bugiardo ci vuol buona memoria
proverb_en: A great liar has need of good memory.

proverb_native: Aver sentito dire è mezza bugia
proverb_en: To have “heard say” is half a lie.

proverb_native: Aver un occhio alla gatta, e l’altro alla padella
proverb_en: To have one eye on the cat and another on the frying-pan.

proverb_native: A veste logorata poca fede vien prestata
proverb_en: A ragged coat finds little credit.

proverb_native: Baldezza di signor, cappel da matto
proverb_en: Faith in a lord, a cap for the fool.

proverb_native: Bandiera vecchia, onor di capitano
proverb_en: An old flag is an honour to its captain.

proverb_native: Barba bagnata è mezza rasa
proverb_en: A beard well lathered is half-shaved.

proverb_native: Basta un matto per casa
proverb_en: One fool is enough in a house.

proverb_native: Batti il buono e’ megliora, batti il cattivo e’ peggiora
proverb_en: Chastise the good and he will mend, chastise the bad and he will grow worse.

proverb_native: Batti il ferro quando è caldo
proverb_en: Strike while the iron is hot.

proverb_native: Batti il villano, e lo avrai per amico
proverb_en: Beat the churl and he will be your friend.

proverb_native: Beata colei che di vecchio pazzo s’innamora
proverb_en: Happy is she who is in love with an old dotard.

proverb_native: Bel colpo non ammazzò mai uccello
proverb_en: A fine shot never killed a bird.

proverb_native: Bella cosa tosto è rapita
proverb_en: All that’s fair must fade.

proverb_native: Bella femmina che ride, vuol dire borsa che piange
proverb_en: A beautiful woman smiling, bespeaks a purse weeping.

proverb_native: Bella promessa lega il matto
proverb_en: Fair promises bind fools.

proverb_native: Belle parole e mele fracide
proverb_en: Fair words and rotten apples.

proverb_native: Belle parole, ma guarda la borsa
proverb_en: Fair words, but look to your purse.

proverb_native: Belle parole non pascon i gatti
proverb_en: Fair words won’t feed a cat.

proverb_native: Bellezza e follia sovente in compagnia
proverb_en: Beauty and folly are often companions.

proverb_native: Ben ama chi non oblia
proverb_en: He loves well who does not forget.

proverb_native: Benchè la bugia sia veloce, la verità l’arriva
proverb_en: Though a lie be swift, truth overtakes it.

proverb_native: Benchè la volpe corra, i polli hanno le ale
proverb_en: Though the fox runs, the pullets have wings.

proverb_native: Ben è cieco chi non vede il sole
proverb_en: He is very blind who cannot see the sun.

proverb_native: Benedetto è quel male che vien solo
proverb_en: Blessed is the misfortune that comes alone.

proverb_native: Ben faremo, ben diremo, mal va la barca senza remo
proverb_en: Say what we will, do what we will, the boat goes but sorrily without oars.

proverb_native: Ben perduto è conosciuto
proverb_en: A good thing lost is valued.

proverb_native: Berretta in mano non fece mai danno
proverb_en: Cap in hand never did any harm.

proverb_native: Bevendo vien la sete
proverb_en: Thirst comes from drinking.

proverb_native: Bevi del vino, e lascia andar l’acqua al mulino
proverb_en: Drink wine and let water go to the mill.

proverb_native: Biasimare i principi è pericolo, e il lodarli è bugia
proverb_en: To censure princes is perilous, and to praise them is lying.

proverb_native: Bisogna unger le ruote, chi vuol che il carro corra
proverb_en: You must grease the wheels if you would have the car run.

proverb_native: Bisogna voltar la vela secondo il vento
proverb_en: You must shift your sail with the wind.

proverb_native: Bocca chiusa ed occhi aperti
proverb_en: Mouth shut and eyes open.

proverb_native: Bocca unta non può dir di no
proverb_en: A greased mouth cannot say no.

proverb_native: Brutta cosa è il povero superbo e ’l ricco avaro
proverb_en: A proud pauper and a rich miser are contemptible beings.

proverb_native: Bue vecchio, solco diritto
proverb_en: An old ox makes a straight furrow.

proverb_native: Bugie hanno corte le gambe
proverb_en: Lies have short legs.

proverb_native: Buona incudine non tema martello
proverb_en: A good anvil does not fear the hammer.

proverb_native: Buon appetito non vuol salsa
proverb_en: A good appetite does not want sauce.

proverb_native: Buona quella lima che doma il ferro senza strepito
proverb_en: It is a good file that cuts iron without making a noise.

proverb_native: Buona vita non può tenere, quel che serve senz’avere
proverb_en: He cannot lead a good life who serves without wages.

proverb_native: Buon cavallo e mal cavallo vuole sproni; buona femmina e mala femmina vuol bastoni
proverb_en: A good horse and a bad horse need the spur; a good woman and a bad woman need the stick.

proverb_native: Buon cavallo non ha bisogno de’ sproni
proverb_en: Never spur a willing horse.

proverb_native: Buon pagatore dell’altrui borsa è signore
proverb_en: He who pays well is master of other men’s purses.

proverb_native: Buon pagatore non si cura di dar buon pegno
proverb_en: A good paymaster does not hesitate to give good security.

proverb_native: Buon principio è la metà dell’opra
proverb_en: Well begun is half done.

proverb_native: Buon principio fa buon fine
proverb_en: A good beginning makes a good ending.

proverb_native: Buon vino fa buon sangue
proverb_en: Good wine makes good blood.

proverb_native: Cader della padella nelle bragie
proverb_en: To fall out of the frying-pan into the fire.

proverb_native: Cade un cavallo, che ha quattro gambe
proverb_en: A horse falls though he has four legs.

proverb_native: Cadono le miglior pere in bocca a’ porci
proverb_en: The best pears fall into the pigs’ mouths.

proverb_native: Calcio di cavalla non fece mai male a poledro
proverb_en: The kick of a mare never hurt a colt.

proverb_native: Can ch’abbaia non morde
proverb_en: A barking cur does not bite.

proverb_native: Can che morde non abbaia in vano
proverb_en: The dog that bites does not bark in vain.

proverb_native: Can dell’ortolano non mangia la lattuga, e non la lascia mangiare agli altri
proverb_en: The gardener’s dog does not eat lettuce and will not let others eat it.

proverb_native: Cane abbaia, e bue pasce
proverb_en: The dog barks and the ox feeds.

proverb_native: Cane affamato non ha paura di bastone
proverb_en: A hungry dog does not fear the stick.

proverb_native: Cane di cucina non fu mai buon per la caccia
proverb_en: A kitchen dog never was good for the chase.

proverb_native: Cane vecchio non abbaia indarno
proverb_en: An old dog does not bark for nothing.

proverb_native: Can ringhioso e non forzoso, guai alla sua pelle
proverb_en: The dog that is quarrelsome and not strong, woe to his hide.

proverb_native: Can vecchio non s’avvezza a portar collare
proverb_en: An old dog does not grow used to the collar.

proverb_native: Capo grasso, cervello magro
proverb_en: Fat head, lean brains.

proverb_native: Carestia prevista non venne mai
proverb_en: Dearth foreseen never came.

proverb_native: Carica volontaria non carica
proverb_en: A voluntary burthen is no burthen.

proverb_native: Caro costa quel che con preghi si compra
proverb_en: That costs dear which is bought with begging.

proverb_native: Caro mi vendi, e giusto mi misura
proverb_en: Sell me dear, and measure me fair.

proverb_native: Casa fatta, possession disfatta
proverb_en: The house completed, possession defeated.

proverb_native: Casa il figlio quando vuoi, e la figlia quando puoi
proverb_en: Marry your son when you please, your daughter when you can.

proverb_native: Casa mia, casa mia, per piccina che tu sia, tu mi sembri una badìa
proverb_en: Home, dear home, small as thou art, to me thou art a palace.

proverb_native: Castello che dà orecchia si vuol rendere
proverb_en: The fortress that parleys soon surrenders.

proverb_native: Cattiva è quella lana che non si può tingere
proverb_en: Bad is the wool that cannot be died.

proverb_native: Cattive nuove presto corrono
proverb_en: Ill news travels fast.

proverb_native: Cattivo è quel sacco che non si può rappezzare
proverb_en: Bad is the sack that will not bear patching.

proverb_native: Cavallo che corre non ha bisogna di sproni
proverb_en: Spur not a willing horse.

proverb_native: Cavallo ingrassato tira calci
proverb_en: A horse grown fat kicks.

proverb_native: Cavallo magro non tira calci
proverb_en: A lean horse does not kick.

proverb_native: Cavallo scappato da sè si gastiga
proverb_en: A runaway horse punishes himself.

proverb_native: Cava un chiodo, e pianta un cavicchio
proverb_en: He takes out a nail and puts in a pin.

proverb_native: Cerca cinque piedi al montone
proverb_en: He is in search of a ram with five feet.

proverb_native: Che ne può la gatta se la massaia è matta?
proverb_en: How can the cat help it if the maid be a fool?

proverb_native: Che sarà, sarà
proverb_en: What will be, will be.

proverb_native: Chi abbisogna, non abbia vergogna
proverb_en: He that wants should not be bashful.

proverb_native: Chi affoga, grida ancor che non sia udito
proverb_en: He that is drowning shouts though he be not heard.

proverb_native: Chi altri giudica, sè condanna
proverb_en: Who judges others, condemns himself.

proverb_native: Chi altri tribola, sè non posa
proverb_en: Who troubles others has no rest himself.

proverb_native: Chi ama, crede
proverb_en: Who loves, believes.

proverb_native: Chiama l’arbore, ama il ramo
proverb_en: Who loves the tree loves the branch.

proverb_native: Chi ama me, ama il mio cane
proverb_en: Love me, love my dog.

proverb_native: Chi ama, teme
proverb_en: Who loves, fears.

proverb_native: Chi asino è, e cervo esser si crede, al saltar del fosso sè ne avvede
proverb_en: He who is an ass and thinks himself a stag, finds his mistake when he comes to leap the ditch.

proverb_native: Chi aspettar puole, ha ciò che vuole
proverb_en: He who can wait obtains what he wishes.

proverb_native: Chiave d’oro apre ogni porta
proverb_en: A gold key opens every door.

proverb_native: Chi ben ama, ben gastiga
proverb_en: Who loves well chastises well.

proverb_native: Chi ben comincia ha la metà dell’opra
proverb_en: Well begun is half done.

proverb_native: Chi ben congettura, bene indovina
proverb_en: He who guesses well prophesies well.

proverb_native: Chi ben dona, caro vende, se villano non è chi prende
proverb_en: Who gives well, sells dear, if the receiver be not a churl.

proverb_native: Chi ben dorme, non sente le pulci
proverb_en: He who sleeps well does not feel the fleas.

proverb_native: Chi ben vive, ben predica
proverb_en: He preaches well who lives well.

proverb_native: Chi biasima, vuol comprare
proverb_en: He who decries, wants to buy.

proverb_native: Chi burla, si confessa
proverb_en: He that jokes, confesses.

proverb_native: Chi burla, vien burlato
proverb_en: The biter is sometimes bit.

proverb_native: Chi butta via oro con lo mani lo cerca co’ piedi
proverb_en: He who throws away money with his hands will seek it with his feet.

proverb_native: Chi canta, i suoi mali spaventa
proverb_en: He who sings drives away sorrow.

proverb_native: Chi cerca mal, mal trova
proverb_en: Harm watch, harm catch.

proverb_native: Chi cerca, trova, e talor quel che non vorrebbe
proverb_en: He that seeks, finds, and sometimes what he would rather not.

proverb_native: Chi colomba si fa, il falcon se la mangia
proverb_en: He who makes himself a dove is eaten by the hawk.

proverb_native: Chi compra a tempo, compra a buon mercato
proverb_en: He who buys betimes buys cheaply.

proverb_native: Chi compra ciò che pagar non può, vende ciò che non vuole
proverb_en: He who buys what he cannot pay for, sells what he would rather not.

proverb_native: Chi compra il magistrato, forza è che venda la giustizia
proverb_en: He that buys the office of magistrate must of necessity sell justice.

proverb_native: Chi compra la scopa, può anche comprar il manico
proverb_en: He who buys the broom can also buy the handle.

proverb_native: Chi compra terra, compra guerra
proverb_en: Who buys land buys war.

proverb_native: Chi con l’occhio vede, di cuor crede
proverb_en: Seeing is believing.

proverb_native: Chi contra il cielo getta pietra, in capo gli ritorna
proverb_en: Who throws a stone at the sky, it falls back on his head.

proverb_native: Chi dà del pane a’ cani d’altri, spesso viene abbaiato da suoi
proverb_en: He who gives bread to others’ dogs is often barked at by his own.

proverb_native: Chi dà, insegna a rendere
proverb_en: Who gives, teaches a return.

proverb_native: Chi dà presto, dà il doppio
proverb_en: He gives twice who gives in a trice.

proverb_native: Chi della serpa è punto, ha paura della lucertola
proverb_en: He who has been stung by a serpent is afraid of a lizard.

proverb_native: Chi dice i fatti suoi, mal tacerà quelli d’altrui
proverb_en: He who tells his own affairs will hardly keep secret those of others.

proverb_native: Chi dimanda non commanda
proverb_en: He who demands does not command.

proverb_native: Chi dinanzi mi pinge, di dietro mi tinge
proverb_en: Who paints me before blackens me behind.

proverb_native: Chi di venti non è, di trenta non sa, di quaranta non ha, mai non sarà, nè mai saprà, nè mai havra
proverb_en: He that at twenty is not, at thirty knows not, and at forty has not, will never be, nor ever know, nor ever have.

proverb_native: Chi divide il miele con l’orso ha la minor parte
proverb_en: Who divides honey with the bear, will be like to get the lesser share.

proverb_native: Chi dorme con cani si leva con pulci
proverb_en: Who lies down with dogs gets up with fleas.

proverb_native: Chi dorme non piglia pesci
proverb_en: He who sleeps catches no fish.

proverb_native: Chi due lepri caccia, l’una non piglia e l’altra lascia
proverb_en: He who hunts two hares does not catch the one and lets the other escape.

proverb_native: Chi due padroni ha da servire, ad uno ha da mentire
proverb_en: He who serves two masters must lie to one of them.

proverb_native: Chi è al coperto quando piove, è ben matto se si muove
proverb_en: He who is under cover when it rains is a great fool if he stirs.

proverb_native: Chi è causa del suo male, pianga se stesso
proverb_en: He who is the cause of his own misfortune may bewail it himself.

proverb_native: Chi è dell’arte ne può ragionare
proverb_en: He who is of the craft can discourse about it.

proverb_native: Chi è diffamato, è mezzo impiccato
proverb_en: He who hath an ill name is half hanged.

proverb_native: Chi è imbarcato col diavolo, ha da passar in sua compagnia
proverb_en: He who is embarked with the devil must make the passage in his company.

proverb_native: Chi è in difetto, è in sospetto
proverb_en: He that is in fault is in suspicion.

proverb_native: Chi è in inferno non sà ciò che sia cielo
proverb_en: He who is in hell knows not what heaven is.

proverb_native: Chi è in peccato, crede che tutti dicano male di lui
proverb_en: He who is guilty believes that all men speak ill of him.

proverb_native: Chi è nato per la forca mai s’annegherà
proverb_en: He that is born to be hanged will never be drowned.

proverb_native: Chi è ritto può cadere
proverb_en: He that stands may fall.

proverb_native: Chi erra nelle decine, erra nelle migliaja
proverb_en: Who errs in the tens errs in the thousands.

proverb_native: Chi esce di commissione, paga del suo
proverb_en: He that exceeds his commission must answer for it at his own cost.

proverb_native: Chi fa à suo modo, non gli duole il capo
proverb_en: He who does as he likes has no headache.

proverb_native: Chi fabbrica su quel d’altri, perde la calcina e le pietre
proverb_en: He who builds on another’s ground loses his stone and mortar.

proverb_native: Chi fabbrica vicino alla strada, ha molti sindicatori
proverb_en: He who builds by the roadside has many surveyors.

proverb_native: Chi fa il conto senza l’oste, gli convien farlo due volte
proverb_en: He that reckons without his host must reckon again.

proverb_native: Chi fa la casa in piazza, o la fa troppo alta o troppo bassa
proverb_en: He who builds a house in the market-place, builds either too high or too low.

proverb_native: Chi fa li fatti suoi, non s’imbratta le mani
proverb_en: He who doth his own business defileth not his fingers.

proverb_native: Chi fa quel ch’e’ può, non fa mai bene
proverb_en: Who does all he may never does well.

proverb_native: Chi fonda in sul popolo, fonda in sulla rena
proverb_en: Who builds on the mob builds on sand.

proverb_native: Chi frequenta la cucina, sente di fumo
proverb_en: Who frequents the kitchen smells of smoke.

proverb_native: Chi giura, è bugiardo
proverb_en: He who swears is a liar.

proverb_native: Chi ha buon cavallo in stalla, può andare a piedi
proverb_en: He who has a good horse in his stable may go on foot.

proverb_native: Chi ha buon vicino, ha buon mattino
proverb_en: He who has a good neighbour has a good morning.

proverb_native: Chi ha capo di cera, non vada al sole
proverb_en: He who has a head of wax must not walk in the sun.

proverb_native: Chi ha cattivo nome, è mezzo impiccato
proverb_en: He who has a bad name is half hanged.

proverb_native: Chi ha coda di paglia, ha sempre paura che gli pigli fuoco
proverb_en: He who has a straw tail is always in fear of its catching fire.

proverb_native: Chi ha compagno, ha padrone
proverb_en: He who has a mate has a master.

proverb_native: Chi ha da esser zanaiuolo, nasce col manico in mano
proverb_en: He who is meant to be a basket-carrier is born with the handle in his hand.

proverb_native: Chi ha danari da buttar via, metta gli operaj, e non vi stia
proverb_en: He who has money to throw away, let him employ workmen, and not stand by.

proverb_native: Chi ha denti, non ha pane; e chi ha pane, non ha denti
proverb_en: He who has teeth has no bread, and he who has bread has no teeth.

proverb_native: Chi ha de’ pani, ha de’ cani
proverb_en: He who has loaves has dogs.

proverb_native: Chi ha, è
proverb_en: Who has, is.

proverb_native: Chi ha il lupo per compagno, porti il cane sotto il mantello
proverb_en: Who makes the wolf his companion should carry a dog under his cloak.

proverb_native: Chi ha l’amor nel petto, ha lo sprone a’ fianchi
proverb_en: Who has love in his heart has spurs in his sides.

proverb_native: Chi ha lingua in bocca, può andar per tutto
proverb_en: Who has a tongue in his head can go all the world over.

proverb_native: Chi ha mala lingua, bisogna che abbia buone reni
proverb_en: He who has a bad tongue should have good loins.

proverb_native: Chi ha mangiato il diavolo, mangia anche le corna
proverb_en: He that has swallowed the devil may swallow his horns.

proverb_native: Chi ha passato il guado, sa quant’acqua tiene
proverb_en: He who has crossed the ford knows how deep it is.

proverb_native: Chi ha pazienza, ha i tordi grassi a un quattrin l’uno
proverb_en: Who has patience may get fat thrushes at a farthing apiece.

proverb_native: Chi ha pazienza, vede la sua vendetta
proverb_en: Who has patience sees his revenge.

proverb_native: Chi ha paura del diavolo, non fa roba
proverb_en: He that is afraid of the devil does not grow rich.

proverb_native: Chi ha paura d’ogni fronde, non vada al bosco
proverb_en: Who is in fear of every leaf must not go into the wood.

proverb_native: Chi ha poca vergogna, tutto il mondo è suo
proverb_en: Who hath little shame the world is all his own.

proverb_native: Chi ha qualche cosa, è qualche cosa
proverb_en: Who has something is something.

proverb_native: Chi ha ragion, teme; chi ha torto, spera
proverb_en: Who is in the right fears, who is in the wrong hopes.

proverb_native: Chi ha sospetto, di rado è in difetto
proverb_en: He who suspects is seldom at fault.

proverb_native: Chi ha tegoli di vetro, non tiri sassi al vicino
proverb_en: He who has a glass roof should not throw stones at his neighbour’s.

proverb_native: Chi ha terra, ha guerra
proverb_en: He who has land has war.

proverb_native: Chi ha un sol porco, facilmente l’ingrassa
proverb_en: He that has but one pig easily fattens it.

proverb_native: Chi la fa, se la dimentica, ma non chi la riceve
proverb_en: He who does the wrong forgets it, but not he who receives it.

proverb_native: Chi lingua ha, a Roma va
proverb_en: He who has a tongue, may go to Rome.

proverb_native: Chi mal comincia, peggio finisce
proverb_en: He who begins ill finishes worse.

proverb_native: Chi mal pensa, mal abbia
proverb_en: Evil be to him who evil thinks.

proverb_native: Chi mal semina, mal raccoglie
proverb_en: Who sows ill reaps ill.

proverb_native: Chi mangia peri col suo signore, non sceglie i migliori
proverb_en: He who eats pears with his master should not choose the best.

proverb_native: Chi molte cose comincia, poche ne finisce
proverb_en: He who begins many things finishes few.

proverb_native: Chi mostra i quattrini, mostra il giudizio
proverb_en: He that shows his money shows his judgment.

proverb_native: Chi muta paese, muta ventura
proverb_en: Who changes country changes luck.

proverb_native: Chi muta stato, muta fortuna
proverb_en: Who changes his condition changes fortune.

proverb_native: Chi nasce bella, nasce maritata
proverb_en: She who is born a beauty is born betrothed.

proverb_native: Chi nasce matto, non guarisce mai
proverb_en: Who is born a fool is never cured.

proverb_native: Chi nel fango casca, quanto più si dimena, più s’imbratta
proverb_en: When a man has fallen into the mire, the more he flounders the more he fouls himself.

proverb_native: Chi niente dice, mai non mente
proverb_en: He who says nothing never lies.

proverb_native: Chi niente sa, di niente dubita
proverb_en: He who knows nothing never doubts.

proverb_native: Chi non arde, non accende
proverb_en: Who glows not burns not.

proverb_native: Chi non dà fine al pensare, non dà principio al fare
proverb_en: Who has never done thinking never begins doing.

proverb_native: Chi non fallisce, non arricchisce
proverb_en: He that never fails never grows rich.

proverb_native: Chi non fa quando può, non fa quando vuole
proverb_en: He who does not when he can, cannot when he will.

proverb_native: Chi non ha cuore, abbia gambe
proverb_en: Who hath no courage must have legs.

proverb_native: Chi non ha danari in borsa, abbia miel in bocca
proverb_en: Who has no money in his purse must have honey in his mouth.

proverb_native: Chi non ha debito, non ha credito
proverb_en: He who is without debt is without credit.

proverb_native: Chi non ha figliuoli, non sa che cosa sia amore
proverb_en: Who has no children does not know what love is.

proverb_native: Chi non ha niente, non teme niente
proverb_en: Who has nothing fears nothing.

proverb_native: Chi non ha, non è
proverb_en: Who has not is not.

proverb_native: Chi non ha nulla, non è nulla
proverb_en: Who has nothing is nothing.

proverb_native: Chi non ha piaghe, se ne fa
proverb_en: Who has no plagues makes himself some.

proverb_native: Chi non ha quattrini, non abbia voglie
proverb_en: Who has no money must have no wishes.

proverb_native: Chi non ha testa, abbia gambe
proverb_en: Who has no head should have legs.

proverb_native: Chi non ha vergogna, tutto il mondo è suo
proverb_en: Who has no shame all the world is his own.

proverb_native: Chi non può batter il cavallo, batte la sella
proverb_en: Who cannot beat the horse let him beat the saddle.

proverb_native: Chi non può far la sua vendetta è debole, chi non vuole è vile
proverb_en: He who cannot revenge himself is weak, he who will not is contemptible.

proverb_native: Chi non può, sempre vuole
proverb_en: He who is unable is always willing.

proverb_native: Chi non rompe le uova, non fa la frittata
proverb_en: There is no making pancakes without breaking the eggs.

proverb_native: Chi non sa adulare, non sa conversare
proverb_en: Who knows not how to flatter knows not how to talk.

proverb_native: Chi non sa dissimulare, non sa regnare
proverb_en: Who knows not how to dissemble knows not how to reign.

proverb_native: Chi non sa orare, vada in mare a navigare
proverb_en: Who knows not how to pray let him go sail the sea.

proverb_native: Chi non s’arrischia non guadagna
proverb_en: He who risks nothing can gain nothing.

proverb_native: Chi non stima altri che sè, è felice quanto un re
proverb_en: He who esteems none but himself is as happy as a king.

proverb_native: Chi non va, non vede; chi non prova, non crede
proverb_en: Who goes not, sees not; who proves not, believes not.

proverb_native: Chi non vede il fondo, non passi l’acqua
proverb_en: Don’t cross the water unless you see the bottom.

proverb_native: Chi non vuol affaticarsi in questo mondo, non ci nasca
proverb_en: He that will not strive in this world should not have come into it.

proverb_native: Chi non vuol piedi sul collo, non s’inchini
proverb_en: Who would not have feet set on his neck, let him not stoop.

proverb_native: Chi non vuol rassomigliarsi al lupo, non porti la sua pelle
proverb_en: Who does not wish to be like the wolf let him not wear its skin.

proverb_native: Chi non vuol servir ad un sol signore, a molti ha da servire
proverb_en: He who will not serve one master must needs serve many.

proverb_native: Chiodo con chiodo da se si cava
proverb_en: One nail drives out another.

proverb_native: Chi offende, non dimentichi
proverb_en: Let the injurer not forget.

proverb_native: Chi offende, non perdona mai
proverb_en: The injurer never forgives.

proverb_native: Chi offende, scrive nella rena; chi è offeso nel marmo
proverb_en: Who offends writes on sand; who is offended, on marble.

proverb_native: Chi paga debito, fa capitale
proverb_en: Who pays a debt creates capital.

proverb_native: Chi paga innanzi, è servito indietro
proverb_en: Who pays beforehand is served behindhand.

proverb_native: Chi parla, semina; chi tace, raccoglie
proverb_en: The talker sows, the listener reaps.

proverb_native: Chi perde, ha sempre torto
proverb_en: He who loses is always in fault.

proverb_native: Chi piglia l’anguilla per la coda, e la donna per la parola, può dir che non tiene niente
proverb_en: Who takes an eel by the tail and a woman at her word, may say he holds nothing.

proverb_native: Chi piglia leone in assenza, teme la talpa in presenza
proverb_en: Who takes a lion at a distance fears a mole present.

proverb_native: Chi pinge il fiore, non gli dà l’odore
proverb_en: He that paints a flower does not give it perfume.

proverb_native: Chi più intende, più perdona
proverb_en: Who knows most, forgives most.

proverb_native: Chi più sa, meno crede
proverb_en: Who knows most believes least.

proverb_native: Chi più sa, meno parla
proverb_en: Who knows most says least.

proverb_native: Chi pratica co’grandi, l’ultimo è a tavola, e’l primo a’strapazzi
proverb_en: Who dangles after the great is the last at table and the first to be cuffed.

proverb_native: Chi prende, si vende
proverb_en: Who accepts, sells himself.

proverb_native: Chi primo arriva al molin, primo macina
proverb_en: The first at the mill grinds first.

proverb_native: Chi raro viene, vien bene
proverb_en: Who comes seldom, is welcome.

proverb_native: Chi ride del mal d’altri, ha il suo dietro l’uscio
proverb_en: Who laughs at others’ ills, has his own behind the door.

proverb_native: Chi s’aiuta, Dio l’aiuta
proverb_en: God helps him who helps himself.

proverb_native: Chi sa la strada, può andar di trotto
proverb_en: He who knows the road can ride full trot.

proverb_native: Chi sa meglio nuotare, è il primo a sommergersi
proverb_en: The best swimmer is the first to drown himself.

proverb_native: Chi sa poco, presto lo dice
proverb_en: He who knows but little quickly tells it.

proverb_native: Chi semina spine, non vada scalzo
proverb_en: Who sows thorns should not go barefoot.

proverb_native: Chi serba, serba al gatto
proverb_en: Who saves, saves for the cat.

proverb_native: Chi serve al commune, ha cattivo padrone
proverb_en: He who serves the public has a sorry master.

proverb_native: Chi serve al commune, serve nessuno
proverb_en: Who serves the public, serves no one.

proverb_native: Chi servo si fa, servo s’aspetta
proverb_en: He who makes himself a servant is expected to remain a servant.

proverb_native: Chi si affoga, s’attaccherebbe a’ rasoj
proverb_en: A drowning man would catch at razors.

proverb_native: Chi si è scottato una volta, l’altra vi soffia su
proverb_en: He who has scalded himself once blows the next time.

proverb_native: Chi si fa fango, il porco lo calpestra
proverb_en: He that makes himself dirt is trod on by the swine.

proverb_native: Chi si fa pecorella, i lupi la mangiano
proverb_en: Make yourself a sheep and the wolves will eat you.

proverb_native: Chi si lascia mettere in spalla la capra, indi a poco è sforzato a portar la vacca
proverb_en: He who lets the goat be laid on his shoulders is soon after forced to carry the cow.

proverb_native: Chi si loda, s’imbroda
proverb_en: He who praises himself befouls himself.

proverb_native: Chi si marita in fretta, stenta adagio
proverb_en: Marry in haste, repent at leisure.

proverb_native: Chi si scusa, s’accusa
proverb_en: Who excuses himself accuses himself.

proverb_native: Chi si scusa senz’esser accusato, fa chiaro il suo peccato
proverb_en: Who excuses himself without being accused makes his fault manifest.

proverb_native: Chi soffia nella polvere, se ne empie gli occhi
proverb_en: He who blows upon dust fills his eyes with it.

proverb_native: Chi sta a vedere, ha due terzi del gioco
proverb_en: He who looks on has two-thirds of the game.

proverb_native: Chi sta bene, non si muova
proverb_en: Let him who is well off stay where he is.

proverb_native: Chi tace, acconsente
proverb_en: Silence gives consent.

proverb_native: Chi tardi arriva, mal alloggia
proverb_en: The late comer is ill lodged.

proverb_native: Chi tocca la pece, s’imbratta
proverb_en: He who touches pitch defiles himself.

proverb_native: Chi troppo abbraccia, nulla stringe
proverb_en: He who grasps too much holds nothing fast.

proverb_native: Chi tutto nega, tutto confessa
proverb_en: He who denies all confesses all.

proverb_native: Chi tutto vuole, tutto perde
proverb_en: All covet, all lose.

proverb_native: Chi un ne castiga, cento ne minaccia
proverb_en: He who chastises one threatens a hundred.

proverb_native: Chi va al mulino, s’infarina
proverb_en: He who goes to the mill gets befloured.

proverb_native: Chi va, lecca, e chi sta, secca
proverb_en: Who moves picks up, who stands still dries up.

proverb_native: Chi va piano, va sano, e chi va sano, va lontano
proverb_en: Who goes softly goes safely, and he that goes safely goes far.

proverb_native: Chi va, vuole; chi manda, non se ne cura
proverb_en: Who goes himself is in earnest, who sends is indifferent.

proverb_native: Chi vien dietro, serra l’uscio
proverb_en: The last comer shuts the door.

proverb_native: Chi vive a minuto, fà le spese a’ suoi ed agli altri
proverb_en: He who buys by the pennyworth keeps his own house and other men’s too.

proverb_native: Chi vive tra lupi, impara a urlare
proverb_en: He who lives among wolves learns to howl.

proverb_native: Chi vuol amici assai, ne pruovi pochi
proverb_en: Who would have many friends let him test but few.

proverb_native: Chi vuol ammazzar il suo cane, basta che dica ch’è arrabbiato
proverb_en: He who wants his dog killed has only to say he’s mad.

proverb_native: Chi vuol andar salvo per lo mondo, bisogna aver occhio di falcone, orecchio d’asino, viso di scimia, bocca di porcello, spalle di camello, e gambe di cervo
proverb_en: To go safely through the world you must have the eye of a falcon, the ear of an ass, the face of an ape, the mouth of a pig, the shoulders of a camel, and the legs of a deer.

proverb_native: Chi vuol arricchire in un anno, è impiccato in sei mesi
proverb_en: He who would be rich in a year gets hanged in six months.

proverb_native: Chi vuol assai, non dimandi poco
proverb_en: He who wants a good deal must not ask for a little.

proverb_native: Chi vuol con piacer mangiare, non veda cucinare
proverb_en: He who would relish his food must not see it cooked.

proverb_native: Chi vuol dar al cane, trova facilmente il bastone
proverb_en: He that would beat his dog can easily find a stick.

proverb_native: Chi vuol dell’acqua chiara, vada alla fonte
proverb_en: He who would have clear water should go to the fountain head.

proverb_native: Chi vuol esser lungo tempo vecchio, bisogna cominciar a buon’ora
proverb_en: He who would be long an old man must begin betimes.

proverb_native: Chi vuol esser mal servito tenga assai famiglia
proverb_en: He that would be ill served should keep plenty of servants.

proverb_native: Chi vuol goder la festa, digiuni la vigilia
proverb_en: He who would enjoy the feast should fast on the eve.

proverb_native: Chi vuol il lavoro mal fatto, paghi innanzi tratto
proverb_en: If you would have your work ill done, pay beforehand.

proverb_native: Chi vuol molti amici, non n’ha nessuno
proverb_en: He that seeks to have many friends never has any.

proverb_native: Chi vuol presto e ben, faccia da se
proverb_en: He that would have a thing done quickly and well must do it himself.

proverb_native: Chi vuol Quaresima corta, faccia debiti da pagar a Pasqua
proverb_en: Who wishes for a short Lent let him contract debts to be paid at Easter.

proverb_native: Chi vuol riposare, convien travagliare
proverb_en: He who would rest must work.

proverb_native: Chi vuol saldar piaga, non la maneggi
proverb_en: He that would heal a wound must not handle it.

proverb_native: Chi vuol turar la bocca a tutti, bisogna che abbia assai farina
proverb_en: He who would stop every man’s mouth must have a great deal of meal.

proverb_native: Ciascuno ha un matto nella manica
proverb_en: Every man has a fool in his sleeve.

proverb_native: Ci è chi vede male, e vorrebbe veder peggio
proverb_en: There are some who see ill, and would like to see worse.

proverb_native: Cieco è l’occhio, se l’animo è distratto
proverb_en: The eye is blind if the mind is absent.

proverb_native: Ciò che si usa, non ha scusa
proverb_en: That which is customary requires no excuse.

proverb_native: Città affamata tosto è espugnata
proverb_en: A starved town is soon forced to surrender.

proverb_native: Coda d’asino non fa crivello
proverb_en: An ass’s tail will not make a sieve.

proverb_native: Cogli la rosa, e lascia star le spine
proverb_en: Pluck the rose and leave the thorns.

proverb_native: Colle chiavi d’oro s’apre ogni porta
proverb_en: The golden key opens every door.

proverb_native: Col tempo e con la paglia, si maturan le nespole
proverb_en: With time and straw medlars ripen.

proverb_native: Col Vangelo si può diventare eretici
proverb_en: With the Gospel men may become heretics.

proverb_native: Come l’arbore è caduto, ognun vi corre colla scure a far legna
proverb_en: When the tree is down every one runs to it with a hatchet to cut wood.

proverb_native: Comincia a diventar cattivo chi si tien buono
proverb_en: He begins to grow bad who believes himself good.

proverb_native: Compra il letto d’un gran debitore
proverb_en: Buy the bed of a great debtor.

proverb_native: Con arte e con inganno si vive mezzo l’anno; con inganno e con arte si vive l’altra parte
proverb_en: With art and knavery we live through half the year; with knavery and art we live through the other.

proverb_native: Con la voglia cresce la doglia
proverb_en: With wishing comes grieving.

proverb_native: Con la volpe convien volpeggiare
proverb_en: With the fox one must play the fox.

proverb_native: Conoscon gl’infelici quali siano i veri amici
proverb_en: The unfortunate know who are their real friends.

proverb_native: Con poco cervello si governa il mondo
proverb_en: The world is governed with little brains.

proverb_native: Contento io, contento il mondo
proverb_en: I being satisfied, the world is satisfied.

proverb_native: Contesa vecchia tosto si fa nuova
proverb_en: An old quarrel is easily renewed.

proverb_native: Contro amore non è consiglio
proverb_en: Counsel is nothing against love.

proverb_native: Corpo satollo non crede al digiuno
proverb_en: The full belly does not believe in hunger.

proverb_native: Corre lontano chi non torna mai
proverb_en: He runs far who never turns.

proverb_native: Corta coda non para mosche
proverb_en: A short tail won’t keep off flies.

proverb_native: Corte Romana non vuol pecora senza lana
proverb_en: The court of Rome likes not sheep without wool.

proverb_native: Corvi con corvi non si cavan gli occhi
proverb_en: Ravens do not peck out ravens’ eyes.

proverb_native: Cosa fatta capo ha
proverb_en: A thing done has a head. (The exultation of an ancient sculptor on his satisfactorily completing the head of his statue.)

proverb_native: Costa men del don quel che si compra
proverb_en: What is bought is cheaper than a gift.

proverb_native: Credenza è morta, il mal pagar l’uccise
proverb_en: Credit is dead, bad pay killed it.

proverb_native: Credi al vantatore come al mentitore
proverb_en: Believe a boaster as you would a liar.

proverb_native: Cuor forte rompe cattiva sorte
proverb_en: A stout heart overcomes ill fortune.

proverb_native: Da cattivo pagatore togliete paglia
proverb_en: From a bad paymaster take straw

proverb_native: Da chi mi fido, mi guardi Iddio; da chi non mi fido, mi guarderò io
proverb_en: From those I trust God guard me, from those I mistrust I will guard myself.

proverb_native: Da chi ti dona, guardati
proverb_en: Beware of him who makes you presents.

proverb_native: Dà del tuo a chi ha del suo
proverb_en: Give to him that has.

proverb_native: Dà tempo al tempo
proverb_en: Give time time.

proverb_native: Dal conto sempre manca il lupo
proverb_en: The wolf is always left out of the reckoning.

proverb_native: Dal detto al fatto vi è un gran tratto
proverb_en: From saying to doing is a long way.

proverb_native: Dall’acqua cheta, mi guardi Iddio; che dalla corrente, mi guarderò io
proverb_en: From still water God keep me; from running water I will keep myself.

proverb_native: Dalla mano alla bocca si perde la zuppa
proverb_en: Between the hand and the month the soup is spilt.

proverb_native: Dalla neve o cotta o pesta non caverai altro che acqua
proverb_en: From snow whether cooked or pounded you will get nothing but water.

proverb_native: Dalla rapa non si cava sangue
proverb_en: There is no getting blood from a turnip.

proverb_native: Dallo stesso fior l’ape cava il miele, e la vespe il fiele
proverb_en: From the same flower the bee extracts honey and the wasp gall.

proverb_native: Dall’unghia si conosce il leone
proverb_en: The lion is known by his claws.

proverb_native: Danari di poveri e arme di poltroni si veggono spesso
proverb_en: Poor men’s money and cowards’ weapons are often flourished.

proverb_native: Dannoso è il dono che toglie la libertà
proverb_en: Injurious is the gift that takes away freedom.

proverb_native: Da stagione tutto è buono
proverb_en: Everything is good in its season.

proverb_native: Date bere al prete, che il chierico ha sete
proverb_en: Give the priest drink, for the clerk is thirsty.

proverb_native: De’ grandi e de’ morti o parla bene, o taci
proverb_en: Of the great and of the dead either speak well or say nothing.

proverb_native: Dei gusti non se ne disputa
proverb_en: There’s no disputing about tastes.

proverb_native: Dell’amico bene; del nemico nè bene nè male
proverb_en: Speak well of your friend; of your enemy neither well nor ill.

proverb_native: Del can che morde, il pelo sana
proverb_en: A hair of the dog cures the bite.

proverb_native: Del cervello ognuno si pensa d’averne più che parte
proverb_en: Every one thinks he has more than his share of brains.

proverb_native: Del cuoio d’altri si fan larghe stringhe
proverb_en: Broad thongs may be cut from other men’s leather.

proverb_native: Del giudizio, ognun ne vende
proverb_en: Of judgment every one has a stock on hand for sale.

proverb_native: Dell’albero, non si giudica dalla scorza
proverb_en: The tree is not to be judged of by its bark.

proverb_native: Della sua istessa colpa amor è scusa
proverb_en: Love is an excuse for its own faults.

proverb_native: Del senno di poi è piena ogni fossa
proverb_en: Every ditch is full of after-wit.

proverb_native: Del tempo passato non vi è appello
proverb_en: There is no appeal from time past.

proverb_native: Del tutto non è savio chi non sa far il pazzo
proverb_en: He is not a thorough wise man who cannot play the fool on occasion.

proverb_native: Del vero s’adira l’uomo
proverb_en: It is truth that makes a man angry.

proverb_native: Dentro da un orecchio e fuori dall’altro
proverb_en: In at one ear and out at the other.

proverb_native: De’ peccati de’ signori fanno penitenza i poveri
proverb_en: Poor men do penance for rich men’s sins.

proverb_native: Di’ all’amico il tuo segreto, e ti terrà il piè sul collo
proverb_en: Tell your secret to your friend and he will set his foot on your neck.

proverb_native: Di buona volontà sta pieno l’inferno
proverb_en: Hell is full of good intentions.

proverb_native: Di casa la gatta il topo non esce a corpo pieno
proverb_en: The mouse does not leave the cat’s house with a bellyful.

proverb_native: Dico a te figliuola, intendilo tu nuora
proverb_en: I speak to you, daughter; hear it daughter-in-law.

proverb_native: Di cosa che non ti cale, non dir nè ben nè male
proverb_en: Of what does not concern you say nothing, good or bad.

proverb_native: Di dove meno si pensa, si leva la lepre
proverb_en: The hare starts from where it is least expected.

proverb_native: Di’ il fatto tuo, e lascia far al diavolo
proverb_en: Tell everybody your business and the devil will do it for you.

proverb_native: Di mal erba non si fà buon fieno
proverb_en: Bad grass does not make good hay.

proverb_native: Dimmi con chi vai, e ti dirò chi sei
proverb_en: Tell me the company you keep, and I’ll tell you what you are.

proverb_native: Di notte tutti i gatti sono neri
proverb_en: By night all cats are black.

proverb_native: Di novello tutto par bello
proverb_en: Everything new is beautiful.

proverb_native: Dio ci manda la carne, ma il diavolo i cuochi
proverb_en: God sends meat and the devil sends cooks.

proverb_native: Dio guarisce, e il medico è ringraziato
proverb_en: God heals and the doctor has the thanks.

proverb_native: Dio manda il freddo secondo i panni
proverb_en: God sends cold according to the clothes.

proverb_native: Dio mi guardi da chi non bee
proverb_en: God save me from one who does not drink.

proverb_native: Dio mi guardi da chi studia un libro solo
proverb_en: God save me from him who studies but one book.

proverb_native: Dio mi guardi da chi ha una faccenda sola
proverb_en: God save me from him who has but one occupation.

proverb_native: Dio non manda se non quel che si può portare
proverb_en: God sends nothing but what can be borne.

proverb_native: Di questo mondo ciascuno n’ha quanto se ne toglie
proverb_en: Of this world each man has as much as he takes.

proverb_native: Dio ti salvi da un cattivo vicino, e da un principiante di violino
proverb_en: God save you from a bad neighbour, and from a beginner on the fiddle.

proverb_native: Di padre santalotto figlio diavolotto
proverb_en: The father a saint the son a devil.

proverb_native: Di picciol uomo spesso grand’ombra
proverb_en: A little man often casts a long shadow.

proverb_native: Di rado il medico piglia medicina
proverb_en: The doctor seldom takes physic.

proverb_native: Di rado visto, presto scordato
proverb_en: Seldom seen soon forgotten.

proverb_native: Dispicca l’impiccato, e impiccherà poi te
proverb_en: Take down a thief from the gallows and he will hang you up.

proverb_native: Di tre cose il diavol si fa insalata: di lingue d’avvocati, di dita di notari, e la terza è riservata
proverb_en: Of three things the devil makes a salad: advocates’ tongues, notaries’ fingers, and a third that shall be nameless.

proverb_native: Di tutte le arti maestro è amore
proverb_en: Love is master of all arts.

proverb_native: Di uovo bianco spesso pulcin nero
proverb_en: Out of a white egg often comes a black chick.

proverb_native: Doglia di moglie morta dura fino alla porta
proverb_en: Grief for a dead wife lasts to the door.

proverb_native: Dolce vivanda vuole salsa acerba
proverb_en: Sweet meat requires sour sauce.

proverb_native: Dolor di capo non toglie la corona reale
proverb_en: A crown is no cure for the headache.

proverb_native: Domanda al mio caro se sono ladro
proverb_en: Ask my chum if I am a thief.

proverb_native: Domandar chi nacque prima, l’uovo o la gallina
proverb_en: Ask which was born first, the hen or the egg.

proverb_native: Donar si chiama pescar
proverb_en: Giving is fishing.

proverb_native: Don differito, e troppo aspettato, non è donato, ma caro venduto
proverb_en: A gift delayed, and long expected, is not given, but sold dear.

proverb_native: Donna che dona di rado è buona; donna che piglia è nell’ altrui artiglia
proverb_en: The woman who gives is seldom good; the woman who accepts is in the power of the giver.

proverb_native: Donna di finestra, uva di strada
proverb_en: A woman who loves to be at the window is like a bunch of grapes on the wayside.

proverb_native: Donna si lagna, donna si duole, donna s’ammala quando la vuole
proverb_en: A woman’s in pain, a woman’s in woe, a woman is ill, when she likes to be so.

proverb_native: Donne, asini, e noci voglion le mani atroci
proverb_en: Women, asses, and nuts, require strong hands.

proverb_native: Donne, preti, e polli non son mai satolli
proverb_en: Women, priests, and poultry, never have enough.

proverb_native: Dopo che i cavalli sono presi, serrar la stalla
proverb_en: To lock the stable after the horses are taken.

proverb_native: Dopo il radere non ci è più che tosare
proverb_en: After shaving there is nothing to shear.

proverb_native: Dopo un papa se ne fa un altro
proverb_en: After one pope another is made.

proverb_native: Dove bisognan rimedj, il sospirar non vale
proverb_en: Where remedies are needed, sighing avails not.

proverb_native: Dove ci manca la pelle di leone, convien cucirvi cuoi di volpe
proverb_en: Where the lion’s skin falls short, piece it out with that of the fox.

proverb_native: Dove è grand’amore, quivi è gran dolore
proverb_en: Where there is great love there is great pain.

proverb_native: Dove è il Papa, ivi è Roma
proverb_en: Where the Pope is there is Rome.

proverb_native: Dov’è l’amore, là è l’occhio
proverb_en: Where love is there the eye is.

proverb_native: Dove è manco cuore, quivi è più lingua
proverb_en: Where there is least heart there is most tongue.

proverb_native: Dove entra il bere, se n’esce il sapere
proverb_en: When the wine’s in the wit’s out.

proverb_native: Dove entra il vino, esce la vergogna
proverb_en: When wine enters modesty departs.

proverb_native: Dove il dente duole, la lingua v’inciampa
proverb_en: The tongue goes to where the tooth aches.

proverb_native: Dove il fiume ha più fondo, fa minor strepito
proverb_en: Where the river is deepest it makes least noise.

proverb_native: Dove la siepe è bassa, ognun vuol passare
proverb_en: Where the hedge is low every one will cross it.

proverb_native: Dove la voglia è pronta, le gambe son leggiere
proverb_en: When the will is prompt the legs are nimble.

proverb_native: Dove l’oro parla, ogni lingua tace
proverb_en: When gold speaks every tongue is silent.

proverb_native: Dove l’uscio è basso, bisogna inchinarsi
proverb_en: He must stoop that has a low door.

proverb_native: Dove non basta la pelle del leone, bisogna attaccarvi quella della volpe
proverb_en: Where the skin of the lion does not suffice, we must join that of the fox.

proverb_native: Dove non è egualità, mai perfetto amor sarà
proverb_en: Where there is not equality there never can be perfect love.

proverb_native: Dove non si crede, l’acqua rompe
proverb_en: The water breaks out where it is not expected.

proverb_native: Dove non sono i cani, la volpe è re
proverb_en: Where there are no dogs the fox is a king.

proverb_native: Dove sono molti cuochi, la minestra sarà troppo salata
proverb_en: Where there are too many cooks the soup will be too salty.

proverb_native: Dove il lupo trovò un agnello, ve ne cerca uno novello
proverb_en: Where the wolf found a lamb he looks for another.

proverb_native: Dove va la nave, può andar il brigantino
proverb_en: Where the ship goes the brig can go.

proverb_native: Due donne e un’oca fanno un mercato
proverb_en: Two women and a goose make a market.

proverb_native: Due teste son migliori che una
proverb_en: Two heads are better than one.

proverb_native: Dura più l’incudine che il martello
proverb_en: The anvil lasts longer than the hammer.

proverb_native: Duro a vecchia licenza nuova legge
proverb_en: Hard is a new law imposed on old licence.

proverb_native: Duro con duro non fece mai buon muro
proverb_en: Hard upon hard never made a good wall.

proverb_native: E ardito il gallo sopra il suo letame
proverb_en: The cock is bold on his own dunghill.

proverb_native: E bello predicare il digiuno a corpo pieno
proverb_en: It is easy to preach fasting with a full belly.

proverb_native: E bene aver degli amici per tutto
proverb_en: It is good to have friends everywhere.

proverb_native: E buon comprare quando un altro vuol vendere
proverb_en: It is good to buy when another wants to sell.

proverb_native: E buon tenere i panni a chi nuota
proverb_en: It is good to hold the clothes of one who is swimming.

proverb_native: E come l’ancora, che sta sempre nel mare e non impara mai nuotare
proverb_en: He is like the anchor that is always in the sea, yet does not learn to swim.

proverb_native: E facile far paura al toro dalla finestra
proverb_en: It is easy to threaten a bull from a window.

proverb_native: Egli ben sa dove la spina il punge
proverb_en: He knows well where the thorn pricks him.

proverb_native: Egli dà le pecore in guardia al lupo
proverb_en: He sets the wolf to guard the sheep.

proverb_native: Egli è buono a mandarlo per la morte
proverb_en: He would be a good one to send for death.

proverb_native: Egli ha bel dir bugie chi viene da lontano
proverb_en: He may lie safely who comes from afar.

proverb_native: Egli ha fatto come quel Perugino, che subito che gli fu rotto il capo, corse a casa per la celata
proverb_en: He has done like the Perugian who, when his head was broken, ran home for his helmet.

proverb_native: Egli misura gli altri con la sua canna
proverb_en: He measures others with his own yard.

proverb_native: Egli sa dove il diavolo tiene la coda
proverb_en: He knows where the devil carries his tail.

proverb_native: Egli scannerebbe una cimice per bersi il sangue
proverb_en: He would slaughter a bug to drink its blood.

proverb_native: Egli vende l’uccello in su la frasca
proverb_en: He sells the bird on the branch.

proverb_native: Egli venderebbe sino alla sua parte del sole
proverb_en: He would sell even his share of the sun.

proverb_native: Ei grida pria d’esser battuto
proverb_en: He cries out before he is hurt.

proverb_native: E la peggior ruota quella che fa più rumore
proverb_en: The worst wheel creaks most.

proverb_native: E mala cosa esser cattivo, ma è peggiore esser conosciuto
proverb_en: It is a bad thing to be a knave, but worse to be known for one.

proverb_native: E mal rubare a casa de’ ladri
proverb_en: It is not easy to steal in thieves’ houses.

proverb_native: E meglio aver oggi un uovo che domani una gallina
proverb_en: Better have an egg to-day than a hen to-morrow.

proverb_native: E meglio aver un’ape sola che un esercito di mosche
proverb_en: Better have one bee than a host of flies.

proverb_native: E meglio cader dalla finestra che dal tetto
proverb_en: Better to fall from the window than the roof.

proverb_native: E meglio dare che aver a dare
proverb_en: Better give than have to give.

proverb_native: E meglio dar la lana che la pecora
proverb_en: Better give the wool than the sheep.

proverb_native: E meglio domandar che errare
proverb_en: Better to ask than go astray.

proverb_native: E meglio esser capo di gatto che coda di leone
proverb_en: Better be the head of a cat than the tail of a lion.

proverb_native: E meglio esser capo di lucertola che coda di dracone
proverb_en: Better be the head of a lizard than the tail of a dragon.

proverb_native: E meglio esser solo che mal accompagnato
proverb_en: Better alone than in bad company.

proverb_native: E meglio esser uccel di bosco che di gabbia
proverb_en: Better be a bird of the wood than a bird in the cage.

proverb_native: E meglio il cuor felice che la borsa
proverb_en: A happy heart is better than a full purse.

proverb_native: E meglio invidia che pietà
proverb_en: Better be envied than pitied.

proverb_native: E meglio lasciare che mancare
proverb_en: It is better to leave than to lack.

proverb_native: E meglio perder la sella che il cavallo
proverb_en: Better lose the saddle than the horse.

proverb_native: E meglio piegare che rompere
proverb_en: Better bend than break.

proverb_native: E meglio sdrucciolare col piè che con la lingua
proverb_en: Better slip with the foot than with the tongue.

proverb_native: E meglio sentir cantar il rossignuolo che rodere il topo
proverb_en: It is better to hear the nightingale sing than the mouse gnaw.

proverb_native: E meglio stuzzicare un cane che una vecchia
proverb_en: It is better to irritate a dog than an old woman.

proverb_native: E meglio tardi che mai
proverb_en: Better late than never.

proverb_native: E meglio una volta che mai
proverb_en: Better once than never.

proverb_native: E meglio un magro accordo che una grassa sentenza
proverb_en: Better a lean agreement than a fat sentence.

proverb_native: E meglio un papa vivo che dieci morti
proverb_en: One living pope is better than ten dead.

proverb_native: E meglio un prossimo vicino che un lontano cugino
proverb_en: A near neighbour is better than a distant cousin.

proverb_native: E meglio un uccello in gabbia che cento fuori
proverb_en: A bird in the cage is worth a hundred at large.

proverb_native: E meglio vin torbo che acqua chiara
proverb_en: Thick wine is better than clear water.

proverb_native: E’ non mi morse mai cane ch’io non avessi del suo pelo
proverb_en: A dog never bit me but I had some of his hair.

proverb_native: E’ non si grida mai al lupo che non sia in paese
proverb_en: There is never a cry of “Wolf!” but the wolf is in the district.

proverb_native: E padron del mondo chi lo disprezza, schiavo chi lo apprezza
proverb_en: He is the world’s master who despises it, its slave who prizes it.

proverb_native: E padrone della vita altrui chi la sua sprezza
proverb_en: He is master of another man’s life who is indifferent to his own.

proverb_native: E picciola la punta della spina, ma chi sente il dolore non se ne dimentica
proverb_en: The point of the thorn is small, but he who has felt it does not forget it.

proverb_native: E ricco chi non ha debiti
proverb_en: He is rich who owes nothing.

proverb_native: Erra il prete all’altare
proverb_en: The priest errs at the altar.

proverb_native: Errore non fa pagamento
proverb_en: Error is no payment.

proverb_native: E sempre buono aver due corde al suo arco
proverb_en: It is always good to have two strings to your bow.

proverb_native: E troppo un nemico, e cento amici non bastano
proverb_en: One enemy is too many, and a hundred friends are too few.

proverb_native: E un cattivo boccone quello che affoga
proverb_en: It’s a bad mouthful that chokes.

proverb_native: E un mal giuoco dove nessun guadagna
proverb_en: It is a bad game where nobody wins.

proverb_native: E’ va più d’un asino al mercato
proverb_en: There goes more than one ass to market.

proverb_native: Fa bene, e non guardare a chi
proverb_en: Do good, and care not to whom.

proverb_native: Facilmente si trova un bastone per dar ad un cane
proverb_en: It is easy to find a stick to beat a dog.

proverb_native: Fammi indovino, e ti farò ricco
proverb_en: Make me a prophet and I will make you rich.

proverb_native: Fanciulli piccioli, dolor di testa; fanciulli grandi, dolor di cuore
proverb_en: Little children and headaches, great children and heartaches.

proverb_native: Fa quel che devi, e n’arrivi ciò che potrà
proverb_en: Do what you ought come what may.

proverb_native: Far conto dell’uovo non ancor nato
proverb_en: Don’t reckon your eggs before they are laid.

proverb_native: Far due chiodi ad un caldo
proverb_en: To make two nails at one heat.

proverb_native: Far d’una mosca un elefante
proverb_en: To make an elephant of a fly.

proverb_native: Fare e dire son due cose
proverb_en: Saying and doing are two things.

proverb_native: Farò quel che potrò, e un poco manco per potervi durare
proverb_en: I will do what I can, and a little less, to be able to continue at it.

proverb_native: Fatta la legge, trovata la malizia
proverb_en: No sooner is the law made than its evasion is discovered.

proverb_native: Fatti di miele, e ti mangeran le mosche
proverb_en: Make yourself honey and the flies will eat you.

proverb_native: Felice non è chi d’esser non sa
proverb_en: He is not happy who knows it not.

proverb_native: Femmine e galline per troppo andar si perdono
proverb_en: Women and hens are lost by too much gadding.

proverb_native: Fidati era un buon uomo, Nontifidare era meglio
proverb_en: Trust was a good man, Trust-not was a better.

proverb_native: Figlie e vetri son sempre in pericolo
proverb_en: Lasses and glasses are always in danger.

proverb_native: Finche vi è fiato vi è speranza
proverb_en: While there’s life there’s hope.

proverb_native: Fino alla morte non si sa la sorte
proverb_en: Until death there is no knowing what may befall.

proverb_native: Forte è l’aceto di vin dolce
proverb_en: Strong is the vinegar of sweet wine.

proverb_native: Fortuna, e dormi
proverb_en: Fortune, and go to sleep.

proverb_native: Fra dir e far si guastano scarpe assai
proverb_en: Between saying and doing many a pair of shoes is worn out.

proverb_native: Fra galantuomini parola è istrumento
proverb_en: Among men of honour a word is a bond.

proverb_native: Fra gli amici guardami Iddio, che fra’ nemici mi guarderò io
proverb_en: God keep me from my friends, from my enemies I will keep myself.

proverb_native: Frà Modesto non fu mai priore
proverb_en: Friar Modest never was a prior.

proverb_native: Freno indorato non megliora il cavallo
proverb_en: A golden bit makes none the better horse.

proverb_native: Gatta inguantata non prese mai topo
proverb_en: A muffled cat is no good mouser.

proverb_native: Gatto rinchiuso doventa leone
proverb_en: A cat pent up becomes a lion.

proverb_native: Gente di confini, o ladri o assassini
proverb_en: Borderers are either thieves or murderers.

proverb_native: Gettar la fune dietro la secchia
proverb_en: To throw the rope after the bucket.

proverb_native: Gettar le margherite ai porci
proverb_en: To cast pearls before swine.

proverb_native: Gli alberi grandi fanno più ombra che frutto
proverb_en: Large trees give more shade than fruit.

proverb_native: Gli errori del medico gli copre la terra
proverb_en: The earth covers the errors of the physician.

proverb_native: Gl’Italiani saggi innanzi il fatto, i Tedeschi nel fatto, i Francesi dopo il fatto
proverb_en: The Italians are wise before the act, the Germans in the act, the French after the act.

proverb_native: Gli onori mutano i costumi
proverb_en: Honours change manners.

proverb_native: Gli stornelli son magri perchè vanno in frotta
proverb_en: Starlings are lean because they go in flocks.

proverb_native: Gli uomini alla moderna, e gli asini all’antica
proverb_en: Men after the modern fashion, and asses after the ancient.

proverb_native: Gli uomini fanno la roba, e le donne la conservano
proverb_en: Men make wealth, and women preserve it.

proverb_native: Gli uomini hanno gli anni ch’e’ sentono, e le donne quelli che mostrano
proverb_en: Men are as old as they feel, and women as they look.

proverb_native: Gola d’adulatori, sepolcro aperto
proverb_en: The flatterer’s throat is an open sepulchre.

proverb_native: Granata nuova spazza ben la casa
proverb_en: New brooms sweep clean.

proverb_native: Granata nuova, tre dì buona
proverb_en: A new broom is good for three days.

proverb_native: Gran chiesa e poca divozione
proverb_en: A great church and little devotion.

proverb_native: Gran fumo, poco arrosto
proverb_en: Great smoke, little roast.

proverb_native: Grassa cucina, magro testamento
proverb_en: A fat kitchen, a lean testament.

proverb_native: Guarda che tu non lasci la coda nell’uscio
proverb_en: Take care you don’t let your tail be caught in the door.

proverb_native: Guarda innanzi che tu salti
proverb_en: Look before you leap.

proverb_native: Guardatevi dal “Se io avessi sospettato.”
proverb_en: Beware of “Had I but known.”

proverb_native: Guardati da aceto di vin dolce
proverb_en: Beware of the vinegar of sweet wine.

proverb_native: Guardati da alchimista povero
proverb_en: Beware of a poor alchemist.

proverb_native: Guardati da chi non ha che perdere
proverb_en: Beware of one who has nothing to lose.

proverb_native: Guardati dall’occasione, e ti guarderà Dio da’ peccati
proverb_en: Keep yourself from opportunities and God will keep you from sins.

proverb_native: Guerra cominciata, inferno scatenato
proverb_en: War begun, hell unchained.

proverb_native: I bocconi grossi spesso strozzano
proverb_en: Big mouthfuls often choke.

proverb_native: I buoni nuotatori alfin s’affogano
proverb_en: Good swimmers are drowned at last.

proverb_native: I cani abbaiano a chi non conoscono
proverb_en: Dogs bark at those they don’t know.

proverb_native: I cenni de’ padroni sono commandamenti
proverb_en: Masters’ hints are commands.

proverb_native: I colpi non si danno a patti
proverb_en: Blows are not given upon conditions.

proverb_native: I danari del comune sono come l’acqua benedetta, ognun ne piglia
proverb_en: Public money is like holy water, every one helps himself to it.

proverb_native: I danari sono tondi, e girano
proverb_en: Money is round, and rolls.

proverb_native: I frutti proibiti sono i più dolci
proverb_en: Forbidden fruit is the sweetest.

proverb_native: I gran dolori sono muti
proverb_en: Great griefs are mute.

proverb_native: Il bugiardo deve aver buona memoria
proverb_en: Liars should have good memories.

proverb_native: Il buon marinaro si conosce al cattivo tempo
proverb_en: The good seaman is known in bad weather.

proverb_native: Il buono a qualcosa è l’asino del publico
proverb_en: He that is good for something is the ass of the public.

proverb_native: Il buono è buono, ma il miglior vince
proverb_en: Good is good, but better beats it.

proverb_native: Il buon pagatore dell’altrui borsa è signore
proverb_en: He who pays well is master of another’s purse.

proverb_native: Il buon pastore tosa, e non scortica
proverb_en: The good shepherd shears, not flays.

proverb_native: Il buon sangue giammai non può mentire
proverb_en: Good blood never lies.

proverb_native: Il campanello di camera è il peggior suono che si possa avere negli orecchi
proverb_en: The chamber-bell (chamber-clapper, or curtain lecture) is the worst sound one can have in his ears.

proverb_native: Il can battuto dal bastone, ha paura dell’ombra
proverb_en: The dog that has been beaten with a stick is afraid of its shadow.

proverb_native: Il can che vuol mordere, non abbaia
proverb_en: The dog that means to bite don’t bark.

proverb_native: Il cane scottato dall’acqua calda, ha paura della fredda
proverb_en: The scalded dog fears cold water.

proverb_native: Il cieco non dee giudicar dei colori
proverb_en: A blind man is no judge of colours.

proverb_native: Il danaro è fratello del danaro
proverb_en: Money is money’s brother.

proverb_native: Il danaro è un compendio del poter umano
proverb_en: Money is an epitome of human power.

proverb_native: Il diavolo dove non può mettere il capo vi mette la coda
proverb_en: Where the devil cannot put his head he puts his tail.

proverb_native: Il diavolo è cattivo, perchè è vecchio
proverb_en: The devil is bad because he is old.

proverb_native: Il diavolo non è cosi brutto come si dipinge
proverb_en: The devil is not so ugly as he is painted.

proverb_native: Il diavolo, quand’è vecchio, si fa romito
proverb_en: When the devil is old he turns hermit.

proverb_native: Il diavolo tenta tutti, ma l’ozioso tenta il diavolo
proverb_en: The devil tempts all, but the idle man tempts the devil.

proverb_native: Il diavolo vuol tentar Lucifero
proverb_en: The devil will tempt Lucifer.

proverb_native: Il domandar costa poco
proverb_en: Asking costs little.

proverb_native: Il fatto non si può disfare
proverb_en: What’s done can’t be undone.

proverb_native: Il fin del corsare è annegare
proverb_en: The end of the corsair is to drown.

proverb_native: Il fin loda l’opera
proverb_en: The end praises the work.

proverb_native: Il fiume non s’ingrossa d’acqua chiara
proverb_en: The river does not swell with clear water.

proverb_native: Il fuoco fa saltar le vespe fuor del vespaio
proverb_en: Fire drives the wasp out of its nest.

proverb_native: Il fuoco non s’estingue con fuoco
proverb_en: Fire is not quenched with fire.

proverb_native: Il leone ebbe bisogno del topo
proverb_en: The lion had need of the mouse.

proverb_native: Il lungo giorno nò, ma il cuor fa l’opera
proverb_en: It is not the long day, but the heart that does the work.

proverb_native: Il lupo non è sempre lupo
proverb_en: The wolf is not always a wolf.

proverb_native: Il lupo piange la pecora, poi se la mangia
proverb_en: The wolf bemoans the sheep, and then eats it.

proverb_native: Il male per libra viene, va via per once
proverb_en: Ill luck comes by pounds and goes away by ounces.

proverb_native: Il mangiare insegna a bere
proverb_en: Eating teaches drinking.

proverb_native: Il medico pietoso fa la piaga puzzolente
proverb_en: The tender surgeon makes the wound gangrene.

proverb_native: Il meglio è nemico del bene
proverb_en: Better is an enemy to well.

proverb_native: Il migliore è meno caro
proverb_en: The best is the cheapest.

proverb_native: Il mirto è sempre mirto, benchè sia tra l’ortiche
proverb_en: The myrtle is always a myrtle, though it be among nettles.

proverb_native: Il molino non macina senz’acqua
proverb_en: The mill does not grind without water.

proverb_native: Il mondo è dei flemmatici
proverb_en: The world belongs to the phlegmatic.

proverb_native: Il mondo è di chi ha pazienza
proverb_en: The world is for him who has patience.

proverb_native: Il mondo è fatto a scale, chi le scende e chi le sale
proverb_en: The world is like a staircase; some go up, others go down.

proverb_native: Il mondo sta con tre cose: fare, disfare, e dare ad intendere
proverb_en: The world wags on with three things: doing, undoing, and pretending.

proverb_native: Il mortaio sa sempre d’aglio
proverb_en: The mortar always smells of garlic.

proverb_native: Il nemico ti fa savio
proverb_en: Your enemy makes you wise.

proverb_native: Il pane degli altri è troppo salato
proverb_en: Others’ bread is too salt.

proverb_native: Il pane degli altri ha sette croste
proverb_en: Others’ bread has seven crusts.

proverb_native: Il pane mangiato è presto dimenticato
proverb_en: Eaten bread is soon forgotten.

proverb_native: Il parer proprio non ha mai torto
proverb_en: A man’s own opinion is never wrong.

proverb_native: Il pesce grande mangia il picciolo
proverb_en: The big fish eat the little.

proverb_native: Il più difficile è il metter il piè in istaffa
proverb_en: The difficult thing is to get foot in the stirrup.

proverb_native: Il più duro passo è quello della soglia
proverb_en: The hardest step is that over the threshold.

proverb_native: Il più forte ha sempre ragione
proverb_en: The strongest is always in the right.

proverb_native: Il primier colpo per due colpi vale
proverb_en: The first blow is as good as two.

proverb_native: Il riso fa buon sangue
proverb_en: Laughter makes good blood.

proverb_native: Il sacco de’ mendici non ha fondo
proverb_en: The beggar’s wallet has no bottom.

proverb_native: Il sangue del soldato fa grande il capitano
proverb_en: The soldier’s blood exalts the captain.

proverb_native: Il satollo non crede al digiuno
proverb_en: The well-fed man does not believe in hunger.

proverb_native: Il secondo pensiero è il migliore
proverb_en: Second thoughts are best.

proverb_native: Il soldato per far male è ben pagato
proverb_en: The soldier is well paid for doing mischief.

proverb_native: Il tempo buono viene una volta sola
proverb_en: The good time comes but once.

proverb_native: Il tempo è una lima sorda
proverb_en: Time is an inaudible file.

proverb_native: I lupi non si mangiano l’un l’altro
proverb_en: Wolves do not eat each other.

proverb_native: Il veleno si spegne col veleno
proverb_en: Poison quells poison.

proverb_native: I matrimonj sono, non come si fanno, ma come riescono
proverb_en: Marriages are not as they are made, but as they turn out.

proverb_native: I matti fanno le feste, ed i savj le godono
proverb_en: Fools make feasts, and wise men eat them.

proverb_native: I migliori alberi sono i più battuti
proverb_en: The best trees are the most beaten.

proverb_native: I mosconi rompon le tele de’ ragni
proverb_en: Big flies break the spider’s web.

proverb_native: In bocca chiusa non cade pera
proverb_en: No pear falls into a shut mouth.

proverb_native: In bocca chiusa non c’entran mosche
proverb_en: No flies get into a shut mouth.

proverb_native: In cammino battuto erba non cresce
proverb_en: No grass grows on a beaten road.

proverb_native: In casa, Argo; di fuori, talpa
proverb_en: Argus at home, a mole abroad.

proverb_native: Indarno si tende la rete in vista degli uccelli
proverb_en: It is in vain to lay a net in sight of the birds.

proverb_native: In guaina d’oro coltello di piombo
proverb_en: In a golden sheath a leaden knife.

proverb_native: In lungo viaggio, anche una paglia pesa
proverb_en: On a long journey even a straw is heavy.

proverb_native: In picciol tempo passa ogni gran pioggia
proverb_en: A heavy shower is soon over.

proverb_native: In quella casa è poca pace ove la gallina canta, ed il gallo tace
proverb_en: There is little peace in that house where the hen crows and the cock is mute.

proverb_native: Insegnando s’impara
proverb_en: We learn by teaching.

proverb_native: In tempo di guerra ogni cavallo ha soldo
proverb_en: In war time there is pay for every horse.

proverb_native: In terra di ciechi beato chi ha un occhio
proverb_en: In the country of the blind blessed is he that hath one eye.

proverb_native: In un giorno non si fe’ Roma
proverb_en: Rome was not built in a day.

proverb_native: Invano si pesca, se l’amo non ha esca
proverb_en: It is vain to fish if the hook is not baited.

proverb_native: I paragoni son tutti odiosi
proverb_en: Comparisons are odious.

proverb_native: I pazzi crescono senza innaffiarli
proverb_en: Fools grow without watering.

proverb_native: I pazzi per lettera sono i maggiori pazzi
proverb_en: There is no fool like a learned fool.

proverb_native: I piccioli ladri s’impiccano per la gola, i grossi per la borsa
proverb_en: Little thieves are hanged by the neck, great ones by the purse.

proverb_native: I principi hanno le braccia lunghe
proverb_en: Princes have long arms.

proverb_native: I salici son deboli, e pur legano le legne più grosse
proverb_en: Willows are weak, yet serve to bind bigger wood.

proverb_native: La barba non fa il filosofo
proverb_en: The beard does not make the philosopher.

proverb_native: L’abito è una seconda natura
proverb_en: Custom is second nature.

proverb_native: L’abito non fa il monaco
proverb_en: The gown does not make the friar.

proverb_native: La bonaccia burrasca minaccia
proverb_en: A calm portends a storm.

proverb_native: La borsa degli amanti va legata con fil di ragno
proverb_en: Lovers’ purses are tied with cobwebs.

proverb_native: La botte non può dare se non del vino ch’ella ha
proverb_en: The cask can give no other wine than that it contains.

proverb_native: La botte piena non fa rumore
proverb_en: The full cask makes no noise.

proverb_native: La buona fama è come il cipresso: una volta tagliato non rinverdisce più
proverb_en: Good repute is like the cypress: once cut, it never puts forth leaf again.

proverb_native: La buona roba non fu mai cara
proverb_en: Good ware was never dear.

proverb_native: La carta non diventa rossa
proverb_en: Paper does not blush.

proverb_native: La chiave d’oro apre ogni porta
proverb_en: The golden key opens every door.

proverb_native: La coda sempre è la più cattiva da scorticare
proverb_en: The tail is always the hardest part to flay.

proverb_native: La commodità fa l’uomo ladro
proverb_en: Opportunity makes the thief.

proverb_native: La coscienza vale per mille testimonj
proverb_en: Conscience is as good as a thousand witnesses.

proverb_native: La diritta è serva della mancina
proverb_en: The right hand is slave to the left.

proverb_native: La donna all’improvviso, e l’uomo a caso pensato
proverb_en: Woman (decides), impromptu; man, on reflection.

proverb_native: La fame caccia il lupo del bosco
proverb_en: Hunger drives the wolf out of the wood.

proverb_native: La fame è il meglior intingolo
proverb_en: Hunger is the best sauce.

proverb_native: La fame muta le fave in mandole
proverb_en: Hunger transmutes beans into almonds.

proverb_native: La forca è fatta per i disgraziati
proverb_en: The gallows was made for the unlucky.

proverb_native: La fortuna aiuta i pazzi
proverb_en: Fortune helps fools.

proverb_native: La fuga del nemico abbi sospetta
proverb_en: Look with suspicion on the flight of an enemy.

proverb_native: La gatta vorrebbe mangiar pesci, ma non pescare
proverb_en: The cat loves fish, but is loth to wet her feet.

proverb_native: Lagrime di donna, fontana di malizia
proverb_en: Women’s tears are a fountain of craft.

proverb_native: La guerra fa i ladri, e la pace gl’impicca
proverb_en: War makes robbers, and peace hangs them.

proverb_native: La luna non cura dell’abbaiar de’ cani
proverb_en: The moon does not heed the baying of dogs.

proverb_native: La madre pietosa fa la figliuola tignosa
proverb_en: A tender-hearted mother makes a scabby daughter.

proverb_native: La mal erba cresce presto
proverb_en: Ill weeds grow apace.

proverb_native: L’amicizia si de’ sdruscire, non istracciare
proverb_en: Friendship should be unpicked, not rent.

proverb_native: L’amico non è conosciuto finchè non è perduto
proverb_en: A friend is not known till he is lost.

proverb_native: L’ammalato dorme quando il debitor non dorme
proverb_en: The sick man sleeps when the debtor cannot.

proverb_native: La moglie e il ronzino piglia dal vicino
proverb_en: For a wife and a horse go to your neighbour.

proverb_native: La mosca che punge la tartaruga si rompe il becco
proverb_en: The fly that bites the tortoise breaks its beak.

proverb_native: La mosca ha la sua milza
proverb_en: Even a fly has its spleen.

proverb_native: La nave non va senza il battello
proverb_en: The ship does not go without the boat.

proverb_native: La necessità torna in volontà
proverb_en: Necessity becomes will.

proverb_native: La nobiltà è una povera vivanda in tavola
proverb_en: High birth is a poor dish on the table.

proverb_native: La padella dice al paiuolo: Fatti in là, che tu mi tigni
proverb_en: The pan says to the pot: Keep off, or you’ll smutch me.

proverb_native: La parola non è mal detta, se non è mal presa
proverb_en: Nothing is ill said if it is not ill taken.
