proverb_en: Put your hand in your conscience and see if it don’t come out as black as pitch.

proverb_native: Steek uw vinger in geen dwazens mond
proverb_en: Thrust not thy finger in a fool’s mouth.

proverb_native: Steek uw voeten niet verder dan uw bed reikt
proverb_en: Stretch your legs no farther than your coverlet.

proverb_native: Stille waters hebben diepe gronden
proverb_en: Still waters are deep.

proverb_native: Streelje den hond, hij bederft uw kleed
proverb_en: Caress your dog, and he’ll spoil your clothes.

proverb_native: Strooit geen rozen voor varkens
proverb_en: Strew no roses before swine.

proverb_native: Tegen stroom is kwaad zwemmen
proverb_en: It is hard to swim against the stream.

proverb_native: Tegen wind en stroom kan men niet opzeilen
proverb_en: It is ill sailing against wind and tide.

proverb_native: Te veel is kwaad genoeg, zij dat wat gij geniet; een schip vaart veiligst door een’ niet te sterken vliet
proverb_en: Rejoice in little, shun what is extreme; the ship rides safest in a little stream.

proverb_native: Te veel van een ding is nergens nut toe
proverb_en: Too much of one thing is good for nothing.

proverb_native: Tijd en plaats doen den dief stelen
proverb_en: Time and place make the thief.

proverb_native: Tijd gewonnen, veel gewonnen
proverb_en: Time gained, much gained.

proverb_native: Tijd is geld
proverb_en: Time is money.

proverb_native: Toen Adam spitte en Eva span, waar vond men toen den edelman?
proverb_en: When Adam delved and Eve span, who was then the gentleman?

proverb_native: Trekt men één varken bij den staart dan schreeuwen zij allen
proverb_en: If you pull one pig by the tail all the rest squeak.

proverb_native: Tucht baart vrucht
proverb_en: Correction bringeth fruit.

proverb_native: Tusschen den hamer en het aanbeeld
proverb_en: Between the hammer and the anvil.

proverb_native: Tusschen lepel en mond, valt het sop te grond
proverb_en: ’Twixt the spoon and the lip, the morsel may slip.

proverb_native: Tusschen twee stoelen valt de aars op de aarde
proverb_en: Between two stools the breech cometh to the ground.

proverb_native: Twee appelen met éénen stok afwerpen
proverb_en: To bring down two apples with one stick.

proverb_native: Twee hanen in een huis, de kat met de muis, een oud man en een ijongwn wijf geeft eeuwig gekijf
proverb_en: Two cocks in one house, a cat and a mouse, an old man and young wife, are always in strife.

proverb_native: Twee honden aan een been komen zelden overeen
proverb_en: Two dogs seldom agree over one bone.

proverb_native: Twee vliegen met éénen klap slaan
proverb_en: To kill two birds with one stone.

proverb_native: Uit den overvloed des harten spreekt de mond
proverb_en: Out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh.

proverb_native: Uit het oog, uit het hart
proverb_en: Out of sight, out of mind.

proverb_native: Vaart gij dan oost, of vaarje west, eens eigen huis is alder best
proverb_en: Travel east or travel west, a man’s own house is still the best.

proverb_native: Van daag voor geld, en morgen voor niet
proverb_en: To-day for money, to-morrow for nothing.

proverb_native: Van de boot komt men in het schip
proverb_en: From the boat we get to the ship.

proverb_native: Van den hak op den tak springen
proverb_en: To make coqs-à-l’âne.

proverb_native: Van den nood eene deugd maken
proverb_en: To make a virtue of necessity.

proverb_native: Van den wal in de sloot vallen
proverb_en: To fall from the wall into the ditch.

proverb_native: Van de vonken brandt ’t huis
proverb_en: From a spark the house is burnt.

proverb_native: Van dreigen sterft man niet
proverb_en: Threats don’t kill.

proverb_native: Van een anders leer is goed riemen snijden
proverb_en: It is pleasant to cut thongs of another man’s leather.

proverb_native: Van een’ vlieg een’ olijfant maaken
proverb_en: To make an elephant of a fly.

proverb_native: Van ’t klein komt men tot ’t groote
proverb_en: From little things men go on to great.

proverb_native: Van kleine beginselen komt men tot groote zaken
proverb_en: From small beginnings come great things.

proverb_native: Van kleine dingen komt dikwijls groote hinder
proverb_en: From trivial things great contests oft arise.

proverb_native: Van twee kwalen moet men de ergste mijden
proverb_en: Of two evils choose the least.

proverb_native: Van vader komt eere, van moeder gemak
proverb_en: From the father comes honour, from the mother comfort.

proverb_native: Van verre gehaalt en duur gekocht, is eten voor me vrouwen
proverb_en: Far fetched and dear bought is meat for ladies.

proverb_native: Veeg eerst voor uwe eigene deur, en dan voor die uws buurmans
proverb_en: Sweep before your own door before you look after your neighbour’s.

proverb_native: Veele handen maaken ligt werk
proverb_en: Many hands make light work.

proverb_native: Veele woorden vullen geen zak
proverb_en: Many words don’t fill the sack.

proverb_native: Veel geschreeuw maar weinig wol, zei de drommel, en hij schoor zijne varkens
proverb_en: Great cry and little wool, quoth the devil, when he sheared his hogs.

proverb_native: Veel geschreeuws, en luttel wol
proverb_en: Great cry and little wool.

proverb_native: Veel herders by de schapen, zullen maar te langer slapen
proverb_en: When many shepherds tend the sheep, they but so much the longer sleep.

proverb_native: Veel honden is der hazen dood
proverb_en: Many hounds are the death of the hare.

proverb_native: Veel hoofden, veel zinnen
proverb_en: Many heads, many minds.

proverb_native: Veel kleintjes maken een groot
proverb_en: Many littles make a mickle.

proverb_native: Veel koks verzouten de brij
proverb_en: Too many cooks oversalt the porridge.

proverb_native: Veel praats en weinig werks
proverb_en: Much talk little work.

proverb_native: Velen openen een deur om een venster te sluiten
proverb_en: Many open a door to shut a window.

proverb_native: Verdraagt het geen gij door uw eigen schuld lijdt
proverb_en: Bear patiently that which thou sufferest by thine own fault.

proverb_native: Verkoop den huid niet, voor gij den beer hebt gevangen
proverb_en: Don’t sell the skin till you’ve caught the bear.

proverb_native: Verloren eer, keert nimmermeer
proverb_en: Honour once lost never returns.

proverb_native: Verstandigen staan naar ampten en de dom-ooren krijgen ze
proverb_en: Wise men sue for offices, and blockheads get them.

proverb_native: Viel den hemel, daar en bleef geen aarden pot heel
proverb_en: Were the sky to fall, not an earthen pot would be left whole.

proverb_native: Voet voor voet gaat men ver
proverb_en: Step by step one goes far.

proverb_native: Vogels van gelijke veeren vliegen graag t’zamen
proverb_en: Birds of a feather flock together.

proverb_native: Volle flesschen en glazen maken vloekers en dwazen
proverb_en: Full bottles and glasses make swearers and asses.

proverb_native: Voor dag uit, voor nacht in
proverb_en: Out before day, in before night.

proverb_native: Voor groote kwalen sterke hulpmiddelen
proverb_en: For great evils strong remedies.

proverb_native: Voor stroom en wind is goed zeilen
proverb_en: It is good sailing with wind and tide.

proverb_native: Voorzigtigheid is de moeder der fijne bierglazen
proverb_en: Caution is the parent of delicate beer-glasses.

proverb_native: Vrienden kost is haast gereed
proverb_en: A friend’s dinner is soon dressed.

proverb_native: Vroeg gras, vroeg hooi
proverb_en: Soon grass, soon hay.

proverb_native: Vroeg of laat komt de waarheid aan den dag
proverb_en: Sooner or later the truth comes to light.

proverb_native: Vroeg rijp, vroeg rot; vroeg wijs, vroeg zot
proverb_en: Soon ripe, soon rotten; soon wise, soon foolish.

proverb_native: Vroeg vuur, vroeg asch
proverb_en: Soon fire, soon ashes.

proverb_native: Waar de bije honig uit zuigt, zuigt de spin venijn uit
proverb_en: Where the bee sucks honey, the spider sucks poison.

proverb_native: Waar de dijk (of dam) het laagst is, loopt het water ’t eerst over
proverb_en: Where the dike (or dam) is lowest the water first runs over.

proverb_native: Waar de hegge het laagste is, wil elk er over
proverb_en: Where the hedge is lowest every one goes over.

proverb_native: Waar de vlijt de deur uit gaat, komt de armoede het venster in
proverb_en: When industry goes out of the door, poverty comes in at the window.

proverb_native: Waar men vruchtboomen zet valt niet op vruchten te rekenen
proverb_en: He who plants fruit-trees, must not count upon the fruit.

proverb_native: Waar niet is, verliest de keizer zijn regt
proverb_en: Where there is nothing, the emperor loses his right.

proverb_native: Wacht u voor een’ man met twee aangezigten
proverb_en: Beware of the man of two faces.

proverb_native: Wanneer de wijn is in de man, dan is de wijsheid in de kan
proverb_en: When the wine is in the man, the wit is in the can.

proverb_native: Wanneer dieven kijven bekomen vrome lieden hare goederen
proverb_en: When thieves fall out, honest men come to their goods.

proverb_native: Wanneer een boom ter aarde zijgt, maakt ieder dat hij takken krijgt
proverb_en: When the tree falls every one runs to cut boughs.

proverb_native: Wapenen, vrouwen, en boeken, behoeven dagelijksche behandeling
proverb_en: Arms, women, and books should be looked at daily.

proverb_native: Was, vlas, en tin; voor groot geld klein gewin
proverb_en: Wax, flax, and tin; much out and little in.

proverb_native: Wat de nuchtere denkt, dat spreekt de dronkaard
proverb_en: What the sober man thinks, the drunkard tells.

proverb_native: Wat de ouden zingen, piepen de ijongen
proverb_en: The old ones sing, the young ones pipe.

proverb_native: Water in eenen korf putten
proverb_en: To put water into a basket.

proverb_native: Water in de zee brengen
proverb_en: To carry water to the sea.

proverb_native: Wat gij den armen geeft, leent gij den Heer
proverb_en: He who giveth to the poor lendeth to the Lord.

proverb_native: Wat het oog niet en ziet, dat begeert het herte niet
proverb_en: What the eye sees not, the heart craves not.

proverb_native: Wat horens heeft wil steken
proverb_en: What has horns will gore.

proverb_native: Wat in ’t gebeente gegroeid is, wil uit het vleesch niet
proverb_en: What is bred in the bone won’t out of the flesh.

proverb_native: Wat niets en kost en deugd niet
proverb_en: What costs nothing is worth nothing.

proverb_native: Wat u niet brand, dat koel niet
proverb_en: That which burns thee not, cool not.

proverb_native: Wat van daag onregt is, is morgen geen regt
proverb_en: What is wrong to-day won’t be right to-morrow.

proverb_native: Weinig gezegd is haast verbeterd
proverb_en: The less said the sooner mended.

proverb_native: Weinig houts veel vruchten
proverb_en: Little wood, much fruit.

proverb_native: Weinig met eer, wat behoef je meer
proverb_en: With honour and store, what would you more.

proverb_native: Wel voorgaan doet wel volgen
proverb_en: Good leading makes good following.

proverb_native: Werelds goed is eb en vloed
proverb_en: Worldly good is ebb and flood.

proverb_native: Wie de roos wil plukken moet de doornen niet ontzien
proverb_en: He who would gather roses, must not fear thorns.

proverb_native: Wie een’ schalk wil vangen, moet achter de deur staan
proverb_en: He who would catch a rogue must watch behind the door.

proverb_native: Wie geen dorst heeft, wat doet hij bij de fontein
proverb_en: Who has no thirst has no business at the fountain.

proverb_native: Wie in zijn 23^e jaar niet sterft, in zijn 24^e niet verdrinkt, en in zijn 25^e niet wordt verslagen, die mag wel spreken van goede dagen
proverb_en: He who dies not in his twenty-third year, drowns not in his twenty-fourth, and is not slain in his twenty-fifth, may boast of good days.

proverb_native: Wie is ’t die zonder op-spraak blijft, die voor de wereld spreekt of schrijft?
proverb_en: Who can escape envy and blame, that speaks or writes for public fame?

proverb_native: Wie loopt, die wordt gejaagd
proverb_en: Who runs is followed.

proverb_native: Wie op twee hazen te gelijk jaagt, vangt geen van beide
proverb_en: He who hunts two hares at once, catches neither.

proverb_native: Wie pepers te veel heeft die pepert zijne boonen
proverb_en: Who has plenty of pepper may pepper his beans.

proverb_native: Wie tegen wind spuwt, maakt zijn baard vuil
proverb_en: Who spits against the wind, fouls his beard.

proverb_native: Wie veeltijds spiegelen, zelden spinnen
proverb_en: They who are often at the looking-glass seldom spin.

proverb_native: Wie zich onder den draf mengt, dien eten de zwijnen
proverb_en: He who mixes himself with the draff will be eaten by the swine.

proverb_native: Wie zijn buren beledigt, maakt het zich zelven daarna zuur
proverb_en: He who slanders his neighbour makes a rod for himself.

proverb_native: Wie zijn kind straft, die heeft eere van hem; wie het niet straft, die zal schande beleven
proverb_en: Who chastises his child will be honoured by him, who chastises him not will be shamed.

proverb_native: Wie zonder eten gaat te bed, dien wordt het slapen ligt belet
proverb_en: Who goes fasting to bed will sleep but lightly.

proverb_native: Wij appelen zwemmen, zei de paardenkeutel
proverb_en: How we apples swim! said the horse-t—d.

proverb_native: Wijsheid in mans, geduld in vrouwen, dat kan het huis in ruste houen
proverb_en: Wisdom in the man, patience in the wife, brings peace to the house, and a happy life.

proverb_native: Wijst mij een’ leugenaar, en ik wijs u een’ dief
proverb_en: Show me a liar, I’ll show you a thief.

proverb_native: Wil men alle dingen met goede oogen aanzien dan moet men staâg door de vingeren kijken
proverb_en: Who would regard all things complacently must wink at a great many.

proverb_native: Zaai geen geld op zee: ’t moet zinken
proverb_en: Sow not money on the sea, lest it sink.

proverb_native: Zacht gaan en verre zien
proverb_en: Go softly and look afar.

proverb_native: Zachte meesters maken stinkende wonden
proverb_en: Tender surgeons make foul wounds.

proverb_native: Zachtzens en soergens gaat verre
proverb_en: Soft and fair goeth far.

proverb_native: Zegt ons met wie dat gij verkeert, en heb ik uwen raad geleerd
proverb_en: Tell me the company you keep, and I’ll tell you what you are.

proverb_native: Zelfs lief niemands lief
proverb_en: Self-love nobody else’s love

proverb_native: Zet u teeringe, na u neeringe
proverb_en: Set thy expense according to thy trade.

proverb_native: Zich bij den neus laten leiden
proverb_en: To be led by the nose.

proverb_native: Zij bijten niet al die haar tanden laten zien
proverb_en: All do not bite that show their teeth.

proverb_native: Zij leven te zamen als honden en katten
proverb_en: They agree like cats and dogs.

proverb_native: Zijn geld bekleed bij hem de plaats van wijsheid
proverb_en: His money takes the place of wisdom.

proverb_native: Zij steekt den bezem uit
proverb_en: She hangs out the broom (wants a husband).

proverb_native: Zij verstaan elkander als dieven op eene kermis
proverb_en: They understand one another like thieves in a fair.

proverb_native: Zij zijn niet allen gelijk die met den keizer rijden
proverb_en: All are not princes who ride with the emperor.

proverb_native: Zoo de gekken geen brood aten, het koren zogoed koop zijn
proverb_en: If fools ate no bread, corn would be cheap.

proverb_native: Zoo gewonnen, zoo geronnen
proverb_en: So got, so gone.

proverb_native: Zoo gij een gek de vingers bied, ’t is vreemd neemt hij de vuisten niet
proverb_en: Offer a clown your finger, and he’ll take your fist.

proverb_native: Zoo gij zaait zoo zult gij maaien
proverb_en: As you sow you shall reap.

proverb_native: Zoo pot, zoo deksel
proverb_en: Like pot, like cover.

proverb_native: Zoo veel hoofden, zoo veel zinnen
proverb_en: So many men, so many minds.

proverb_native: Zuinigheid is een groote rente
proverb_en: Economy is a great revenue.

proverb_native: Zulk begin, zulk einde
proverb_en: So begun, so done.

proverb_native: Zulke heer, zulke knecht
proverb_en: Like master, like man.

proverb_native: Zware beurzen en ligte harten kunnen veel verzetten
proverb_en: Heavy purses and light hearts can sustain much.

proverb_native: Zweegen de dwazen zij waren wijs
proverb_en: Were fools silent they would pass for wise.

proverb_native: Zwijgen antwoordt veel
proverb_en: Silence answers much.

proverb_native: Aaret har en vid Mund og en stor Mave
proverb_en: The year has a wide mouth and a big belly.

proverb_native: Adam fik en Hak og Eva fik en Rok, deraf er al vor Adels-Flok
proverb_en: Adam got a hoe, and Eve got a spinning-wheel, and thence come all our nobles.

proverb_native: Af Falsk og Svig vorder ingen riig
proverb_en: Deceit and treachery make no man rich.

proverb_native: Af Læp vorder Hund læderaadig
proverb_en: By gnawing skin a dog learns to eat leather.

proverb_native: Af liden Gnist kommer ofte stor Ild
proverb_en: A large fire often comes from a small spark.

proverb_native: Af Ögler komme Ögleunger
proverb_en: Vipers breed vipers.

proverb_native: Af Skade bliver man klog, men sielden rig
proverb_en: Damage suffered makes you knowing, but seldom rich.

proverb_native: Af to onde Kaar skal man vælge det bedste
proverb_en: Of two evils choose the least.

proverb_native: Alderdom er ond Reisebroder
proverb_en: Age is a sorry travelling companion.

proverb_native: Alderdom giör mangen hvidere, men ikke bedre
proverb_en: Age makes many a man whiter, but not better.

proverb_native: Aldrig er Fugl saa liden, söger jo eget Bo
proverb_en: A bird may be ever so small, it always seeks a nest of its own.

proverb_native: Aldrig er saa gammel en Kiærling, kommer der Ild i hende hun springer jo
proverb_en: A woman may be ever so old, if she takes fire she will jump.

proverb_native: Aldrig læges Saar saa vel, at Arret jo synes
proverb_en: A wound never heals so well that the scar cannot be seen.

proverb_native: Aldrig var færre Adel, end naar hver vil være det
proverb_en: There were never fewer nobles than when all would be so.

proverb_native: Alle Baader hiælpe, sagde Soen, hun greb et Myg
proverb_en: Every little helps, said the sow, when she snapped at a gnat.

proverb_native: Alle Nögle ere ei bundne ved en Kones Laar
proverb_en: All keys hang not at one woman’s girdle.

proverb_native: Alle Qvinder ere gode lutherske de prædike heller end de höre Messe
proverb_en: All women are good Lutherans, they would rather preach than hear mass.

proverb_native: Alle see hans bolde Arm, ingen seer hans slunkne Tarm
proverb_en: Every one sees his smart coat, no one sees his shrunken belly.

proverb_native: Alle ville længe leve, men Ingen vil gammel hedde
proverb_en: All wish to live long, but none to be called old.

proverb_native: Almisse tömmer ei Pung, og ei Messe Dagsfærd
proverb_en: Alms do not empty the purse, and a mass does not exhaust the day’s duty.

proverb_native: Alting har en Ende—uden Pölsen, den har to
proverb_en: Everything has an end—except a sausage, which has two.

proverb_native: Altid at spare, er altid at fattes
proverb_en: Always to be sparing is always to be in want.

proverb_native: Alt leer folsk Mand, naar Anden leer
proverb_en: A fool laughs when others laugh.

proverb_native: Alt Vand vil til Strand, og Pengene til rig Mands Haand
proverb_en: As water runs towards the shore, so does money towards the rich man’s hand.

proverb_native: Alt voxer Rakketand, mens gammel Hund bider Been
proverb_en: The teeth of the puppy are growing, while the old dog is gnawing bones.

proverb_native: Alvor og Gammen kunne bedst sammen
proverb_en: Earnestness and sport go well together.

proverb_native: Anden Tid giver andet Folk
proverb_en: Other times, other folk.

proverb_native: Arbeide har en bitter Rod, men söd Smag
proverb_en: Labour has a bitter root, but a sweet taste.

proverb_native: Arm er den Muus som kun har et Hul
proverb_en: It is a poor mouse that has but one hole.

proverb_native: Armod og Kiærlighed ere onde at dölge
proverb_en: Love and poverty are hard to conceal.

proverb_native: Arne Bande bider bedst
proverb_en: The curse on the hearth wounds the deepest.

proverb_native: Arrig Quinde og bidsk Hund vogte Huset
proverb_en: A cross-grained woman and a snappish dog take care of the house.

proverb_native: At bede og smöre sinker ei at kiöre
proverb_en: To bait and to grease does not retard a journey.

proverb_native: At bie og lide, stiller mangen en Qvide
proverb_en: To wait and be patient soothes many a pang.

proverb_native: At sige Daaren Raad, det er som at slaae Vand paa en Gaas
proverb_en: To give counsel to a fool is like throwing water on a goose.

proverb_native: At skære i fremmed Öre er ikke anderledes end i Filtehat
proverb_en: To cut into another man’s ear is like cutting into a felt hat.

proverb_native: At tie Sandhed, er at begrave Guld
proverb_en: To withhold truth is to bury gold.

proverb_native: At vide Lov og giöre Ret er tvende haande
proverb_en: To know the law and do the right are two things.

proverb_native: Ave er god naar hun kommer i Tide
proverb_en: Correction is good when administered in time.

proverb_native: Avind kommer ikke i öde Huus
proverb_en: Envy does not enter an empty house.

proverb_native: Avindsyg er sin egen Böddel
proverb_en: Envy is its own torturer.

proverb_native: Avind var aldrig god Talsmand
proverb_en: Envy was never a good spokesman.

proverb_native: Ærlig Mand er ei disværre, at en Hund göer ad ham
proverb_en: An honest man is not the worse because a dog barks at him.

proverb_native: Bag efter kommer tyndt Öl
proverb_en: Small beer comes the last.

proverb_native: Bande bider ei Öie ud, uden Næven fölger med
proverb_en: A curse will not strike out an eye, unless the fist goes with it.

proverb_native: Bange Hierte vandt aldrig fager Mö
proverb_en: Faint heart never won fair lady.

proverb_native: Barneryg vil böies i Tide
proverb_en: A child’s back must be bent early.

proverb_native: Barn skal krybe til det lærer at gaae
proverb_en: A child must creep until it learns to walk.

proverb_native: Barne Sorg varer stakket
proverb_en: A child’s sorrow is short-lived.

proverb_native: Bær Asenet en Guldsæk, det æder dog Tidsel
proverb_en: Though the ass may carry a sack of gold, it nevertheless feeds on thistles.

proverb_native: Beder Gud dig drage, han faaer dig vel Reb; beder han dig ride, han faaer dig vel Hest
proverb_en: If God bids thee draw, he will find thee a rope; if he bids thee ride, he will find thee a horse.

proverb_native: Bedre Ægget i Dag end Hönen i Morgen
proverb_en: Rather the egg to-day than the hen to-morrow.

proverb_native: Bedre at Barn græder end at Moder sukker
proverb_en: Better the child cry, than the mother sigh.

proverb_native: Bedre at blæse hart end at brænde sig
proverb_en: Better blow hard than burn yourself.

proverb_native: Bedre at gaae en liden Krog end at væde sin Brog
proverb_en: Better make a short circuit than to wet your hose.

proverb_native: Bedre at komme seent til Kirke end aldrig
proverb_en: Better come late to church than never.

proverb_native: Bedre at spare paa Bredden, end paa Bunden
proverb_en: Better spare at the brim than at the bottom.

proverb_native: Bedre at være fri Fugl end fangen Konge
proverb_en: Better to be a free bird than a captive king.

proverb_native: Bedre brænder den vaade Green end den törre Steen
proverb_en: The wet branch burns better than the dry stone.

proverb_native: Bedre een Fugl i Haanden end to paa Taget
proverb_en: One bird in the hand is better than two on the roof.

proverb_native: Bedre en salt Sild over sit eget Bord, end en fersk Giedde over et fremmed
proverb_en: Better a salt herring on your own table, than a fresh pike on another man’s.

proverb_native: Bedre er aaben Fjende end Hykle-Ven
proverb_en: Better an open enemy than a false friend.

proverb_native: Bedre er at Barn græder, end gammel Mand
proverb_en: Better the child cry than the old man.

proverb_native: Bedre er at gaae fri i en grön Eng, end at være bunden til en Tornebusk
proverb_en: Better walk unshackled in a green meadow, than be bound to a thorn-bush.

proverb_native: Bedre er at lide for Sandhed, end at Lönnes for Lögn
proverb_en: Better suffer for truth, than prosper by falsehood.

proverb_native: Bedre er at stemme Bækken end Aaen
proverb_en: It is easier to stem the brook than the river.

proverb_native: Bedre er at tinge ved Busken end ved Boien
proverb_en: It is better to make conditions in the bush than in prison.

proverb_native: Bedre er at Verden veed du er en Synder end at Gud veed du er en Hykler
proverb_en: Better the world should know you as a sinner than God know you as a hypocrite.

proverb_native: Bedre er Bröd end Fuglesang
proverb_en: Bread is better than the song of birds.

proverb_native: Bedre er den Green der böier, end den der brister
proverb_en: Better is the branch that bends, than the branch that breaks.

proverb_native: Bedre er dyrt at kiöbe, end ilde at svelte
proverb_en: It is better to buy dearly than to hunger direly.

proverb_native: Bedre er een Ko med Ro end syv med Uro
proverb_en: Better one cow in peace than seven in trouble.

proverb_native: Bedre er et magert Forliig, end en fed Trætte
proverb_en: A meagre compromise is better than a fat lawsuit.

proverb_native: Bedre er et Ord for, end ti Ord efter
proverb_en: One word beforehand is better than ten afterwards.

proverb_native: Bedre er grov Traad, end bart Laar
proverb_en: Better coarse cloth than naked thighs.

proverb_native: Bedre er heelt end med Guld bödet
proverb_en: Better whole than patched with gold.

proverb_native: Bedre er Held end hundrede Mark
proverb_en: Luck is better than a hundred marks.

proverb_native: Bedre er liden Nagle for Huus end slet ingen
proverb_en: A small bolt to the house is better than none at all.

proverb_native: Bedre er selv at have, end Söster at bede
proverb_en: Better to have something yourself, than to beg of your sister.

proverb_native: Bedre er snild Tunge, end kæmt Haar
proverb_en: A smooth tongue is better than smooth locks.

proverb_native: Bedre er strax at nægte, end længe at love
proverb_en: Better to deny at once, than to promise long.

proverb_native: Bedre er svang Hest end tom Grime. Bedre er halvt Bröd end Alt mist. Bedre er lidet Boskab end tomt Huus
proverb_en: Better a poor horse than an empty stall. Better half a loaf than none at all. Better a little furniture than an empty house.

proverb_native: Bedre er tiende Ord end tarvlös Tale
proverb_en: Speaking silence is better than senseless speech.

proverb_native: Bedre er tösser maalt, end eengang og galt
proverb_en: Better twice measured than once wrong.

proverb_native: Bedre er tyndt Öl end tom Tönde
proverb_en: Better weak beer than an empty cask.

proverb_native: Bedre er Venne-Napp, end Fiende-Klap
proverb_en: Better a friend’s bite than an enemy’s caress.

proverb_native: Bedre i gammel Vogn end i nyt Skib
proverb_en: Better in an old carriage than in a new ship.

proverb_native: Bedre ingen Lov, end uden Fremgang
proverb_en: Better no law, than law not enforced.

proverb_native: Bedre lidt med Ro og Rette, end meget med Uro og Trætte
proverb_en: Better a little in peace and with right, than much with anxiety and strife.

proverb_native: Bedre noget paa Armen end alt i Tarmen
proverb_en: Better something on the arm than all in the stomach.

proverb_native: Bedre paa Træbeen at gaae, end paa Baar at bæres
proverb_en: Better walk on wooden legs, than be carried on a wooden bier.

proverb_native: Bedre sildig end aldrig
proverb_en: Better late than never.

proverb_native: Bedst brænder Ild paa egen Arne
proverb_en: The fire burns brightest on one’s own hearth.

proverb_native: Bid byder anden
proverb_en: One bite brings another.

proverb_native: Blind Due finder ogsaa stundom Hvedekorn
proverb_en: A blind pigeon may sometimes find a grain of wheat.

proverb_native: Blodet er aldrig saa tyndt, at det jo er tykkere end Vand
proverb_en: Let the blood be ever so thin, it is always thicker than water.

proverb_native: Blomster er Frugtens Fæstepenge
proverb_en: Flowers are the pledges of fruit.

proverb_native: Bondens Fied giör Ageren fed
proverb_en: The foot of the farmer manures the field.

proverb_native: Bön er dyrest Kiöb
proverb_en: What is got by begging is dearly bought.

proverb_native: Börn er fattig Mands Rigdom
proverb_en: Children are the riches of the poor.

proverb_native: Börn er vis Sorg, men uvis Glæde
proverb_en: Children are certain sorrow, but uncertain joy.

proverb_native: Brændt Barn rædes gierne Ilden, og bidt Barn Hund
proverb_en: A burnt child fears the fire, and a bitten child fears a dog.

proverb_native: Bugen vil intet borge
proverb_en: The belly gives no credit.

proverb_native: Daare lader Raad gaae ind ad det ene Öre, og ud ad det andet
proverb_en: Advice to a fool goes in at one ear and out at the other.

proverb_native: Daarligt er at frygte det man ei kan flye
proverb_en: It is folly to fear what one cannot avoid.

proverb_native: Da begynde Svanerne at synge, naar Raagerne tie
proverb_en: When the rooks are silent the swans begin to sing.

proverb_native: Da er Ild god inde, naar Hunsegel hænger ude
proverb_en: The fire is welcome within, when icicles hang without.

proverb_native: Dagen er aldrig saa hellig at jo Gryden vil syde
proverb_en: The day is never so holy that the pot refuses to boil.

proverb_native: De ere ei alle Jægere, som blæse i Horn
proverb_en: All are not hunters that blow the horn.

proverb_native: De ere ikke alle gode Stegere som bær lange Knive
proverb_en: All are not good cooks who carry long knives.

proverb_native: De flye ei Alle, som vende Ryggen
proverb_en: It is not all who turn their backs that flee.

proverb_native: De gamle Stude har de stive Horn
proverb_en: Old oxen have stiff horns.

proverb_native: De Gamle til Raad—de Unge til Daad
proverb_en: The aged in council—the young in action.

proverb_native: Deilig er Frugten som Dyden er i Træet
proverb_en: As the virtue in the tree, such is the fruit.

proverb_native: Deilighed bær Medgiften i Ansigtet
proverb_en: Beauty carries its dower in its face.

proverb_native: De lumske Sviin æde Masken, de galne löbe uden om
proverb_en: The still swine eat the mash, the wild ones run past it.

proverb_native: Den Bænk er vel prydet (som) med gode Qvinder er sat
proverb_en: That bench is well adorned that is filled with virtuous women.

proverb_native: Den Dag kommer vel, at Koen haver sin Hale behov
proverb_en: The day is sure to come when the cow will want her tail.

proverb_native: Den der er slaaet til en Skilling bliver aldrig Daler
proverb_en: That which is stamped a penny will never be a pound.

proverb_native: Den der har Glastag paa sit eget Huus, maa ikke kaste Steen paa andres
proverb_en: He who has a glass roof on his own house, must not throw stones at others’.

proverb_native: Den der ikke sparer paa Skillingen, faaer aldrig Daleren
proverb_en: He that does not save pennies, will never have pounds.

proverb_native: Den der jager to Harer af een Busk, faaer sielden nogen af dem
proverb_en: He who hunts two hares from one bush, is not likely to catch either.

proverb_native: Den der tager en Enke med tre Börn, tager fire Tyve
proverb_en: He who marries a widow with three children, marries four thieves.

proverb_native: Den der venter paa död Mands Skoe, kommer længe til at gaae barfodet
proverb_en: He who waits for dead men’s shoes, may have to go long barefoot.

proverb_native: Den der vil have Hunden hængt, siger den bider Faar
proverb_en: He that wants to hang a dog, says that it bites the sheep.

proverb_native: Den Ene jager, den Anden æder Haren
proverb_en: One hunts the hare, and another eats it.

proverb_native: Den ene Ravn hugger ikke Öinene ud paa den Anden
proverb_en: One raven does not peck out another’s eyes.

proverb_native: Den ene Stodder lider ikke at den anden har to Poser
proverb_en: One beggar likes not that another has two wallets.

proverb_native: Den er en Giek sig selv roser, og en Gal sig selv vil laste
proverb_en: He is a fool that praises himself, and he a madman that speaks ill of himself.

proverb_native: Den er god at borge Byg som eier Havre
proverb_en: It is safe to lend barley to him who has oats.

proverb_native: Den er ilde skikket til Bager, der har et Hoved af Smör
proverb_en: He is little suited to be a baker, whose head is made of butter.

proverb_native: Den er ingen nyttig, som ei er sig selv nyttig
proverb_en: He who is of no use to himself, is of no use to any one.

proverb_native: Den er ingensteds som allesteds vil være
proverb_en: He who would be everywhere will be nowhere.

proverb_native: Den er let at lokke, som efter vil hoppe
proverb_en: He is easy to lure, who is ready to follow.

proverb_native: Den er Mand, som giör Mands Gierning
proverb_en: He is a man, who acts like a man.

proverb_native: Den er næst Gud som mindst behöver
proverb_en: He is nearest to God who has the fewest wants.

proverb_native: Den er Tingen næst, som har den i Hænder
proverb_en: He is nearest a thing, who has it in his hands.

proverb_native: Den er ung nok som er sund, og rig nok som er uden Gield
proverb_en: He is young enough who has health, and he is rich enough who has no debts.

proverb_native: Den er værd det Söde som har smagt det Suure
proverb_en: He is worthy of sweets, who has tasted bitters.

proverb_native: Den Fattige fattes meget, men den Gierrige alt
proverb_en: The poor man wants much, the miser everything.

proverb_native: Den fede So veed ei hvad den sultne lider
proverb_en: The fat sow knows not what the hungry sow suffers.

proverb_native: Den förste Fugl fanger det förste Korn
proverb_en: The first bird gets the first grain.

proverb_native: Den Gaas kiækker höit, der har en god Gasse
proverb_en: The goose that has a good gander cackles loudly.

proverb_native: Den Gaas kiækker ikke mere som Hovedet er af
proverb_en: The goose that has lost its head no longer cackles.

proverb_native: Den Gamle skal man ære, den Unge skal man lære
proverb_en: Honour the old, teach the young.

proverb_native: Den giemmer til Kat, som giemmer til Nat
proverb_en: He who puts by for the night, puts by for the cat.

proverb_native: Den glæder sig meest, som selv qvæder
proverb_en: He that sings himself is the best pleased.

proverb_native: Den har svært Löb, som nödes til at löbe
proverb_en: He runs heavily who is forced to run.

proverb_native: Den Hund man skal nöde til Skovs, beder ikke mange Dyr
proverb_en: The dog that is forced into the woods will not hunt many deer.

proverb_native: Den kan snarest spilde, som har Karret i Haand
proverb_en: He is most likely to spill who holds the vessel in his hand.

proverb_native: Den Kloges Arv findes i alle Lande
proverb_en: A clever man’s inheritance is found in every country.

proverb_native: Den Korset haver signer sig selv först
proverb_en: He that bears the cross, blesses himself first.

proverb_native: Den lade Dreng og den varme Seng kunne ei vel skilles ad
proverb_en: A lazy boy and a warm bed are difficult to part.

proverb_native: Den Lade faaer ingen Lön uden Last
proverb_en: Blame is the lazy man’s wages.

proverb_native: Den leder ikke gierne bag Dören, som ei selv har staaet der
proverb_en: A man does not look behind the door unless he has stood there himself.

proverb_native: Den leer bedst som leer sidst
proverb_en: He who laughs last, laughs best.

proverb_native: Den lever ikke i al Verden, som kan flaae Huden af en Qvadsteen
proverb_en: He does not live in this world that can skin a grindstone.

proverb_native: Den Milde giver sig rig, den Gierrige tager sig fattig
proverb_en: The generous man grows rich in giving, the miser poor in taking.

proverb_native: Den Penge man sparer er saa god som den man avler
proverb_en: Money saved is as good as money gained.

proverb_native: Den Pung er tom anden Mands Penge ligge udi
proverb_en: That’s but an empty purse which is full of other men’s money.

proverb_native: Den Qværnsteen maler og, der under ligger
proverb_en: The millstone that lies undermost also helps to grind.

proverb_native: Den raader for Hesten, der rider ham
proverb_en: He who rides the horse is his master.

proverb_native: Den ræddes at komme i Sæk, som för har været i
proverb_en: He fears the sack who has been in it.

proverb_native: Den sanker god Rigdom der varer sig for Skade
proverb_en: He that keeps out of harm’s way will gather goodly riches.

proverb_native: Den sene Ko faaer det sure Græs
proverb_en: The laggard cow gets the sour grass.

proverb_native: Den sidder vel, som kan selv reise sig
proverb_en: He sits well who can rise without help.

proverb_native: Den skal ei være vaanden ad Svar, som giver andre onde Ord
proverb_en: He who abuses others must not be particular about the answer he gets.

proverb_native: Den skal endnu födes, der kan befalde alle
proverb_en: He is not yet born who can please everybody.

proverb_native: Den skal have en lang Skee, der vil söbe af Fad med Fanden
proverb_en: He needs a long spoon that would eat out of the same dish with the devil.

proverb_native: Den skal have meget Meel der vil stoppe alle Munde
proverb_en: He must have much meal who would stop all mouths.

proverb_native: Den skal have rene Fingre som en andens Næse vil snyde
proverb_en: He must have clean fingers who would blow another’s nose.

proverb_native: Den skal see ilde ud, som skal kyse Fanden
proverb_en: He must be ill-favoured who scares the devil.

proverb_native: Den skal sig selv love, der haver onde Grander
proverb_en: He who has bad neighbours is fain to praise himself.

proverb_native: Den skal staae paa et höit Bierg, der skal see sin Skiebne til Ende
proverb_en: He must stand high that would see the end of his own destiny.

proverb_native: Den skal staae tidlig op, der vil giöre alle tilpas
proverb_en: He had need rise betimes who would please everybody.

proverb_native: Den skal være en klog Vært, som vil tage Fanden i Herberge
proverb_en: He must be a clever host that would take the devil into his hostelry.

proverb_native: Den som ei vil lyde Fader, faaer vel at lyde Stivfader
proverb_en: He who will not obey father, will have to obey stepfather.

proverb_native: Den som er bidt af en Snog er bange for en Aal
proverb_en: He who has been bitten by a snake is afraid of an eel.

proverb_native: Den som Hönen föder, bör at have Ægget
proverb_en: He who feeds the hen ought to have the egg.

proverb_native: Den som kaster sig selv under Bænken, den lader de andre nok ligge
proverb_en: He who throws himself under the bench will be left to lie there.

proverb_native: Den som siger hvad han vil, maa höre hvad han ikke vil
proverb_en: He who says what he likes, must hear what he does not like.

proverb_native: Den som skal æde af en Andens Haand, bliver sielden mæt
proverb_en: He who is fed by another’s hand seldom gets enough.

proverb_native: Den som vel kan tigge, kan lade sine Penge ligge
proverb_en: He who knows how to beg may leave his money at home.

proverb_native: Den som vil gienne en anden over tre Gierder, maa selv over de to
proverb_en: He who would drive another over three dikes must climb over two himself.

proverb_native: Den Steen bliver eengang vaad, som Hvermand spytter paa
proverb_en: The stone that everybody spits upon will be wet at last.

proverb_native: Den Steen der ofte flyttes, bliver ikke mossegroet
proverb_en: The oft moved stone gathers no moss.

proverb_native: Den sviges værst, som sviger sig selv
proverb_en: He is most cheated who cheats himself.

proverb_native: Den taber ei i Trætte, som tvinger sin Tunge
proverb_en: He loses least in a quarrel who keeps his tongue in check.

proverb_native: Den veed bedst hvor Skoen trykker, som har den paa
proverb_en: He knows best where the shoe pinches who wears it.

proverb_native: Den Vei er ond at kiende, som Skibet löber i Havet
proverb_en: It is hard to track the path the ship follows in the ocean.

proverb_native: Der ere tre onde Naboer: store Floder, store Herrer, og Alfarvei
proverb_en: There are three bad neighbours: great rivers, great lords, and great roads.

proverb_native: Der er haardt i Stivmoders Haand
proverb_en: A stepmother has a hard hand.

proverb_native: Der er ikke saa liden Fisk, han stunder jo til at blive en Hval
proverb_en: Every little fish expects to become a whale.

proverb_native: Der er ingen Ild som jo haver nogen Smög
proverb_en: There is no fire without smoke.

proverb_native: Der er ingen Lægedom mod Bagvadskers Bid
proverb_en: There is no cure against a slanderer’s bite.

proverb_native: Der er mange Dage i Aaret, og end flere Maaltider
proverb_en: There are many days in the year, and still more meals.

proverb_native: Der er Raad mod Alt, uden mod Döden
proverb_en: There is help for everything, except death.

proverb_native: Der gaae mange Ord i en Sæk
proverb_en: It takes many words to fill a sack.

proverb_native: Der hörer Lykke til at fange Harer med Tromme
proverb_en: You must have good luck to catch hares with a drum.

proverb_native: Der hörer mere til Dands end et Par Kork Skoe
proverb_en: A pair of light shoes is not all that is needed for dancing.

proverb_native: Der hörer Styrke til at drage om Reb med sin Overmand
proverb_en: You must be strong to pull a rope against a stronger.

proverb_native: Der hvor Forstanden slipper, tager Lykken ved
proverb_en: When wisdom fails, luck helps.

proverb_native: Der hvor Lov slipper bör Hæder at möde
proverb_en: Where law lacks, honour should eke it out.

proverb_native: Der kommer ingen Klogskab ud, hvor ingen er inde
proverb_en: Where there is no wit within no wit will come out.

proverb_native: Der kommer ofte Ild i Spotterens Huus
proverb_en: The scoffer’s own house is often on fire.

proverb_native: Der löber meget Vand i Dammen, medens Mölleren sover
proverb_en: Much water runs by while the miller sleeps.

proverb_native: Der skal ædes, vare end alle Træer Galger
proverb_en: People must eat, even were every tree a gallows.

proverb_native: Der skal en höi Muur til at holde Frygten ude
proverb_en: It needs a high wall to keep out fear.

proverb_native: Der skal være mange Muus om at bide en Kat
proverb_en: It takes a good many mice to kill a cat.

proverb_native: Dersom Hönen ikke kaglede, vidste man ikke hvad hun havde giort
proverb_en: If the hen did not cackle no one would know what she had been about.

proverb_native: Dersom Skiægget gialdt, kunde Giedebukken præke
proverb_en: If the beard were all, the goat might preach.

proverb_native: Der vil let Hu til tung Skiæbne
proverb_en: It needs a light spirit to bear a heavy fate.

proverb_native: Der vil Salt til at drysse Sandhed med, om hun ellers skal ned
proverb_en: Truth must be seasoned to make it palatable.

proverb_native: Der vil skarp Lud til skurvet Hoved
proverb_en: A scald head needs strong lye.

proverb_native: Desmere man rörer ved Skarn desværre lugter det
proverb_en: The more you stir filth the worse it stinks.

proverb_native: De sove ikke alle der snive og snarke
proverb_en: All who snore are not asleep.

proverb_native: Det Barn der faaer Stivmoder faaer ogsaa Stivfader
proverb_en: The child who gets a stepmother also gets a stepfather.

proverb_native: Det bedste Giödsel sidder under Bondens Sko
proverb_en: The best manure is under the farmer’s shoe.

proverb_native: Det bliver vel Dag om end Hanen ei galer
proverb_en: Daylight will come, though the cock do not crow.

proverb_native: Det den Ædru har i Hiertet, har den Drukne i Munden
proverb_en: What the sober man has in his heart, the drunken man has on his lips.

proverb_native: Det dig ikke brænder, behöver du ei at blæse paa
proverb_en: There is no need to blow what does not burn you.

proverb_native: Det duer ikke for Svanen, at lære Örneunger at synge
proverb_en: It is not for the swan to teach eaglets to sing.

proverb_native: Det duer til intet at gjöre Skoe til Gæs
proverb_en: It is of no use making shoes for geese.

proverb_native: Det ene Sværd holder det andet i Skeden
proverb_en: One sword keeps another in the scabbard.

proverb_native: Det er bedre at möde vred Mand end fastende
proverb_en: Better cross an angry man than a fasting man.

proverb_native: Det er bedre at see ved Ord end ved Hugg
proverb_en: Better be convinced by words than by blows.

proverb_native: Det er bedre at spörge to Gange, end at fare vild en Gang
proverb_en: Better ask twice than lose your way once.

proverb_native: Det er daarligt at drukne paa tört Land
proverb_en: It is folly to drown on dry land.

proverb_native: Det er daarligt at synge to Viser for den Döve
proverb_en: It is folly to sing twice to a deaf man.

proverb_native: Det er daarligt at tage Tornen af en Andens Fod og sætte den i sin egen
proverb_en: It is folly to take a thorn out of another’s foot and put it into your own.

proverb_native: Det er dit som ingen kan tage fra dig
proverb_en: You may call that your own which no one can take from you.

proverb_native: Det er dyrt Smör som slikkes af en Hegle
proverb_en: It is dearbought butter that is licked off a woolcomb.

proverb_native: Det er ei alt Guld som glimrer
proverb_en: It is not all gold that glitters.

proverb_native: Det er ei Alt i Mave godt (som) i Mund södt er
proverb_en: What is sweet in the mouth is not always good in the stomach.

proverb_native: Det er ei Börneværk naar Kiærling dandser
proverb_en: It is no child’s play when an old woman dances.

proverb_native: Det er ei for Koens Bedste, at hun kommer op at age
proverb_en: It is not for the good of the cow when she is driven in a carriage.

proverb_native: Det er ei hvert Slags Træ som duer til Træskoe
proverb_en: Not every wood will make wooden shoes.

proverb_native: Det er ei hvert Sviin Kragen vil ride
proverb_en: It is not every hog that the crow will ride.

proverb_native: Det er en arm Steg, som intet drypper af
proverb_en: It is a poor roast that gives no dripping.

proverb_native: Det er en dristig Muus der giör Rede i Kattens Öre
proverb_en: It is a bold mouse that makes her nest in the cat’s ear.

proverb_native: Det er en fattig Roes, man skal laane af Forfædre
proverb_en: Praise borrowed from ancestors is but very sorry praise.

proverb_native: Det er en lad Fugl, der ei gider bygge sin egen Rede
proverb_en: It is a lazy bird that will not build its own nest.

proverb_native: Det er en ond Brönd som man skal bære Vand udi
proverb_en: It is a bad well that needs water to be carried to it.

proverb_native: Det er en ond Haand, der ei vil sit Hoved værge
proverb_en: It is a bad hand that refuses to guard the head.

proverb_native: Det er en ond Hest som ikke er Havren værd
proverb_en: It is a poor horse that is not worth its oats.

proverb_native: Det er en ond Hielp, at hielpe een fra Dynen i Halmen
proverb_en: That is poor help that helps you from the feather-bed to the straw.

proverb_native: Det er en ond Skade som kommer ingen til Gavn
proverb_en: It is an ill turn that does no good to any one.

proverb_native: Det er en ond Smed der ræddes for Gnister
proverb_en: He is a poor Smith who is afraid of sparks.

proverb_native: Det er en ringe Aarsag, hvorfor Ulven æder Faaret
proverb_en: It needs but slight provocation to make the wolf devour the lamb.

proverb_native: Det er en slem Höne, der giör Æg uden Gaarde
proverb_en: It is a bad hen that lays her eggs away from the farm.

proverb_native: Det er et klogt Barn der kiender sin egen Fader
proverb_en: It is a wise child that knows its own father.

proverb_native: Det er farligt at æde Kirsebær med store Herrer, de kaste een Stenene i Hovedet
proverb_en: It is dangerous to eat cherries with the great, they throw the stones at your head.

proverb_native: Det er for sildigt at raabe Kat, naar Flæsket er ædt
proverb_en: It is too late to cry cat, when the bacon is eaten.

proverb_native: Det er for sildigt at skyde Brönden igien naar Barnet er druknet
proverb_en: It is too late to cover the well when the child is drowned.

proverb_native: Det er for sildigt at slaae Vand paa Asken, naar Huset er brændt
proverb_en: It is too late to throw water on the cinders when the house is burnt down.

proverb_native: “Det er godt at gjöre med god Rede,” sagde Fanden, han redede sin Moders Haar med en Möghakke
proverb_en: ”It is easy to work with a good comb,” said the devil, when he combed his mother’s hair with a pitchfork.

proverb_native: Det er godt at laane Gud og Ageren, de give gode Renter
proverb_en: It is good to lend to God and to the soil—they pay good interest.

proverb_native: Det er godt at raade naar Lykken er med
proverb_en: It is easy to manage when fortune favours.

proverb_native: Det er godt at sove paa heelt Skind
proverb_en: It is good to sleep on a whole skin.

proverb_native: Det er godt at stange under anden Mands Blus
proverb_en: It is easy to poke another man’s fire.

proverb_native: Det er godt at svömme, naar en anden holder Hovedet op
proverb_en: It is easy to swim, when another holds up your head.

proverb_native: Det er godt at være kostfri af en andens Pung
proverb_en: It is easy to be generous out of another man’s purse.

proverb_native: Det er godt at være Præst om Paaske, Barn om Faste, Bonde om Juul, Föl om Hösten
proverb_en: It is good to be priest at Easter, child in Lent, peasant at Christmas, and foal in harvest-time.

proverb_native: Det er haard Kost at æde sine egne Ord
proverb_en: It is bitter fare to eat one’s own words.

proverb_native: Det er ikke enhver som veed at tage den rette So ved Öret
proverb_en: It is not every one who takes the right sow by the ear.

proverb_native: Det er ikke for ingenting naar Fanden lægger sig i Gröften
proverb_en: It is not for nothing that the devil lays himself down in the ditch.

proverb_native: Det er ikke godt at kiende sit Smör i en andens Kaal
proverb_en: It is not easy to know your butter in another man’s cabbage.

proverb_native: Det er ikke godt at skride paa Fandens Iis
proverb_en: It is not easy to walk upon the devil’s ice.

proverb_native: Det er ikke godt at stinge Biörn med Skiev
proverb_en: It is not easy to sting a bear with a straw.

proverb_native: Det er ikke hver Mand der kan bære Hög paa Haand
proverb_en: It is not every man that can carry a falcon on his hand.

proverb_native: Det er ikke lang Vei til Vennehuus
proverb_en: To a friend’s house the road is never long.

proverb_native: Det er ilde for Hönen, naar Ægget vil lære hende at kagle
proverb_en: The hen is ill off when the egg teaches her how to cackle.

proverb_native: Det er ingen liden Skalk der kiender en stor
proverb_en: He is no small knave who knows a great one.

proverb_native: Det er Konst at lee, naar det gaaer een selv ilde
proverb_en: It is a great art to laugh at your own misfortunes.

proverb_native: Det er let at byde Fanden til Gjæst, men ondt at faae ham af Huset
proverb_en: It is easy to bid the devil be your guest, but difficult to get rid of him.

proverb_native: Det er let at krybe i Træ naar det er fældet
proverb_en: It is easy to stride a tree when it is down.

proverb_native: Det er let at lægge Riis til, naar en anden lægger Rumpen til
proverb_en: It is easy to find the rod when another finds the bottom.

proverb_native: Det er lige nær til Himmels, i hvor man döer
proverb_en: The road to heaven is equally short, where’er we die.

proverb_native: Det er ondt at faae mange Hoveder under een Hat
proverb_en: It is difficult to get many heads under one hat.

proverb_native: Det er ondt at gabe mod en Ovns Mund
proverb_en: It is useless to gape against an oven.

proverb_native: Det er ondt at giöre Ild paa kold Arne
proverb_en: It is hard to make a fire on a cold hearth.

proverb_native: Det er ondt at lære gammel Hund at kure
proverb_en: It is hard to teach an old dog tricks.

proverb_native: Det er ondt at lokke Höge med tomme Hænder
proverb_en: It is hard to lure hawks with empty hands.

proverb_native: Det er ondt at plukke Haar af den Skaldede
proverb_en: It is not easy to pluck hairs from a bald pate.

proverb_native: Det er ondt at sidde imellem to Ilde
proverb_en: It is bad to be between two fires.

proverb_native: Det er ondt at skiule det hver Mand veed
proverb_en: It is difficult to hide what everybody knows.

proverb_native: Det er ondt at spytte Honning for den som har Munden fuld af Galde
proverb_en: It is difficult to spit honey out of a mouth full of gall.

proverb_native: Det er ondt at vogte den Höne som borte vil værpe
proverb_en: It is not easy to guard the hen that lays her eggs abroad.

proverb_native: Det er ondt Faar der ei gider baaret sit eget Fæt
proverb_en: It is a bad sheep that is too lazy to carry its own fleece.

proverb_native: Det er ondt Jern, som ikke er Staal udi
proverb_en: It is bad iron in which there is no steel.

proverb_native: Det er saa godt at ligge nögen som intet at have paa sig
proverb_en: It is as well to be naked as to have no covering.

proverb_native: Det er saa ondt at spytte Ilden ud og skiende sig, som at synke den og brænde sig
proverb_en: It is as bad to spit out the fire and be shamed, as it is to swallow it and be burnt.

proverb_native: Det er Sæd at Börn giöre Börne-Gierning
proverb_en: From children you must expect childish acts.

proverb_native: Det er strengt at trælle med tom Tarm
proverb_en: It is hard to labour with an empty belly.

proverb_native: Det er ringe Tröst for Beenbrud, at en anden har brudt Halsen
proverb_en: It is poor comfort for one who has broken his leg, that another has broken his neck.

proverb_native: Det er Tid at tage Hatten af, naar man seer Manden
proverb_en: It is time enough to take off your hat when you see the man.

proverb_native: Det förste Raad af en Qvinde er det bedste
proverb_en: A woman’s first counsel is the best.

proverb_native: Det giöres ei Behov at blæse ad den Ild som selv brænder
proverb_en: There is no use in blowing a fire that burns well.

proverb_native: Det har blæst haardt—Skarnet er flöiet op i Höisædet
proverb_en: It has been blowing hard—the dirt has been blown into high places.

proverb_native: Det hjælper ei at dölge for Ven det Uven veed
proverb_en: It is no use hiding from a friend what is known to an enemy.

proverb_native: Det höieste Bierg at komme over er Dörtærskelen
proverb_en: The most difficult mountain to cross is the threshold.

proverb_native: Det kan snart skee, som længe maa angres
proverb_en: That may be soon done, which brings long repentance.

proverb_native: “Det kommer igien,” sagde Manden, han gav sin So Flæsk
proverb_en: ”It will come back,” said the man, when he gave his sow pork.

proverb_native: Det kommer op i Tö (som) man fjæler i Snö
proverb_en: Thaw reveals what has been hidden by snow.

proverb_native: Det kommer vel der skee skal
proverb_en: That which must be, will be.

proverb_native: Det Korn vorder ilde malet, som bæres paa en ond Qværn
proverb_en: The corn that is taken to a bad mill, will be badly ground.

proverb_native: Det man adlyster kommer ikke far snart
proverb_en: A pleasant thing never comes too soon.

proverb_native: Det man æder af Gryden, faaer man ikke paa Fadet
proverb_en: That which has been eaten out of the pot cannot be put into the dish.

proverb_native: Det man lærer med Skade, husker man længe
proverb_en: What you learn to your cost you remember long.

proverb_native: Det Ord som er talt, sletter man ikke ud med en Svamp
proverb_en: Words once spoken cannot be wiped out with a sponge.

proverb_native: Det qvemmer bedre at skrabe Osten end skrælde den
proverb_en: It is better to scrape the cheese than to peel it.

proverb_native: Det Saar man ei kan hele, skal man ei rive op
proverb_en: If you cannot heal the wound, do not tear it open.

proverb_native: Det skal aarle kröge, som god Krog skal vorde
proverb_en: The branch must be bent early that is to make a good crook.

proverb_native: Det skal være en haard Vinter, om den ene Ulv æder den anden
proverb_en: It must be a hard winter when one wolf devours another.

proverb_native: Det som er taugt kan siges; det som er sagt kan ei ties
proverb_en: That which is unsaid, may be spoken; that which is said, cannot be unsaid.

proverb_native: Det sömmer ikke Spurv at gaae i Tranedands
proverb_en: It does not become the sparrow to mix in the dance of the cranes.

proverb_native: Det spörger en Blind om, hvorfor den elskes som er smuk
proverb_en: It is only the blind who ask why they are loved who are fair.

proverb_native: Det staaer ikke til Hunde, naar Hors skal döe
proverb_en: It does not depend upon the dog when the horse shall die.

proverb_native: Det stille Vand har den dybe Grund
proverb_en: Still water runs deep.

proverb_native: Det Ung nemmer, Gammel ei glemmer
proverb_en: What youth learns, age does not forget.

proverb_native: De Unge skal man lære, de Gamle skal man ære
proverb_en: Young people must be taught, old ones be honoured.

proverb_native: Det var Lyst at age, skulde man ikke vælte
proverb_en: It is pleasant driving when there is no danger of upsetting.

proverb_native: Döden blæser ei Lyd for sig
proverb_en: Death does not blow a trumpet.

proverb_native: Dören lukkes haardt i Laas for sandför Mand
proverb_en: A truthteller finds the doors closed against him.

proverb_native: Dristig Mand har Lykken i sit Fölge
proverb_en: A bold man has luck in his train.

proverb_native: Drukken Mand kan snart komme op at dandse
proverb_en: A drunken man may soon be made to dance.

proverb_native: Drukken Mands Glæde er tidt ædru Mands Sorg
proverb_en: The drunken man’s joy is often the sober man’s sorrow.

proverb_native: Du har nok af Munden og lidt af Ulden, sagde Fanden, han klippede en So
proverb_en: Much noise and little wool, said the devil, when he was shearing the sow.

proverb_native: Duk dig, lad gaae over; Veiret vil have sin Villie
proverb_en: Stoop, and let it pass; the storm will have its way.

proverb_native: Dyden i Midten, sagde Fanden, han sad imellem to Procuratorer
proverb_en: Virtue in the middle, said the Devil, when seated between two lawyers.

proverb_native: Eed og Æg ere snart brudte
proverb_en: Eggs and oaths are easily broken.

proverb_native: Een er ikke ond fordi en anden er god
proverb_en: One man is not bad because another is good.

proverb_native: Een er skabt til Pengene, den Anden til Pungen
proverb_en: One man is born to the money, and another to the purse.

proverb_native: Een Fjende er for meget, og hundrede Venner ere ei nok
proverb_en: One enemy is too much, and a hundred friends are not enough.

proverb_native: Een Haand fuld af Penge, er stærkere end to Hænder fulde af Sandhed
proverb_en: One hand full of money is stronger than two hands full of truth.

proverb_native: Een imod een, og to imod Fanden
proverb_en: One to one, and two to the devil.

proverb_native: Een Nar giör flere
proverb_en: One fool makes many.

proverb_native: Een plöier, en Anden saaer, den Tredie veed ei hvo det faaer
proverb_en: One ploughs, another sows, who will reap no one knows.

proverb_native: Een Skalk skal man fange med en anden
proverb_en: Set a thief to catch a thief.

proverb_native: Een Skilling er bedre paa Land, end ti paa Havet
proverb_en: One penny is better on land than ten on the sea.

proverb_native: Een snakker tidt den anden af Bænken, og sætter sig selv op
proverb_en: One man often talks another off his bench, and seats himself upon it.

proverb_native: Een Svale giör ingen Sommer
proverb_en: One swallow don’t make a summer.

proverb_native: Eet godt Raad er bedre end en Pose fuld
proverb_en: One piece of good advice is better than a bag full.

proverb_native: Eet Haar efter andet, gjör Bonden skaldet
proverb_en: Hair by hair, and the head gets bald.

proverb_native: Eet Jomfru Haar drager stærkere end ti Par Öxen
proverb_en: One hair of a maiden’s head pulls harder than ten yoke of oxen.

proverb_native: Eet skabet Faar fordærver en heel Flok
proverb_en: One mangy sheep spoils a whole flock.

proverb_native: Efter en god Avler kommer en god Oder
proverb_en: After one that earns comes one that wastes.

proverb_native: Efter god Mad og from Hustru töver man ei for længe
proverb_en: For a good dinner and a gentle wife you can afford to wait.

proverb_native: Efter söd Klöe kommer suur Svie
proverb_en: After pleasant scratching comes unpleasant smarting.

proverb_native: Egen Arne er Guld værd
proverb_en: A hearth of your own is worth gold.

proverb_native: Egen Arne koger bedst
proverb_en: The pot boils best on your own hearth.

proverb_native: Elsk mig lidt og elsk mig længe
proverb_en: Love me little and love me long.

proverb_native: En Ambolt er ikke ræd for en god Forhammer
proverb_en: The anvil does not fear a good sledge-hammer.

proverb_native: En andens Hest og egen Svöbe, kan meget taale
proverb_en: Another man’s horse and your own whip can do a great deal.

proverb_native: En Bonde bliver Bonde, sov han end paa Silkebolster
proverb_en: A boor remains a boor, though he sleep on silken bolsters.

proverb_native: En döv Hörer giör en galen Svarer
proverb_en: A deaf auditor makes a crazy answerer.

proverb_native: En döv Sax giör en skievmundet Skrædder
proverb_en: Dull scissors make crooked-mouthed tailors.

proverb_native: End vinder folsk Mand förste Leg
proverb_en: A fool only wins the first game.

proverb_native: Ene i Raad, ene i Sorg
proverb_en: Alone in counsel, alone in sorrow.

proverb_native: En Fisk og en Gjæst lugter ilde den tredie Dag
proverb_en: Fish and guests smell at three days old.

proverb_native: En Gaas drikker saa meget som en Gasse
proverb_en: A goose drinks as much as a gander.

proverb_native: En god Dag skal man rose om Aftenen
proverb_en: Praise a fair day in the evening.

proverb_native: En grön Juul giver en fed Kirkegaard
proverb_en: A green Christmas makes a fat churchyard.

proverb_native: Enhver bær sin Fjende i egen Barm
proverb_en: Every man carries an enemy in his own bosom.

proverb_native: Enhver Dag har sin Aften
proverb_en: Every day has its evening.

proverb_native: Enhver er sin Lykkes Smed
proverb_en: Every man is the architect of his own fortune.

proverb_native: Enhver faaer at bære sin egen Sæk til Mölle
proverb_en: Let every man carry his own sack to the mill.

proverb_native: Enhver Fugl har sine Fieder behov
proverb_en: Every bird needs its own feathers.

proverb_native: Enhver Fugl synger af sit Næb
proverb_en: Let every bird sing its own note.

proverb_native: Enhver mener hans Kobber er Guld
proverb_en: Every man thinks his copper is gold.

proverb_native: Enhver Tosse mener, han er klog nok
proverb_en: Every fool thinks he is clever enough.

proverb_native: Enhver vil gjerne over Gierdet hvor det er lavest
proverb_en: Every one tries to cross the fence where it is lowest.

proverb_native: Enhver vil rage Ild ad sin Gryde
proverb_en: Every one rakes the fire under his own pot.

proverb_native: Enhver Vind er læk Skib imod
proverb_en: Every wind is against a leaky ship.

proverb_native: En liden Ild at varme sig paa, er bedre end en stor at brænde sig paa
proverb_en: A small fire that warms you, is better than a large one that burns you.

proverb_native: En Nar kan spörge meer end ti Vise kunne svare til
proverb_en: One fool may ask more questions than ten wise men can answer.

proverb_native: En ond Hest æder saa meget som en god
proverb_en: A bad horse eats as much as a good one.

proverb_native: En ond Mund skal lave sig paa en stærk Ryg
proverb_en: A foul mouth must be provided with a strong back.

proverb_native: En Skalk kan vel skiules under en Messesærk
proverb_en: There’s many a knave concealed under a surplice.

proverb_native: En Skilling er i Tide saa god som en Daler
proverb_en: A penny in time is as good as a dollar.

proverb_native: En So kan saa vel finde et Agern, som en Galt
proverb_en: A sow may find an acorn as well as a hog.

proverb_native: En Spurv har saa ondt af at bryde sit Laar, som en Friis Hest
proverb_en: A sparrow suffers as much when it breaks its leg as does a Flanders horse.

proverb_native: En villig Hielper töver ei til man beder
proverb_en: A willing helper does not wait until he is asked.

proverb_native: Er Fuglen af Haanden er den ond at faae tilbage
proverb_en: The bird once out of hand is hard to recover.

proverb_native: Et andet Aar kommer der en anden Juleaften
proverb_en: Another year will bring another Christmas.

proverb_native: Ethvert Kar maa staa paa sin egen Bund
proverb_en: Every tub must stand on its own bottom.

proverb_native: Et lidet Muln kan skiule baade Sol og Maane
proverb_en: A small cloud may hide both sun and moon.

proverb_native: Et skabet Faar fordærver den hele Hjord
proverb_en: One scabbed sheep will mar a whole flock.

proverb_native: Faaer man ei Fuglen, da faaer man vel en Fieder deraf
proverb_en: If you cannot get the bird, get one of its feathers.

proverb_native: Faa have Lykken, alle have Döden
proverb_en: Few have luck, all have death.

proverb_native: Faa Qvinder graaner for Mandens Död
proverb_en: Few women turn grey because their husband dies.

proverb_native: Faar som bræge meest, malke mindst
proverb_en: The sheep that bleat the most give the least milk.

proverb_native: Fader og Moder ere gode, end er Gud bedre
proverb_en: Father and mother are kind, but God is kinder.

proverb_native: Fagerhed uden Tugt—Rose uden Lugt
proverb_en: Beauty without virtue is like a rose without scent.

proverb_native: Fagert Ansigt faaer Lov nok, dog man selv tier stille
proverb_en: A fair face will get its praise, though the owner keep silent.

proverb_native: Fagre Börn qvæde fagre Viser
proverb_en: Pretty children sing pretty songs.

proverb_native: Fagre Ord fryde en Daare, og stundom fuldvis en Mand
proverb_en: Fair words please the fool, and sometimes the wise.

proverb_native: Fagre Ord uden Fynd naae ikke langt
proverb_en: Fine words without deeds go not far.

proverb_native: Falder Himlen ned, da briste mange Leergryder
proverb_en: Should the heavens fall, many pipkins will be broken.

proverb_native: Falskhed ligger ofte under fagert Haar
proverb_en: Falseness often lurks beneath fair hair.

proverb_native: Fattige Frænder faae liden Hæder
proverb_en: Poor relations have little honour.

proverb_native: Fattig Mand har faa Kyndinger
proverb_en: A poor man has few acquaintances.

proverb_native: Fattig Mands Hovmod varer stakket
proverb_en: The pride of the poor does not endure.

proverb_native: Fattig Mands Kæde haver mange Ulæde
proverb_en: A poor man’s joy has much alloy.

proverb_native: Fattig Mands Korn voxer altid tyndt
proverb_en: The poor man’s corn always grows thin.

proverb_native: Fattig Mand söger om Maden, den Rige om Lyst til at æde den
proverb_en: The poor man seeks for food, the rich man for appetite.

proverb_native: Far som de Fleste, saa spotte dig de Færreste
proverb_en: Do as others do, and few will mock you.

proverb_native: Flere Folk dræbes af Nadver end af Sværd
proverb_en: More people are slain by suppers than by the sword.

proverb_native: Flykket hænger ikke saa höit, at jo Hunden agter sig Benet
proverb_en: The flitch hangs never so high but a dog will look out for the bone.

proverb_native: Flyver end Fuglen over dit Hoved, saa lad den ei bygge Bo i dit Haar
proverb_en: Though the bird may fly over your head, let it not make its nest in your hair.

proverb_native: Föl bliver ikke altid Faderen liig
proverb_en: Every foal is not like its sire.

proverb_native: Folsk Vise vorder mangelunde qvædet
proverb_en: A silly song may be sung in many ways.

proverb_native: Fordi er Verden vid, at hver haver sin Id
proverb_en: Every man has his lot, and a wide world before him.

proverb_native: För een lærer at hænge er han halv död
proverb_en: Before a man learns to hang he is half dead.

proverb_native: Forgieves er at mede uden Krog, at lære uden Bog
proverb_en: It is vain to fish without a hook, or learn to read without a book.

proverb_native: For Konst skal man Mesteren ære
proverb_en: The master derives honour from his art.

proverb_native: Forliigt Venskab er som ilde lægt Saar
proverb_en: Reconciled friendship is like a badly healed wound.

proverb_native: For meget og for lidt fordærver alting
proverb_en: Too little and too much spoils everything.

proverb_native: For Vilkaar og Vedtægt maa Loven vige
proverb_en: To circumstances and custom the law must yield.

proverb_native: “Frænde er Usle værst,” sagde Ræven, han saae de röde Hunde
proverb_en: ”Own kin are the worst friends,” said the fox, when he saw the foxy dogs after him.

proverb_native: Freden föder, Krigen öder; Freden nærer, Krigen tærer
proverb_en: Peace feeds, war wastes; peace breeds, war consumes.

proverb_native: Fred og velbygget Huus kjöbes ei for dyrt
proverb_en: Peace and a well-built house cannot be bought too dearly.

proverb_native: Fred skal man dyrt kjöbe
proverb_en: Peace must be bought even at a high price.

proverb_native: Fremmed Mad smager altid bedst
proverb_en: Strangers’ meat is the greatest treat.

proverb_native: Frisk vovet er halvt vundet
proverb_en: A bold attempt is half success.

proverb_native: “Fruer har Fruenoder,” sagde Gal’ Ane, hun slæbte sin Kaabe i Rendesteenen
proverb_en: ”Ladies have ladies’ whims,” said crazy Ann, when she draggled her cloak in the gutter.

proverb_native: Fuglen flyver aldrig saa höit, han söger jo sit Föde paa Jorden
proverb_en: However high a bird may soar, it seeks its food on earth.

proverb_native: Fuldt Kar skal man varligen bære
proverb_en: A full vessel must be carried carefully.

proverb_native: “Fy dig an, saa sort du er!” sagde Gryden til Leerpotten
proverb_en: ”Fie upon thee, how black thou art!” said the kettle to the saucepan.

proverb_native: Fyrm og Faste læger mangen Sot
proverb_en: Abstinence and fasting cure many a complaint.

proverb_native: Gaaer Lykkens Vogn vel, da hænge Skam og Avind ved Hiulene
proverb_en: When fortune’s chariot rolls easily, envy and shame cling to the wheels.

proverb_native: Gaasen gaaer saa længe i Stegerset, til hun fastner ved Spedet
proverb_en: The goose goes so often into the kitchen, till at last she sticks to the spit.

proverb_native: “Gak med,” og “see til,” ere to gode Tyende i Bondens Gaard
proverb_en: ”I’ll go myself,” and “I’ll see to it,” are two good servants on a farm.

proverb_native: Galne Hunde faae revet Skind
proverb_en: Mad dogs get their coats torn.

proverb_native: Gamle Fugle fanger man ikke med Avner
proverb_en: Old birds are not caught with chaff.

proverb_native: Gamle Mærker daare ikke
proverb_en: Old signs do not deceive.

proverb_native: Gamle Sviin har haarde Tryner, gamle Stude haarde Horn
proverb_en: Old swine have hard snouts, old oxen hard horns.

proverb_native: Gammel Giek er værre end ung Daare
proverb_en: An old fool is worse than a young simpleton.

proverb_native: Gammel Green bryder naar den skal böies
proverb_en: The old branch breaks when it is bent.

proverb_native: Gammel Mands Sagn er sielden usand
proverb_en: An old man’s sayings are seldom untrue.

proverb_native: Gammel Ulv ræddes ei ved höie Raab
proverb_en: An old wolf is not scared by loud cries.

proverb_native: Gavnet er bedre end Navnet
proverb_en: Profit is better than fame.

proverb_native: Giedden bliver stor af de smaae Fisk
proverb_en: The pike grows big on small fry.

proverb_native: Giem din Mund, og giem din Ven
proverb_en: Keep your mouth, and keep your friend.

proverb_native: Gien ei af dig det, der ei löber paa dig
proverb_en: Drive not away what never came near you.

proverb_native: Giensti vorder ofte Glapsti
proverb_en: A short cut is often a wrong cut.

proverb_native: Gierrig Mands Taske bliver aldrig fuld
proverb_en: The miser’s bag is never full.

proverb_native: Gierrig Qværn maler allehaande Korn
proverb_en: A greedy mill grinds all kinds of corn.

proverb_native: Giev Mand giör sig ikke til Hund for et Beens Skyld
proverb_en: An honest man does not make himself a dog for the sake of a bone.

proverb_native: Gift din Sön naar du vil, din Datter naar du kan
proverb_en: Marry your son when you will, your daughter when you can.

proverb_native: Giör ærligen, og svar diærveligen
proverb_en: Act honestly, and answer boldly.

proverb_native: Giör saa i Dalen, at du frygter ei hvo staaer paa Valden
proverb_en: Act so in the valley, that you need not fear those who stand on the hill.

proverb_native: Giör vel imod en Skalk, og bed til Gud, han lönner dig ikke
proverb_en: Do good to a knave, and pray God he may not do the same to thee.

proverb_native: Giort Gierning staaer ikke til at vende
proverb_en: What is done cannot be undone.

proverb_native: Giver Gud ei Skiepper fulde, saa giver han Skeer fulde
proverb_en: If God give not bushelfuls, he gives spoonfuls.

proverb_native: Giv Griis naar han grynter, og Barn naar det græder, saa faaer Du fager Griis og fuult Barn
proverb_en: Give a pig when it grunts, and a child when it cries, and you will have a fine pig, and a bad child.

proverb_native: Giv Skalken et Spand, han tager vel heel Alen
proverb_en: Give a rogue an inch, and he will take an ell.

proverb_native: Glad Aftensang giör ofte sorrigfuld Ottesang
proverb_en: A joyous evening often leads to a sorrowful morning.

proverb_native: Glæde er som Koldesygen; een god Dag imellem to onde
proverb_en: Joy is like the ague; one good day between two bad ones.

proverb_native: Gode Ord læge Venne-Saar
proverb_en: Kind words heal friendship’s wounds.

proverb_native: Gode Raad rodne ikke, naar de komme törre i Huus
proverb_en: Good counsel will not rot, if it be got in dry.

proverb_native: God Konge er bedre end gammel Lov
proverb_en: A good king is better than an old law.

proverb_native: God Nabo er bedre end Broder i anden By
proverb_en: A good neighbour is better than a brother far off.

proverb_native: God Styrmand kiendes ei naar Havet er stille og Veiret til Villie
proverb_en: A good pilot is not known when the sea is calm and the weather fair.

proverb_native: Godt er at have reent Bröd i sin Pose
proverb_en: It is well to have clean bread in one’s wallet.

proverb_native: Godt er at hvile paa giort Gierning
proverb_en: Rest is good after the work is done.

proverb_native: Godt er at sidde ved Styret i stille Veir
proverb_en: It is easy to sit at the helm in fine weather.

proverb_native: Godt Haandværk har en gylden Grund
proverb_en: A good handicraft has a golden foundation.

proverb_native: Godt kommer aldrig for tidt
proverb_en: Good never comes too often.

proverb_native: Gold So var aldrig Griise god
proverb_en: A barren sow is never kind to pigs.

proverb_native: Graa Haar ere Dödens Blomster
proverb_en: Grey hairs are death’s blossoms.

proverb_native: Gud bedre Faarene naar Ulven er Dommer
proverb_en: God help the sheep when the wolf is judge.

proverb_native: Gud giver alle Mad som han giver Mund
proverb_en: God never sends mouths but he sends meat.

proverb_native: Gud giver hver Fugl sin Föde, men kaster den ei i Reden til ham
proverb_en: God gives every bird its food, but does not throw it into the nest.

proverb_native: Gud giver hver saa Kuld, som Klæder
proverb_en: God sends men cold according to their clothes.

proverb_native: Gud giver smaa Folk smaa Gaver
proverb_en: God gives little folks small gifts.

proverb_native: Gud giver Villien, Nöden giver Tvang
proverb_en: God gives the will, necessity gives the law.

proverb_native: Gud kommer tilsidst, naar vi troe han er længst borte
proverb_en: God comes at last, when we think he is farthest off.

proverb_native: Guds Raadkammer har ingen Nögle
proverb_en: To God’s council-chamber there is no key.

proverb_native: Guld bliver vel Guld, om end det ligger i Skalkepung
proverb_en: Gold is gold, though it be in a rogue’s purse.

proverb_native: Guld Nögle lukker alle Döre op uden Himlens
proverb_en: A golden key opens every door except that of heaven.

proverb_native: Gunst og Gave kommer Retten til at rave
proverb_en: Favour and gifts disturb justice.

proverb_native: Haabe og vente er Giekkerente
proverb_en: Hope and expectation are a fool’s income.

proverb_native: Haabet er de Vaagendes Dröm
proverb_en: Hope is the dream of the waking.

proverb_native: Haabet er et Æg hvoraf Een faaer Blommen, en Anden Hviden, en Tredie Skallen
proverb_en: Hope is an egg of which one man gets the yolk, another the white, and a third the shell.

proverb_native: Haand skal anden toe, eller baade urene være
proverb_en: One hand must wash the other, or both will be dirty.

proverb_native: Haardt Bidsel giör ikke Hesten bedre
proverb_en: A hard bit does not make the horse better.

proverb_native: Haardt er ufödt Hest at binde ved Krybbe
proverb_en: It is difficult to tie an unborn horse to the manger.

proverb_native: Halvt at rose er halvt at skielde
proverb_en: Faint praise is akin to abuse.

proverb_native: Han ager et godt Læs i Gaard, som en god Hustru faaer
proverb_en: He drives a good waggonful into his farm who gets a good wife.

proverb_native: Han ager ikke ilde som veed at vende
proverb_en: He is not a bad driver who knows how to turn.

proverb_native: Han banker dristig paa, som bær godt Budskab
proverb_en: He knocks boldly at the door who brings a welcome message.

proverb_native: Han dandser og, som dandser nödig
proverb_en: A man dances all the same, though he may dance against his will.

proverb_native: Han drukner ikke, der hænge skal, uden Vandet gaaer over Galgen
proverb_en: He who was born to be hanged will not be drowned, unless the water go over the gallows.

proverb_native: Han er ikke bedre der fjæler, end han der stjæler
proverb_en: He that hides is no better than he that steals.

proverb_native: Han er ikke bedre der holder end den der flaaer
proverb_en: He that holds is no better than he that scourges.

proverb_native: Han er ingensteds, som er allesteds
proverb_en: He who is everywhere, is nowhere.

proverb_native: Han föler bedst hvor Skoen trykker, som har den paa
proverb_en: He knows best where the shoe pinches who wears it.

proverb_native: Hæng den unge Tyv, saa stiæler den gamle ikke
proverb_en: Hang the young thief, and the old one will not steal.

proverb_native: Han kommer ei til Skov der ræddes for hver Busk
proverb_en: He will never get into the wood who starts at every bush.

proverb_native: Han kommer og frem, der ager med Stude
proverb_en: Even he gets on who is drawn by oxen.

proverb_native: Han kom tidlig nok, der blev hængt ved Lys
proverb_en: He came time enough who was hung by candlelight.

proverb_native: Han maae have Skaden, som har sögt den
proverb_en: He that courts injury will obtain it.

proverb_native: Han raader for Sækken, der sidder paa den
proverb_en: He has command of the sack who is seated on it.

proverb_native: Han skal have Fingre af Jern, som Fanden vil flaae
proverb_en: He must have iron fingers who would flay the devil.

proverb_native: Han skal have meget Smör, som skal stoppe hver Mands Mund
proverb_en: He must have plenty of butter, who would stop everybody’s mouth.

proverb_native: Han skal see grandt til, der Sanden skal sige
proverb_en: He must keep a sharp look-out who would speak the truth.

proverb_native: Han skal skrige höit, som vil skræmme Fanden
proverb_en: He must cry loud who would scare the devil.

proverb_native: Han skal være skiær som en anden vil skielde
proverb_en: He must be pure who would blame another.

proverb_native: Han skiender, som ei kan skade
proverb_en: He scolds most that can hurt the least.

proverb_native: Han veed bedst af Vandet, som vadet har
proverb_en: He knows the water best who has waded through it.

proverb_native: Har du lært at bie, kan du blive Dronning i Sverrig
proverb_en: If you have learnt to wait, you may be Queen of Sweden.

proverb_native: Har du sagt hvad du vil, skal du höre hvad du ikke vil
proverb_en: He that says what he should not, will hear what he would not.

proverb_native: Hævd giör god Hest og ikke höre Stalde
proverb_en: Care, and not fine stables, make a good horse.
