proverb_native: Havde nær ikke været saa havde Kiærlingen bidt en Ulv
proverb_en: Had it not been for an if, the old woman would have bitten a wolf.

proverb_native: Heiren straffer Vandet, fordi han ei kan svömme
proverb_en: The heron blames the water because he cannot swim.

proverb_native: Hellere seer jeg Rögen af min egen Skorsteen, end Ilden paa en andens
proverb_en: I would rather see smoke from my own chimney than the fire on another’s hearth.

proverb_native: Hemmelig Gave faaer aabenbar Lön
proverb_en: Secret gifts are openly rewarded.

proverb_native: Herrebön er Herrebud
proverb_en: Great men’s requests are commands.

proverb_native: Herrer og Narre have frit Sprog
proverb_en: Lords and fools speak freely.

proverb_native: Herre uden Land, er Fadet uden Viin
proverb_en: A lord without land, is like a cask without wine.

proverb_native: Hest skal gaae til Krybben, ikke Krybben til Hest
proverb_en: The horse must go to the manger, and not the manger to the horse.

proverb_native: Hielp dig selv da hielper dig Gud
proverb_en: Help yourself, and God will help you.

proverb_native: Hielp er god, saa nær som i Grödfadet
proverb_en: Help is good everywhere, except in the porridge-bowl.

proverb_native: Himlen er dog blaa, om end den Blinde ei seer det
proverb_en: The sky is not the less blue because the blind man does not see it.

proverb_native: Hoflevnet er ofte Helvedes Gienvei
proverb_en: Life at court is often a short cut to hell.

proverb_native: Hold dig til de Smaae, saa bide de Store dig ikke
proverb_en: Keep to the little ones, and the big ones will not bite you.

proverb_native: Hold din Næse fra andres Gryder
proverb_en: Keep your nose out of another’s mess.

proverb_native: Holder man ei Kieppen til, agter Hunden intet Bud
proverb_en: It is the raised stick that makes the dog obey.

proverb_native: Hör en Mand för du svarer; hör flere för du dömmer
proverb_en: Hear one man before you answer; hear several before you decide.

proverb_native: Hovedkulds Raad—halslös Gierning
proverb_en: Precipitate counsel—perilous deed.

proverb_native: Hovmod gaaer for Fald
proverb_en: Pride will have a fall.

proverb_native: Hvad man med Synd faaer, det med Sorg gaaer
proverb_en: That which comes with sin, goes with sorrow.

proverb_native: Hvad skal Spurve i Tranedands, deres Been ere saa korte
proverb_en: Sparrows should not dance with cranes, their legs are too short.

proverb_native: Hvem der hvidsker, han lyver
proverb_en: He who whispers, lies.

proverb_native: Hvem der ikke seer sig for, maa tage Skade for Hiemgiæld
proverb_en: He who does not look before him, must take misfortune for his earnings.

proverb_native: Hvem der saaer Penge, höster Armod
proverb_en: He who sows money, will reap poverty.

proverb_native: Hvem der skammer sig ved at spörge, skammer sig ved at lære
proverb_en: He who is ashamed of asking, is ashamed of learning.

proverb_native: Hvem der vil hænge sig, finder nok en Strikke
proverb_en: He who would hang himself is sure to find a rope.

proverb_native: Hver er sin Synd söd og sin Anger led
proverb_en: Every one finds sin sweet and repentance bitter.

proverb_native: Hver hörer helst sin egen Lov
proverb_en: Every man likes his own praise best.

proverb_native: Hver i sin Færd, som han er hjemme lært
proverb_en: A man conducts himself abroad as he has been taught at home.

proverb_native: Hver Mand siger det; ingen Mand veed det
proverb_en: Everybody says it; nobody knows it.

proverb_native: Hver Mand sin Lyst
proverb_en: Every man has his liking.

proverb_native: Hvermands Ven, hvermands Nar
proverb_en: Every man’s friend is every man’s fool.

proverb_native: Hver Ræv varer sin Bælg
proverb_en: Every fox looks after his own skin.

proverb_native: Hver skal bære sin egen Sæk til Mölle
proverb_en: Every man must carry his own sack to the mill.

proverb_native: Hvert Liv sin Lyst, hver Lyst sin Lov
proverb_en: Every life has its joy, every joy its law.

proverb_native: Hvo aarle riis, han vorder mangt viis
proverb_en: He who rises early will gather wisdom.

proverb_native: Hvo alle tiene vil, faaer Tak af Ingen
proverb_en: He who would serve everybody gets thanks from nobody.

proverb_native: Hvo avelös lever, han ærelös döer
proverb_en: He who lives without restraint, will die without honour.

proverb_native: Hvo der ei har Penge i Pungen, maa have gode Ord paa Tungen
proverb_en: He that has no money in his purse, should have fair words on his lips.

proverb_native: Hvo der ei kan faae Flæsket, faaer at nöies med Kaalen
proverb_en: He who can’t get bacon, must be content with cabbage.

proverb_native: Hvo der ei vil til Helvede, kommer ikke til Hove
proverb_en: He who would not go to hell, must not go to court.

proverb_native: Hvo der er födt til Penning, bliver aldrig Dalers Herre
proverb_en: He who was born to pennies, will never be master of dollars.

proverb_native: Hvo der er langt fra sit Hjem, er næst ved sin Skade
proverb_en: He who is far from home, is near to harm.

proverb_native: Hvo der flyer, gjör sig selv sagfældig
proverb_en: He who flees, proves himself guilty.

proverb_native: Hvo der föder en Ulv, han styrker sin Fjende
proverb_en: He who feeds a wolf, strengthens his enemy.

proverb_native: Hvo der gaaer i Dands, see til hvem han tager i Haand
proverb_en: When you go to dance, take heed whom you take by the hand.

proverb_native: Hvo der gaaer i Seng med Hunde, skal staa op med Lopper
proverb_en: He that lies down with dogs, will get up with fleas.

proverb_native: Hvo der har mange Jern i Ilden, han faaer somme brændt
proverb_en: He who has many irons in the fire, will let some of them burn.

proverb_native: Hvo der haver en hvid Hest og deilig Kone, er sjelden uden Sorg
proverb_en: He who has a white horse and a fair wife is seldom without trouble.

proverb_native: Hvo der haver Giekken i Ærmet, der vil han alt kige ud
proverb_en: If a man has folly in his sleeve, it will be sure to peep out.

proverb_native: Hvo der hugger over sig, ham falder Spaanerne i Öinene
proverb_en: He that cuts above himself, will get splinters in his eye.

proverb_native: Hvo der ikke byger vel, han bleger ikke vel
proverb_en: They who do not wash well, do not bleach well.

proverb_native: Hvo der ikke har Falk, han skal bede med Ugler
proverb_en: He who has no falcon, must hunt with owls.

proverb_native: Hvo der ikke vil kjöbe Raad for godt Kjöb, skal kjöbe Anger dyret
proverb_en: He who will not take cheap advice, will have to buy dear repentance.

proverb_native: Hvo der jager med Katte, han fanger Muus
proverb_en: He who hunts with cats will catch mice.

proverb_native: Hvo der kan sidde paa en Steen og föde sig, skal ikke flytte
proverb_en: He who can sit upon a stone and feed himself should not move.

proverb_native: Hvo der kommer paa Söen, maa enten seile eller synke
proverb_en: He that is once at sea, must either sail or sink.

proverb_native: Hvo der omgaaes med Ulv, han lærer at tude
proverb_en: He who herds with wolves, learns to howl.

proverb_native: Hvo der saaer Ærter ved Adelvei, faaer ei alle Bælge i Laden
proverb_en: He who sows peas on the highway does not get all the pods into his barn.

proverb_native: Hvo der saaer Had, skal höste Anger
proverb_en: He who sows hatred shall gather rue.

proverb_native: Hvo der selv vil gjöre sig til Giek, faaer mange til at hjælpe sig
proverb_en: He who would make a fool of himself will find many to help him.

proverb_native: Hvo der slaaer een paa Halsen, han slaaer ham ikke langt fra Hovedet
proverb_en: He who strikes another on the neck, does not strike far from the head.

proverb_native: Hvo der staaer höit, den seer man vidt
proverb_en: He who stands high is seen from afar.

proverb_native: Hvo der staaer Vedhuggeren nær, faaer en Spaan i Hovedet
proverb_en: He who stands near the woodcutter is likely to be hit by a splinter.

proverb_native: Hvo der tager Barnet ved Haanden tager Moderen ved Hjertet
proverb_en: He who takes the child by the hand, takes the mother by the heart.

proverb_native: Hvo der vil æde Kjernen, faae bryde Skallen
proverb_en: He who would eat the kernel, must crack the shell.

proverb_native: Hvo der vil binde for en andens Mund, skal först snöre for sin egen
proverb_en: He who would close another man’s mouth, should first tie up his own.

proverb_native: Hvo der vil have god Kaal, faaer at koste den
proverb_en: He who would have good cabbage, must pay its price.

proverb_native: Hvo der vil have godt af Ilden maa taale Rögen
proverb_en: He who would enjoy the fire must bear the smoke.

proverb_native: Hvo der vil have Hunden hængt, finder nok et Reb
proverb_en: He that wants to hang a dog, is sure to find a rope.

proverb_native: Hvo der vil Honning slikke, maa ikke ræddes for Bier
proverb_en: He who would steal honey, must not be afraid of bees.

proverb_native: Hvo der vil kjöbe Pölse af Hunden maa give ham Flesk igjen
proverb_en: He who would buy sausage of a dog must give him bacon in exchange.

proverb_native: Hvo der vil smage al Saad, brænder tidt sin Mund
proverb_en: He who tastes every man’s broth, often burns his mouth.

proverb_native: Hvo en anden vil række en Brand, vare sig at han ei brænder sin Haand
proverb_en: Let him who would reach another a brand, beware that he do not burn his own hand.

proverb_native: Hvo höit vil klyve, falder ofte ned
proverb_en: Those who climb high, often have a fall.

proverb_native: Hvo Kjærnen vil æde, skal Nödden bryde
proverb_en: He who would eat the kernel, must crack the nut.

proverb_native: Hvo kommer först til Mölle, faaer först malet
proverb_en: He who comes first to the mill is first served.

proverb_native: Hvo lidet forsmaaer, han bliver sjelden rig
proverb_en: He who despises small things seldom grows rich.

proverb_native: Hvo lidet saaer, lidet faaer
proverb_en: He who sows little, reaps little.

proverb_native: Hvo meget haver af Smörret, han kaster somt i Kaalen
proverb_en: He who has plenty of butter, may put some in his cabbage.

proverb_native: Hvo meget spörger, bliver meget viis
proverb_en: He that inquires much, learns much.

proverb_native: Hvor Aadselet er, forsamles Örnene
proverb_en: Where the carrion is, there the eagles gather.

proverb_native: Hvor der er Ave er Tugt; hvor der er Fred er Frugt
proverb_en: Where there is discipline there is virtue; where there is peace there is plenty.

proverb_native: Hvor der er Hierterum, er der Huusrum
proverb_en: When there is room in the heart, there is room in the house.

proverb_native: Hvo ret vil spare, skal begynde af Munden
proverb_en: He who would save, should begin with the mouth.

proverb_native: Hvor Mand ei selv kommer, der bliver ei heller hans Hoved tvættet
proverb_en: Where a man never goes, there his head will never be washed.

proverb_native: Hvor man ei kan komme over, maa man krybe under
proverb_en: Where you cannot climb over, you must creep under.

proverb_native: Hvor Mistanke gaaer ind, gaaer Kjærlighed ud
proverb_en: When mistrust enters, love departs.

proverb_native: Hvor Penge fattes og Raad, er bedst, ikke at krige
proverb_en: Where money and counsel are wanting, it is best not to make war.

proverb_native: Hvor Villie er Ret, der er Loven landflygtig
proverb_en: Where will is right, law is banished.

proverb_native: Hvor Vrede blinder, der Sandhed forsvinder
proverb_en: When anger blinds the eyes, truth disappears.

proverb_native: Hvo sig blander med Saader, ham æde Sviin
proverb_en: He who lies down in the wash will be eaten by swine.

proverb_native: Hvo sig selv kildrer, kan lee naar han lyster
proverb_en: He who tickles himself can laugh when he pleases.

proverb_native: Hvo sig selv laster, ham lover ingen Mand
proverb_en: He who speaks ill of himself is praised by no one.

proverb_native: Hvo som bygger efter hver Mands Raad, hans Huus kommer kroget at staae
proverb_en: He who builds according to every man’s advice will have a crooked house.

proverb_native: Hvo som har en god Nabo, har en god Morgen
proverb_en: He who has a good neighbour has a good morning.

proverb_native: Hvo som rædes for Ord, har ei Hierte til Gierning
proverb_en: He who is scared by words, has no heart for deeds.

proverb_native: Hvo som rörer ved Beg, han smitter sig
proverb_en: He that touches pitch defiles himself.

proverb_native: Hvo som Skalken kiender, han kiöber ham ei
proverb_en: He who knows a knave, makes no bid for him.

proverb_native: Hvo som Synden saaer skal höste Skam
proverb_en: He who sows iniquity shall reap shame.

proverb_native: Hvo som vil gjöre et stort Spring, skal gaae vel tilbage
proverb_en: He who would leap high must take a long run.

proverb_native: Hvo som vil hævne sig, skal vare sig
proverb_en: He who would seek revenge must be on his own guard.

proverb_native: Hvo som vil röre i Skarn, faaer og lugte det
proverb_en: If you will stir up the mire, you must bear the smell.

proverb_native: Hvo sorg elsker faaer altid noget at qvide
proverb_en: He who loves sorrow, will always find something to mourn over.

proverb_native: Hugg, saa falder Spaaner
proverb_en: Chop, and you will have splinters.

proverb_native: Hunden bliver ei lös, om han end bider i Lænken
proverb_en: The dog will not get free by biting his chain.

proverb_native: Hund er Hund om han er aldrig saa broget
proverb_en: A dog is a dog whatever his colour.

proverb_native: Hundrede Vogne med Sorg betale ei en Haandfuld Gield
proverb_en: A hundred waggonsful of sorrow will not pay a handful of debt.

proverb_native: Hunger er det bedste Suul
proverb_en: Hunger is the best sauce.

proverb_native: Hungrig Hund og törstig Hest passer ei om Hugg
proverb_en: A hungry dog and a thirsty horse take no heed of blows.

proverb_native: Hurtig til Hatten, og seen til Pungen, giör ingen Skade
proverb_en: Put your hand quickly to your hat, and slowly in your purse, and you will take no harm.

proverb_native: Huusbonds Öie giör en fed Hest
proverb_en: The eye of the master makes the horse fat.

proverb_native: Iæt har ingen Dyd uden det holdes
proverb_en: There is no virtue in a promise unless it be kept.

proverb_native: Idag Guld, imorgen Muld
proverb_en: To-day in gold, to-morrow in the mould.

proverb_native: Idig Gierning giör duelig Mester
proverb_en: Diligent work makes a skilful workman.

proverb_native: I Krig er bedst at binde sin Hest ved fremmed Krybbe
proverb_en: In war it is best to tie your horse to a strange manger.

proverb_native: Ilde giemmer man Pölse i Hundehuus
proverb_en: The dog’s kennel is not the place to keep a sausage.

proverb_native: Ilden skrötter ikke, hvis Kappe den svier
proverb_en: The fire heeds little whose cloak it burns.

proverb_native: Ilde strider hovedlös Hær
proverb_en: A headless army fights badly.

proverb_native: Ild Hund haver Ar i Næse
proverb_en: An ill-tempered dog has a scarred nose.

proverb_native: Ild og Halm giör snar Lue
proverb_en: Fire and straw soon make a flame.

proverb_native: Ild og Vand ere gode Tienere, men onde Herrer
proverb_en: Fire and water are good servants but bad masters.

proverb_native: Ild pröver Guld, og Nöd Vennehuld
proverb_en: Gold is proved in the fire, friendship in need.

proverb_native: Ildt er Rakke at lege med Biörnehvalp
proverb_en: It is bad for puppies to play with bear-cubs.

proverb_native: Ingen bliver greben paa det Sted, hvor han ikke kommer
proverb_en: No one can be caught in places he does not visit.

proverb_native: Ingen er mere döv end den som ikke vil höre
proverb_en: None so deaf as those who won’t hear.

proverb_native: Ingen er saa lang, han maa jo række sig, ingen saa liden han maa jo bukke sig
proverb_en: No man is so tall that he need never stretch, and none so small that he need never stoop.

proverb_native: Ingen er saa riig, han har jo sin Grande nödig
proverb_en: No one is rich enough to do without his neighbour.

proverb_native: Ingen faaer Skam, uden han selv hielper til med
proverb_en: Shame comes to no man unless he himself help it on the way.

proverb_native: Ingen falder siidt, uden han vil stige höit
proverb_en: No one falls low unless he attempt to climb high.

proverb_native: Ingen halter af Andres Skade
proverb_en: No man limps because another is hurt.

proverb_native: Ingen Helgen er saa ringe, han vil jo have sit Voxlys
proverb_en: Let a saint be ever so humble, he will have his wax taper.

proverb_native: Ingen kiender Præsten bedre end Degnen
proverb_en: No one knows the parson better than the clerk.

proverb_native: Ingen Ko kaldes broget, uden hun haver en Flek
proverb_en: A cow is not called dappled unless she has a spot.

proverb_native: Ingen kommer i Skaden, uden han selv hielper til
proverb_en: No one gets into trouble without his own help.

proverb_native: Ingen leder om en anden i Sækken, uden han selv har været der för
proverb_en: No man looks for another in a sack, unless he have been there himself.

proverb_native: Ingen skal foragte lidet Saar, fattig Frænde, eller ringe Fjende
proverb_en: Despise not a small wound, a poor kinsman, or a humble enemy.

proverb_native: Ingen Veibyrde er bedre at bære end Viisdom
proverb_en: Wisdom is the least burdensome travelling pack.

proverb_native: Intet er saa nyt, at jo för er skeet
proverb_en: Nothing is so new but it has happened before.

proverb_native: Intet Svar er ogsaa Svar
proverb_en: No answer is also an answer.

proverb_native: I stille Vand gaae de store Fiske
proverb_en: In still water are the largest fish.

proverb_native: I tyst Vand ere Orme værst
proverb_en: In still water the worms are worst.

proverb_native: “Jeg kiender nok Karsen,” sagde Bonden, han aad Skarntyde
proverb_en: ”I am a judge of cresses,” said the peasant, as he was eating hemlock.

proverb_native: “Jeg siger det ikke for min Skyld,” sagde Ræven, “at der er god Gaasegang i Skoven.”
proverb_en: ”It is not for my own sake,” said the fox, “that I say there is a good goose-green in the wood.”

proverb_native: Jo ædlere Blod, jo mindre Hovmod
proverb_en: The nobler the blood the less the pride.

proverb_native: Jo argere Skalk, jo bedre Lykke
proverb_en: The more knave, the better luck.

proverb_native: Jo flere Hyrder, jo værre Vogt
proverb_en: The more shepherds the less care.

proverb_native: Jo flere Kokke, jo værre Spad
proverb_en: The more cooks, the worse broth.

proverb_native: Jo kiærere Barn, jo skarpere Riis
proverb_en: The dearer the child, the sharper must be the rod.

proverb_native: Jo mere af Lov, jo mindre af Ret
proverb_en: The more by law, the less by right.

proverb_native: Jo mere Frygt, jo nærmere Skaden
proverb_en: The greater the fear, the nearer the danger.

proverb_native: Jorden er altid frossen for utrevne Sviin
proverb_en: The earth is always frozen to lazy swine.

proverb_native: Kald ikke paa Fanden, han kommer vel ubuden
proverb_en: Call not the devil, he will come fast enough unbidden.

proverb_native: Kappen giör hverken Præst eller Degn
proverb_en: It is not the surplice that makes parson or clerk.

proverb_native: Kast ei Bulöxe til Vor Herre, han vender det skarpe igien
proverb_en: Throw not thy hatchet at the Lord, He will turn the sharp edge against thee.

proverb_native: Kast ikke Barnet ud med Badet
proverb_en: Throw not the child out with the bath.

proverb_native: Kattens Leeg er Musens Död
proverb_en: What is play to the cat is death to the mouse.

proverb_native: Kiærligheds Væxt vil vandes med Graad, og dyrkes med Umag
proverb_en: Love’s plant must be watered with tears, and tended with care.

proverb_native: Kiært Barn har mange Navne
proverb_en: A pet child has many names.

proverb_native: Kiend Ulv Paternoster, han siger dog: “Lam! Lam!”
proverb_en: Though you teach a wolf the paternoster, he will say: “Lamb! lamb!”

proverb_native: Kloge Höns giör og i Nælder
proverb_en: Even clever hens sometimes lay their eggs among nettles.

proverb_native: Koen malkes, og ei Oxen; Faaret klippes, og ei Hesten
proverb_en: The cow is milked, not the ox; the sheep is shorn, not the horse.

proverb_native: Koen veed ikke af, hvad hendes Hale duer til, för hun har mistet den
proverb_en: The cow does not know the value of her tail till she has lost it.

proverb_native: Konst er Konst, om og ei Lykken er med
proverb_en: Art is art, even though unsuccessful.

proverb_native: Konst og Lære giver Bröd og Ære
proverb_en: Art and knowledge bring bread and honour.

proverb_native: Kragen er ikke des hvidere, at hun tidt toer sig
proverb_en: A crow is never the whiter for often washing.

proverb_native: Krage söger vel sin Mage
proverb_en: The crow will find its mate.

proverb_native: Kroget Jern kan Hammeren rette
proverb_en: Crooked iron may be straightened with a hammer.

proverb_native: Krukken gaaer saa længe til Kilde, til hun faaer knæk
proverb_en: The pitcher goes so often to the well that it gets broken at last.

proverb_native: Kys er Kiærligheds bud
proverb_en: Kisses are the messengers of love.

proverb_native: Laan din Ven, og kræv din Uven
proverb_en: Lend to your friend, and ask payment of your enemy.

proverb_native: Laant Hest og egen Sporer giör korte Mile
proverb_en: A borrowed horse and your own spurs make short miles.

proverb_native: Længe at leve er længe at lide
proverb_en: To live long is to suffer long.

proverb_native: Længe lever truet Mand, om han fanger Bröd
proverb_en: A threatened man lives long, if he can get bread.

proverb_native: Lad Barn have sin Villie, da græder det ei
proverb_en: Let a child have its will, and it will not cry.

proverb_native: Lad den blive ved Aaren, som har lært at roe
proverb_en: Let him stay at the oar who has learnt to row.

proverb_native: Lad din Hustru have den stakkede Kniv, og hav selv den lange
proverb_en: Give your wife the short knife, and keep the long one for yourself.

proverb_native: Lad Hund til Honningtrug, da springer han i med baade Been
proverb_en: Let a dog get at a dish of honey, and he will jump in with both legs.

proverb_native: Lands Skik, er Lands Hæder
proverb_en: National customs are national honours.

proverb_native: Langt fra Öine snart af Sinde
proverb_en: Out of sight, out of mind.

proverb_native: Lastelig er, at flye for en levende Fjende, og at skjelde den döde
proverb_en: It is discreditable to fly from a living enemy, or to abuse a dead one.

proverb_native: Lediggang er Fandens Hovedpude
proverb_en: Idleness is the devil’s bolster.

proverb_native: Legen gaaer bedst med Jævnlige
proverb_en: It is best to play with equals.

proverb_native: Leger du med Narren i Huset, saa leger han med dig paa Gaden
proverb_en: If you play with the fool at home, he will play with you abroad.

proverb_native: Leergryde baader intet af at komme i Lag med Kobberpotte
proverb_en: The earthen pan gains nothing by contact with the copper pot.

proverb_native: Leilighed giör Tyve
proverb_en: Opportunity makes the thief.

proverb_native: Let er den Byrde som en anden bær
proverb_en: Another man’s burden is always light.

proverb_native: Liden Hvile er altid god
proverb_en: A short rest is always good.

proverb_native: Liden Tue vælter ofte stort Læs
proverb_en: A little stone may upset a large cart.

proverb_native: Liden Vinding smager vel
proverb_en: Small profits are sweet.

proverb_native: Lide og bie, lyde og lære, hjelper fattig Barn til Ære
proverb_en: Suffering and patience, obedience and application, help the lowly born to honour.

proverb_native: Lige Brödre giöre bedst Leg
proverb_en: Like plays best with like.

proverb_native: Lige Bytte giör mindst Trætte
proverb_en: A fair exchange brings no quarrel.

proverb_native: Liggende Ulv löber ikke Lam i Munde
proverb_en: Lambs don’t run into the mouth of the sleeping wolf.

proverb_native: List har liden Ære
proverb_en: Cunning has little honour.

proverb_native: Löfter og raat Klæde löbe meget ind
proverb_en: Promises and undressed cloth are apt to shrink.

proverb_native: Lögn bliver ikke træt af at gaae lange Veie
proverb_en: Falsehood never tires of going round about.

proverb_native: Lögn er Fandens Datter, og taler sin Faders Maal
proverb_en: Falsehood is the Devil’s daughter, and speaks her father’s tongue.

proverb_native: Lögn og Dravel giver ond Avel
proverb_en: Lies and gossip have a wretched offspring.

proverb_native: Lögn og Latin löbe Verden omkring
proverb_en: Lies and Latin go round the world.

proverb_native: Lögn reiser om for hun vil voxe
proverb_en: Falsehood travels and grows.

proverb_native: Lög og Rög og en ond Qvinde komme god Mands Öine til at rinde
proverb_en: Onions, smoke, and a shrew, make a good man’s eyes water.

proverb_native: Lov baner Vei til Venskab
proverb_en: Praise paves the way to friendship.

proverb_native: Loven er ærlig, Holden er besværlig
proverb_en: To promise is easy, to keep is troublesome.

proverb_native: Loven er de Vaagendes, Lykken er de Sovendes
proverb_en: Law helps the waking, luck may come to the sleeping.

proverb_native: Lovkiön og Maler kan snart giöre hvidt til sort
proverb_en: Lawyers and painters can soon change white to black.

proverb_native: Luen er ikke langt fra Rögen
proverb_en: The flame is not far from the smoke.

proverb_native: Lykken banker ofte paa, Tossen lader hende ved Dören staa
proverb_en: Fortune often knocks at the door, but the fool does not invite her in.

proverb_native: Lykken bær en Mand over Bækken, om han gider sprunget
proverb_en: Luck will carry a man across the brook if he is not too lazy to leap.

proverb_native: Lykken gaaer til Dören, spörger om Forsyn er inde
proverb_en: Luck taps at the door and inquires whether prudence is within.

proverb_native: Lykken har meget til Mange, men nok til Ingen
proverb_en: Luck has much for many, but enough for no one.

proverb_native: Lykken haver skröbeligt Ankerhold
proverb_en: Luck has but a slender anchorage.

proverb_native: Lyst og Villie giör Arbeidet ringe
proverb_en: Cheerfulness and goodwill make labour light.

proverb_native: Mager Kalv glemmer at springe
proverb_en: A lean calf forgets to skip.

proverb_native: Magten gaaer ofte for Konsten
proverb_en: Power often goes before talent.

proverb_native: Magt og Mod vil have Vid med i Fölge
proverb_en: Might and courage require wit in their suite.

proverb_native: Man angrer ofte sin Tale, men sielden sin Taushed
proverb_en: Speech is oft repented, silence seldom.

proverb_native: Man bliver ei snarere skiden end af Skarn
proverb_en: Meddle with dirt and some of it will stick to you.

proverb_native: Mand kysser ofte den Haand, han gierne saae af at være
proverb_en: A man often kisses the hand he would like to see cut off.

proverb_native: Mands Ord, Mands Ære
proverb_en: A man’s word is his honour.

proverb_native: Mands Villie, Mands Himmerige
proverb_en: A man’s will is his heaven.

proverb_native: Man er dog ei Greve, for man æder store Leve
proverb_en: A man is not a lord because he feeds off fine dishes.

proverb_native: Man faaer at bage af det Meel man har
proverb_en: You must contrive to bake with the flour you have.

proverb_native: Man faaer det for gode Ord, som man mister for onde
proverb_en: You may gain by fair words what may fail you by angry ones.

proverb_native: Man faaer ei Ko af kolös Mand
proverb_en: You cannot take a cow from a man who has none.

proverb_native: Man faaer ei meer af Ræven end Bælgen
proverb_en: You can have no more of the fox than his skin.

proverb_native: Man fanger flere Fluer med en Draabe Honning end med en Tönde Ædike
proverb_en: More flies are caught with a drop of honey than with a barrel of vinegar.

proverb_native: Man fylder för Maven paa en Skalk end Öiet
proverb_en: It is easier to fill a rogue’s belly than his eye.

proverb_native: Mange Bække smaae, giöre en stor Aa
proverb_en: Many little rivulets make a great river.

proverb_native: Mange have formeget, men Ingen har nok
proverb_en: Many have too much, but none have enough.

proverb_native: Mange Hunde ere Harens död
proverb_en: Many dogs are the death of the hare.

proverb_native: Mange kysser Barnet for Ammens Skyld
proverb_en: Many kiss the child for the nurse’s sake.

proverb_native: Mange Sandkorn kommer Skib til at synke
proverb_en: Many grains of sand will sink a ship.

proverb_native: Mangen er god Ven, men ond Grande
proverb_en: Many a man is a good friend but a bad neighbour.

proverb_native: Mangen et Faar gaaer tykuldet ud og kommer klippet hiem
proverb_en: Many a sheep goes out woolly and comes home shorn.

proverb_native: Mangen giör megen Spad paa lidet Kiöd
proverb_en: Much broth is sometimes made with little meat.

proverb_native: Mangen Ko staaer i Vang, og kiger i Fælled
proverb_en: Many a cow stands in the meadow and looks wistfully at the common.

proverb_native: Mangen qvider for den Dag han ikke bider
proverb_en: Many a man labours for the day he will never live to see.

proverb_native: Mangen saae gierne sin egen Skam paa en andens Ryg
proverb_en: Many a one would like to lay his own shame on another man’s back.

proverb_native: Mangen vil gierne rose Ret og giöre Uret
proverb_en: Many love to praise right and do wrong.

proverb_native: Man giör ei god Erkebisp af en Skalk
proverb_en: You cannot make a good archbishop of a rogue.

proverb_native: Man giör ei godt Jagthorn af en Svinehale
proverb_en: You cannot make a good hunting-horn of a pig’s tail.

proverb_native: Man gnaver noget af Been, men intet af Steen
proverb_en: You may get something off a bone, but nothing off a stone.

proverb_native: Man har ei længer Fred end Naboen vil
proverb_en: You cannot have peace longer than your neighbour chooses.

proverb_native: Man har Leiligheden i Ærmet, naar man vil
proverb_en: You may always find an opportunity in your sleeve, if you like.

proverb_native: Man hugger ikke Hovedet af fordi det er skurvet
proverb_en: A head is not to be cut off because it is scabby.

proverb_native: Man hvisker stundom een noget i Öret, og hörer det over den hele By
proverb_en: Many a thing whispered into one ear is heard over the whole town.

proverb_native: Man kan ei drage haardt med brudet Reb
proverb_en: You cannot pull hard with a broken rope.

proverb_native: Man kan ei seile hvorhen man vil, men hvor Vinden blæser
proverb_en: You cannot sail as you would, but as the wind blows.

proverb_native: Man kan ikke bære al sin Slægt paa Skuldrene
proverb_en: A man cannot carry all his kin on his back.

proverb_native: Man kan ikke drikke og pibe paa eengang
proverb_en: You cannot drink and whistle at the same time.

proverb_native: Man kan ikke klippe Faaret længer end til Skindet
proverb_en: You cannot shear the sheep closer than the skin.

proverb_native: Man kan ikke see en Anden længer end til Tænderne
proverb_en: No one can see into another further than his teeth.

proverb_native: Man kan lukke for en Tyv, men ikke for en Lögner
proverb_en: You may shut your doors against a thief, but not against a liar.

proverb_native: Man kan nöde en Hest til Vands, men ikke til at drikke
proverb_en: You may force a horse to the water, but you cannot make him drink.

proverb_native: Man kan nöde en Mand til at blunde, men ikke til at sove
proverb_en: You may force a man to shut his eyes, but not to sleep.

proverb_native: Man kan nöde en Oxe til Vands, men ikke nöde ham til at drikke
proverb_en: You may force an ox to the water, but you cannot make him drink.

proverb_native: Man kan tænde et andet Lys af sit uden Skade
proverb_en: You may light another’s candle at your own without loss.

proverb_native: Man koger saa vel i smaae Gryder som i de store
proverb_en: You may cook in small pots as well as in large ones.

proverb_native: Man lader sig hellere bide af Ulve end af Faar
proverb_en: One would rather be bitten by wolves than by sheep.

proverb_native: Man maa bære sit Kors med Taalmodighed, sagde Manden, han tog sin Kone paa Ryggen
proverb_en: We must bear our cross with patience, said the man when he took his wife on his back.

proverb_native: Man maa ikke lade Narre see halvgiort Arbeide
proverb_en: Never let fools see half-finished work.

proverb_native: Man maa tage suur Sild af onde Gieldinger
proverb_en: Of bad debtors you may take spoilt herrings.

proverb_native: Man meder og den Fisk som nödig vil
proverb_en: Even that fish may be caught that strives the hardest against it.

proverb_native: Man skal bruge den Sol som nu skinner
proverb_en: Make use of the sun while it shines.

proverb_native: Man skal ei bære Segel i Anden Mands Korn
proverb_en: Take not your sickle to another man’s corn.

proverb_native: Man skal ei bide den Hund igien som bider
proverb_en: Bite not the dog that bites.

proverb_native: Man skal ei forsmaae gamle Venner eller gamle Veie
proverb_en: Old friends and old ways ought not to be disdained.

proverb_native: Man skal ei have to Tunger i een Mund
proverb_en: Keep not two tongues in one mouth.

proverb_native: Man skal ei kaste Steen efter den Hund som ligger stille
proverb_en: Throw no stones at a sleeping dog.

proverb_native: Man skal ei skue given Hest i Munden
proverb_en: Look not a gift horse in the mouth.

proverb_native: Man skal fire den Stub, man har Ly af
proverb_en: Honour the tree that gives you shelter.

proverb_native: Man skal ikke agte Hunden efter Haarene
proverb_en: Do not judge the dog by his hairs.

proverb_native: Man skal ikke troe en springsk Hest, eller en stor Herre. naar de ryste paa Hovedet
proverb_en: Trust not a skittish horse, nor a great lord, when they shake their heads.

proverb_native: Man skal ikke svide alt det som loddent er
proverb_en: All hairy skins must not be singed.

proverb_native: Man skal længe gaae bag en Vildgaas för man hitter en Strudsfieder
proverb_en: You must walk a long while behind a wild goose before you find an ostrich feather.

proverb_native: Man skal længe gabe, för en stegt Due flyver een i Munden
proverb_en: A man must keep his mouth open a long while before a roast pigeon flies into it.

proverb_native: Man skal længe hugge paa en Elletrunte, för man faaer en Bisværm deraf
proverb_en: You must knock a long while against an alder-bush before you get a swarm of bees out of it.

proverb_native: Man skal meget lide, eller tidlig döe
proverb_en: We must suffer much, or die young.

proverb_native: Man skal og saae efter en ond Höst
proverb_en: We must sow even after a bad harvest.

proverb_native: Man skal rævse godt Barn at det ikke bliver ondt, og ondt Barn at det ikke bliver værre
proverb_en: Chastise a good child, that it may not grow bad, and a bad one, that it may not grow worse.

proverb_native: Man skal Sæd fölge, eller Land flye
proverb_en: Follow the customs, or fly the country.

proverb_native: Man skal sætte Tæring efter Næring
proverb_en: Live according to your means.

proverb_native: Man skal skiemte med sin Lige
proverb_en: Jest with your equals.

proverb_native: Man skal skue en Pige i et Trug Deig, og ikke i en Springedands
proverb_en: You must judge a maiden at the kneading trough, and not in a dance.

proverb_native: Man skal smede Jernet medens det er hedt
proverb_en: Strike while the iron is hot.

proverb_native: Man skal tude med de Ulve man er iblandt
proverb_en: You must howl with the wolves when you are among them.

proverb_native: Man skal Vidien vride medens hun er ung
proverb_en: Bend the willow while it is young.

proverb_native: Man prædike aldrig saa længe for Ulv, han siger dog Lam ad Aften
proverb_en: You may preach ever so long to the wolf, he will nevertheless call for lamb before night.

proverb_native: Man tör ei ved at hænge Klokke paa Giekken, han ringer sig nok selv
proverb_en: There is no need to fasten a bell to a fool, he is sure to tell his own tale.

proverb_native: Mæt Mave roser Fasten
proverb_en: A full stomach praises Lent.

proverb_native: Medens græsset groer döer Horsemoder
proverb_en: While the grass is growing the mare dies.

proverb_native: Medens Hundene veire, löber Haren ad Skoven
proverb_en: While the dogs yelp, the hare flies to the wood.

proverb_native: Med Lov skal man Land bygge
proverb_en: With law must the land be built.

proverb_native: Med Ondt skal Ondt fordrives
proverb_en: Evil must be driven out by evil.

proverb_native: Med Ræv skal man Ræv fange
proverb_en: Set a fox to catch a fox.

proverb_native: Mellem sige og giöre er en lang Vei
proverb_en: Between saying and doing there is a great distance.

proverb_native: Mennesket spaaer, Gud raa’er
proverb_en: Man proposes, God disposes.

proverb_native: Mistanke er for Venskab Gift
proverb_en: Distrust is poison to friendship.

proverb_native: Mölleren er Aldrig saa drukken, at han glemmer at tolde
proverb_en: The miller is never so drunk that he forgets to take his dues.

proverb_native: Morgenstund har Guld i Mund
proverb_en: The morn hour has gold in its mouth.

proverb_native: Mude volder at Venskab holder
proverb_en: Gifts make friendship lasting.

proverb_native: Munden taler tidt det Halsen maa gielde
proverb_en: The mouth often utters that which the head must answer for.

proverb_native: Musen veed meget, men Katten veed mere
proverb_en: The mouse is knowing, but the cat more knowing.

proverb_native: Naar de store Klokker gaae hörer ingen de smaae
proverb_en: While the great bells are ringing no one hears the little ones.

proverb_native: Naar der regner paa Præsten saa drypper det paa Degnen
proverb_en: When it pours upon the parson, it drops upon the clerk.

proverb_native: Naar det regner Vælling, saa har Stodderen ingen Skee
proverb_en: When it rains porridge the beggar has no spoon.

proverb_native: Naar det skal være Held, kælver Tyren saa godt som Koen
proverb_en: If it is to be luck, the bull may as well calve as the cow.

proverb_native: Naar een Fod snubler er den anden nær ved Fald
proverb_en: When one foot stumbles, the other is near falling.

proverb_native: Naar Enden er god er alting godt
proverb_en: All’s well that ends well.

proverb_native: Naar Enhver faaer sit, faaer Fanden intet
proverb_en: When every man gets his own the devil gets nothing.

proverb_native: Naar Gaasen troer Ræven, saa vee hendes Hals
proverb_en: When the goose trusts the fox then woe to her neck.

proverb_native: Naar Giekken kommer til Torvs faaer Kræmmeren Penge
proverb_en: When fools go to market the huckster gets money.

proverb_native: Naar Glæden er i Stuen, er Sorgen i Forstuen
proverb_en: When joy is in the parlour, sorrow is in the passage.

proverb_native: Naar Hovedet værker da værke alle Lemmer
proverb_en: When the head aches all the limbs ache.

proverb_native: Naar hver agter sit, bliver Gierningen giort
proverb_en: When every one minds his own business the work is done.

proverb_native: Naar jeg har Penge i min Pung, da har jeg Mad i min Mund
proverb_en: When I have money in my purse, I have food in my mouth.

proverb_native: Naar Kat og Muus giör eet, har Bonden tabt
proverb_en: When cat and mouse agree, the farmer has no chance.

proverb_native: Naar Katten er borte, löbe Musene paa Bænken
proverb_en: When the cat’s away the mice will play.

proverb_native: Naar Knarren er rorlös, gaaer den for Vrag
proverb_en: When the helm is gone the ship will soon be wrecked.

proverb_native: Naar Kokken steger for Kieldersvenden, saa gielder det Herrens Vinfad
proverb_en: When the cook is roasting for the butler, woe to the master’s wine-cask.

proverb_native: Naar Krybben er tom rives Hestene
proverb_en: When the manger is empty the horses fight.

proverb_native: Naar lade Heste ville afsted, gamle Qvinder dandse, og hvide Skyer regne, da er intet Ophör
proverb_en: When lazy horses begin to start, old women to dance, and white clouds to rain, there is no stopping them.

proverb_native: Naar Legen er feirest, er han bedst at lade fare
proverb_en: When the game is most thriving it is time to leave off.

proverb_native: Naar man er i Sækken, skal man ud af Munden eller af Bunden
proverb_en: When a man is in a sack, he must get out at the mouth or at the bottom.

proverb_native: Naar man seer Ulvens Ören, er han selv ikke langt borte
proverb_en: When the wolf’s ears appear, his body is not far off.

proverb_native: Naar man selv gaaer, sparer man Budleien
proverb_en: He that performs his own errand saves the messenger’s hire.

proverb_native: Naar man vil fange Ræven, spænder man Gæs for
proverb_en: If you would catch a fox you must hunt with geese.

proverb_native: Naar Musen er mæt, er Melet bedsk
proverb_en: When the mouse has had enough the meal is bitter.

proverb_native: Naar Öllet gaaer ind, da gaaer Viddet ud
proverb_en: When the beer goes in the wits go out.

proverb_native: Naar Ræven prædiker for Gaasen, staaer hendes Hals i vove
proverb_en: When the fox preaches to the goose her neck is in danger.

proverb_native: Naar Ræven slikker sin Fod, maa Bonden agte sin Gaas
proverb_en: When the fox licks his paw let the farmer look after his geese.

proverb_native: Naar Saaret er lægt er Svien glemt
proverb_en: When the wound is healed the pain is forgotten.

proverb_native: Naar Skarn kommer til Ære, veed det ei hvad det vil være
proverb_en: When dirt comes to honour it knows not what to be.

proverb_native: Naar Stolen raver bliver der ei længe Sæde af
proverb_en: A rickety chair will not long serve as a seat.

proverb_native: Naar Strængen er stindest, da brister han snarest
proverb_en: When the cord is tightest it is nearest snapping.

proverb_native: Naar Sværdet er i Munden, skal man klappe Balgen
proverb_en: When the sword is in the mouth you must caress the sheath.

proverb_native: Naar to Uvenner blæse i et Horn gaaer det over den Tredie ud
proverb_en: When two enemies blow one horn, the third will have to suffer for it.

proverb_native: Naar Tranen gaaer i Dands med Stodhesten, faaer hun brudne Been
proverb_en: When the crane attempts to dance with the horse she gets broken bones.

proverb_native: Naar Træet falder, vil alle sanke Spaaner
proverb_en: When the tree falls every one runs to gather sticks.

proverb_native: Naar Tyvene trættes, faaer Bonden sine Koster
proverb_en: When thieves fall out the peasant recovers his goods.

proverb_native: Naar Verten leer gladest, da mener han Giæstens Pung
proverb_en: When the host smiles most blandly he has an eye to the guest’s purse.

proverb_native: Naar Vognen helder, vil enhver skyde efter
proverb_en: When the waggon is tilting everybody gives it a shove.
